stages.explanation: A hard, fixed, rapidly enlarging thyroid mass with local invasion and recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy in an elderly patient (often arising in a long-standing goiter) is classic for anaplastic thyroid carcinoma, one of the most aggressive human cancers. Diagnosis is confirmed with a core needle or open/incisional biopsy because FNA may be inadequate for these undifferentiated, necrotic tumors; biopsy also enables molecular testing (e.g., BRAF). Subacute thyroiditis is painful, follows a viral illness, and is self-limited—not a hard invasive mass. Graves disease causes hyperthyroidism, not a fixed invasive mass. Toxic multinodular goiter is functional and slow-growing, not rapidly invasive.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms anaplastic thyroid carcinoma with tracheal invasion and lung metastases. Which statement best guides management?
options: Radioactive iodine ablation is first-line and usually curative
It is staged IVB/IVC; management is multidisciplinary with airway protection, possible chemoradiation, BRAF-targeted therapy if mutated, and early goals-of-care discussion
TSH-suppressive levothyroxine alone controls the disease
Observation with serial imaging is appropriate given his age
stages.explanation: All anaplastic thyroid carcinoma is stage IV by definition; with tracheal invasion and distant metastases it is IVB/IVC. Care is urgent and multidisciplinary—secure the airway, consider chemoradiation for local control, and use BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy (dabrafenib/trametinib) if BRAF V600E-mutated, while initiating early goals-of-care/palliative discussions because prognosis is poor (median survival months). Radioactive iodine is useless—anaplastic cells are undifferentiated and do not concentrate iodine. TSH-suppressive levothyroxine helps differentiated cancer, not anaplastic disease. Passive observation is inappropriate for a rapidly lethal, airway-threatening malignancy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: To identify a potentially actionable targeted therapy for this anaplastic carcinoma, which tumor test is most important to obtain urgently on the biopsy specimen?
stages.explanation: Urgent BRAF V600E testing is critical because a substantial fraction of anaplastic thyroid carcinomas harbor this mutation, and BRAF/MEK inhibitors (dabrafenib/trametinib) can produce rapid, sometimes dramatic responses—occasionally enabling subsequent resection. Given the aggressive course, molecular testing should be expedited. Thyroglobulin staining helps confirm follicular-cell origin in differentiated cancers but does not guide targeted therapy here. Radioactive iodine uptake is irrelevant—anaplastic cells are dedifferentiated and do not concentrate iodine, so it offers no therapeutic or diagnostic value. TSH receptor antibody is a test for Graves disease and has no role in anaplastic carcinoma management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The mass continues to enlarge despite therapy and his airway is again threatened. He wants to avoid aggressive interventions. What is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Insist on emergent total thyroidectomy for cure
Engage palliative care for symptom control and honor goals-of-care preferences
Transfer to a chemotherapy trial regardless of his wishes
stages.explanation: Given a rapidly progressive, incurable anaplastic carcinoma and the patient's clearly stated preference to avoid aggressive measures, the appropriate disposition is to engage palliative care for airway-related dyspnea, pain, and secretion management and to honor his goals of care—this is patient-centered and guideline-concordant. 'Curative' total thyroidectomy is not feasible for an unresectable, invasive, metastatic anaplastic tumor and contradicts his wishes. Enrolling him in a trial against his wishes violates autonomy and informed consent. Discharging him without support abandons a symptomatic patient and fails the standard of compassionate end-of-life care.

pearls: A hard, fixed, rapidly enlarging neck mass with hoarseness/stridor in an elderly patient—often in a long-standing goiter—is anaplastic carcinoma until proven otherwise.
Airway comes first: stridor and positional dyspnea require urgent ENT/anesthesia evaluation before diagnostics.
Anaplastic carcinoma is undifferentiated, does not take up radioiodine, and is stage IV by definition with a dismal prognosis.
Confirm with core/incisional biopsy (for molecular testing), and integrate BRAF-targeted therapy and early palliative care.

cases.title: An unexpected finding on a neck scan
cases.chiefComplaint: A thyroid nodule was reported incidentally on a carotid ultrasound done for a bruit.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman is referred after a carotid Doppler ultrasound—ordered to evaluate an asymptomatic carotid bruit—incidentally noted a thyroid nodule. She feels well and has no neck symptoms: no lump she can feel, no pain, no dysphagia, no hoarseness, and no compressive symptoms. She denies palpitations, tremor, heat or cold intolerance, weight change, constipation, or dry skin. PMH includes hyperlipidemia and well-controlled hypertension. Medications: atorvastatin 20 mg daily and hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily; no allergies. She has never smoked, drinks wine occasionally, and exercises regularly. She has no history of childhood neck irradiation and no family history of thyroid cancer or MEN syndromes. She is postmenopausal and takes calcium with vitamin D. Review of systems is otherwise negative, and she is mainly anxious about whether the nodule could be cancer. She has had no prior thyroid imaging or testing.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable woman in no distress. The thyroid is not visibly enlarged; on careful palpation no discrete nodule is appreciated and the gland is non-tender and moves normally with swallowing. There is no cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. A soft right carotid bruit is audible. No proptosis, lid lag, tremor, or skin or hair changes. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal and reflexes are normal with normal relaxation phase.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For this incidentally discovered thyroid nodule, what is the appropriate first test?
options: Serum TSH (with a dedicated thyroid ultrasound)
stages.explanation: The first test for any thyroid nodule is serum TSH, paired with a dedicated diagnostic thyroid ultrasound to characterize the nodule (the carotid study is not adequate for thyroid assessment). TSH determines functional status: if low, a radioiodine scan is the next step to find an autonomous nodule, which rarely needs FNA. Immediate FNA is premature before TSH and proper sonographic risk stratification. CT with contrast is unnecessary for initial nodule evaluation and the iodine load complicates later radioiodine studies. Calcitonin is not routine first-line testing in a patient without a family history or sonographic suspicion of medullary carcinoma.

stages.label: LABS & ULTRASOUND
stages.content:
TSH 2.0 mIU/L (ref 0.4–4.0)
Dedicated thyroid ultrasound: 1.4 cm solid isoechoic nodule, smooth margins, wider-than-tall, no microcalcifications, no suspicious vascularity
No abnormal cervical lymph nodes
Sonographic pattern: intermediate suspicion

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: With a normal TSH and an intermediate-suspicion 1.4 cm nodule, which factor most appropriately determines whether FNA is performed?
options: The combination of sonographic risk pattern and nodule size thresholds
stages.explanation: The decision to perform FNA is driven by the sonographic risk pattern combined with size thresholds (e.g., ACR TI-RADS / ATA categories): higher-suspicion patterns trigger biopsy at smaller sizes, while lower-suspicion patterns require larger sizes or surveillance. For an intermediate-suspicion nodule, FNA is generally recommended at ≥1.5 cm, so a 1.4 cm intermediate nodule sits near the threshold and may be biopsied or followed. Age and sex modestly affect risk but are not the primary decision rule. Thyroglobulin does not guide biopsy decisions for an undiagnosed nodule. The carotid bruit is unrelated to thyroid cancer risk.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: FNA is performed. Which system is used to report thyroid cytology and convey malignancy risk?
options: The Bethesda System for Reporting Thyroid Cytopathology
The Bethesda staging system used for cervical cancer
stages.explanation: Thyroid FNA results are reported with the Bethesda System for Reporting Thyroid Cytopathology, which assigns categories I–VI each tied to an estimated malignancy risk and recommended management (e.g., II = benign, VI = malignant). TI-RADS is a SONOGRAPHIC risk-stratification system used to decide whether to biopsy—not a cytology reporting system. There is no 'Bethesda staging system for cervical cancer'; cervical cytology uses the unrelated Bethesda system for Pap smears, which is a different application and not used for thyroid nodules. The Gleason system grades prostate adenocarcinoma histology and has no role in thyroid cytology.

stages.label: FNA CYTOLOGY
stages.content:
Ultrasound-guided FNA performed at patient request given size near threshold
Cytology: abundant follicular cells in macrofollicular/colloid-rich pattern, no nuclear atypia
Bethesda category II — benign

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The FNA is benign (Bethesda II). What is the most appropriate follow-up plan?
options: Levothyroxine suppression to shrink the nodule
Clinical and ultrasound surveillance (e.g., repeat ultrasound in 12–24 months)
stages.explanation: A cytologically benign nodule has a very low malignancy risk and is managed with surveillance—typically a repeat ultrasound in roughly 12–24 months, with re-biopsy if it grows significantly or develops suspicious features. Total thyroidectomy is unnecessary and exposes her to surgical risk for a benign nodule. Levothyroxine suppression is not recommended in iodine-sufficient regions; it inconsistently shrinks nodules and risks iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis and adverse bone/cardiac effects. 'No follow-up ever' is too cavalier—FNA has a small false-negative rate, so periodic sonographic monitoring is appropriate, particularly if growth occurs.

pearls: Start every thyroid nodule workup with serum TSH plus a dedicated diagnostic thyroid ultrasound.
A low TSH points to an autonomous nodule—get a radioiodine scan; a normal/high TSH proceeds to sonographic risk stratification.
FNA decisions combine the sonographic suspicion pattern with size thresholds (TI-RADS/ATA), not patient demographics alone.
A benign (Bethesda II) nodule is followed with surveillance ultrasound; routine levothyroxine suppression is not recommended.

cases.title: An abnormal value on routine bloodwork
cases.chiefComplaint: A high calcium level found on routine annual labs in an otherwise well patient.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman is referred after routine annual labs showed an elevated serum calcium. She feels generally well and was surprised by the result. On careful questioning she admits to mild fatigue and slightly increased thirst but denies bone pain, abdominal pain, constipation, nausea, kidney stones, or confusion. She has no history of fractures. PMH includes hypertension and osteopenia noted on a prior DXA. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily and a daily multivitamin; she takes no thiazide diuretic, no lithium, and no calcium supplements beyond the multivitamin. No known allergies. She is postmenopausal, drinks coffee daily and alcohol rarely, and has never smoked. Family history is negative for hypercalcemia, kidney stones, or endocrine tumors. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable. She works part-time as a librarian and is independent in all activities. She has not had recent immobilization, malignancy, or granulomatous disease.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Neck is supple with no palpable thyroid nodule or mass; no cervical lymphadenopathy. Abdomen is soft, non-tender, without organomegaly or masses. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness and no bony tenderness over the spine or long bones. Neurologic exam is normal with intact strength, sensation, and reflexes; mentation is clear and oriented. No proximal muscle weakness. Skin and mucous membranes appear well hydrated. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Serum calcium is confirmed elevated. What is the single most important next test to determine the cause of hypercalcemia?
stages.explanation: Intact PTH is the pivotal test that splits hypercalcemia into PTH-mediated (high or inappropriately normal PTH → primary hyperparathyroidism, the most common outpatient cause) versus PTH-independent (suppressed PTH → malignancy, vitamin D excess, granulomatous disease). 25-OH vitamin D is useful but secondary—it does not establish the mechanism and is checked once PTH is known. Serum protein electrophoresis screens for myeloma when malignancy is suspected, but PTH should come first to triage the differential. A 24-hour urine calcium is important later to distinguish primary hyperparathyroidism from familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia, but only after PTH establishes a PTH-mediated process.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Serum calcium 11.3 mg/dL (ref 8.5–10.5)
Intact PTH 96 pg/mL (ref 15–65)
Phosphate 2.3 mg/dL (ref 2.5–4.5)
25-hydroxyvitamin D 24 ng/mL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
24-hour urine calcium 320 mg/24h (elevated)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: High calcium, elevated PTH, low-normal phosphate, and high urine calcium are present. Which condition must be excluded before diagnosing primary hyperparathyroidism, and how?
options: Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH); the high 24-hour urine calcium argues against it
stages.explanation: FHH (a CaSR mutation) can mimic primary hyperparathyroidism with high calcium and high-normal/mildly elevated PTH, but it characteristically causes LOW urinary calcium (low calcium/creatinine clearance ratio). Her elevated 24-hour urine calcium and normal phosphate strongly favor primary hyperparathyroidism, not FHH—important because FHH is benign and should not be sent to parathyroid surgery. Myeloma causes PTH-independent hypercalcemia with a suppressed PTH, not this pattern, and is not confirmed by PTH. Sarcoidosis causes 1,25-vitamin D–mediated, PTH-suppressed hypercalcemia and is unrelated to calcitonin. Hypothyroidism does not cause this calcium/PTH picture.

stages.label: IMAGING & BONE DENSITY
stages.content:
Sestamibi parathyroid scan: focal uptake at the right inferior parathyroid
Neck ultrasound: 1.2 cm hypoechoic lesion posterior to the right thyroid lobe
DXA: T-score −2.6 at the distal one-third radius
Renal ultrasound: no nephrolithiasis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has primary hyperparathyroidism with a DXA T-score of −2.6 at the distal radius. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Parathyroidectomy (minimally invasive, guided by the localized adenoma)
Thiazide diuretic to lower serum calcium
stages.explanation: She meets surgical criteria for asymptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism: a T-score ≤ −2.5 at any site (osteoporosis) is an indication, as are calcium >1 mg/dL above normal, age <50, eGFR <60, hypercalciuria with stone risk, or nephrolithiasis. With a localized single adenoma, minimally invasive parathyroidectomy is curative and is the preferred treatment. Observation is appropriate only when none of the surgical criteria are met. Bisphosphonates treat bone loss but do not cure the disease or fully correct hypercalcemia. Thiazides are contraindicated—they reduce urinary calcium excretion and can worsen hypercalcemia.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On the first postoperative day after parathyroidectomy she reports perioral tingling and finger cramps. What is the most likely explanation?
stages.explanation: Perioral paresthesias and carpal cramps soon after parathyroidectomy reflect acute hypocalcemia, often from sudden withdrawal of PTH drive or rapid skeletal calcium uptake ('hungry bone syndrome'), especially with preexisting high bone turnover. Management is calcium (and calcitriol) supplementation with monitoring. Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury causes hoarseness or, if bilateral, airway compromise—not tetany. Persistent hyperparathyroidism would maintain hypercalcemia, the opposite of these hypocalcemic symptoms. Wound infection presents with erythema, warmth, fever, and drainage over days, not immediate neuromuscular irritability.

pearls: Intact PTH is the first branch point in hypercalcemia: high/inappropriately normal PTH = primary hyperparathyroidism; suppressed PTH = malignancy or other PTH-independent cause.
Check 24-hour urine calcium to exclude familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (low urine calcium) before referring for parathyroid surgery.
Surgical criteria include calcium >1 mg/dL above normal, age <50, osteoporosis (T ≤ −2.5), nephrolithiasis, hypercalciuria, or eGFR <60.
Watch for postoperative hypocalcemia / hungry bone syndrome—perioral tingling and cramps after parathyroidectomy.

cases.title: Confusion and severe dehydration in a smoker
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive confusion, constipation, and fatigue admitted for a markedly elevated calcium level.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man is admitted from clinic after lab work showed a markedly elevated calcium during evaluation of weeks of worsening fatigue, constipation, polyuria, and intermittent confusion noted by his wife. He has a chronic cough and a 30 lb weight loss over four months. PMH includes COPD and a 50-pack-year smoking history; he continues to smoke. Medications: tiotropium and albuterol inhalers; no thiazide, lithium, calcium, or vitamin D supplements. No known allergies. He drinks beer most weekends and lives with his wife. Family history is noncontributory for endocrine tumors or hypercalcemia. Review of systems is positive for the cough, weight loss, anorexia, constipation, polyuria, and confusion; he denies bone pain, prior kidney stones, hematuria, or fevers. He has not seen a physician regularly in years and has had no prior imaging. On arrival he is drowsy but arousable and his wife confirms he has been eating and drinking poorly for several days.
cases.exam: Drowsy, cachectic man who is slow to respond but oriented to person and place. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is reduced. There is decreased breath sounds and dullness at the right lung base. Abdomen is soft with hypoactive bowel sounds and mild diffuse tenderness; no rebound, no palpable mass, no organomegaly. There is no thyroid nodule or cervical lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam shows generalized lethargy without focal deficit; reflexes are diminished. No bony tenderness. Cardiac exam reveals sinus tachycardia without murmur.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His calcium is 14.6 mg/dL with signs of volume depletion and lethargy. What is the most important immediate treatment?
stages.explanation: Severe symptomatic hypercalcemia (>14 mg/dL) is treated first with aggressive IV isotonic (normal) saline to restore intravascular volume and promote renal calcium excretion—he is clearly volume-depleted. After adequate rehydration, IV bisphosphonate (e.g., zoledronic acid) is added for durable lowering, and calcitonin can give a rapid but transient effect. Oral phosphate binders are irrelevant to acute hypercalcemia management. A loop diuretic before volume repletion is harmful—it worsens dehydration; loop diuretics are only considered after the patient is euvolemic and not as first-line. Oral bisphosphonates are too slow and poorly absorbed for an acute crisis.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Calcium 14.6 mg/dL (ref 8.5–10.5)
Albumin 3.4 g/dL
Intact PTH <5 pg/mL (ref 15–65)
Phosphate 3.6 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL
Chest X-ray: 4 cm right hilar mass with right pleural effusion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Calcium is markedly elevated with a suppressed PTH and a hilar lung mass in a heavy smoker. Which mechanism best explains the hypercalcemia?
options: PTH-related peptide (PTHrP) secretion by squamous cell lung cancer
stages.explanation: A suppressed PTH with hypercalcemia is PTH-independent; in a heavy smoker with a hilar lung mass and weight loss, the leading cause is humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy from PTHrP, classically secreted by squamous cell carcinoma of the lung. PTHrP mimics PTH at its receptor, raising calcium while feedback suppresses native PTH. Primary hyperparathyroidism would show high/inappropriately normal PTH, not <5. FHH causes mild hypercalcemia with high-normal PTH and low urine calcium—not a suppressed PTH with a lung mass. Sarcoidosis raises calcium via 1,25-vitamin D, but the clinical picture (smoker, hilar mass, cachexia) points to malignancy.

stages.label: CONFIRMATORY WORKUP
stages.content:
PTHrP: markedly elevated
25-hydroxyvitamin D: normal; 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D: low
CT chest: 4.5 cm cavitating right hilar mass with mediastinal nodes
Bronchoscopic biopsy: squamous cell carcinoma
Post-hydration calcium 12.8 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Calcium is still 12.8 mg/dL after rehydration. What is the best next step for more durable calcium control?
stages.explanation: After volume repletion, an IV bisphosphonate such as zoledronic acid provides durable lowering by inhibiting osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, the main driver of PTHrP-mediated hypercalcemia; calcitonin can bridge the 2–4 days until bisphosphonate takes effect, and denosumab is an option in bisphosphonate-refractory or renally impaired cases. Oral calcium and vitamin D would worsen the hypercalcemia. Parathyroidectomy is irrelevant—the parathyroids are suppressed and not the source. Thiazides reduce urinary calcium excretion and would raise calcium further, so they are contraindicated. Ultimately, treating the underlying malignancy is essential for lasting control.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What does the development of PTHrP-mediated hypercalcemia generally indicate about this patient's cancer?
options: It is unrelated to tumor burden or prognosis
It typically reflects advanced disease and a poor prognosis, warranting oncology and goals-of-care discussion
It means the tumor is benign
stages.explanation: Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy usually develops in advanced, high-burden disease and portends a poor prognosis; its appearance should prompt prompt oncology involvement for treatment of the underlying squamous cell carcinoma and an early goals-of-care discussion. It does not indicate early or curable disease—on the contrary, it is a marker of significant tumor burden. It is not prognostically neutral; it correlates with worse outcomes. It certainly does not imply a benign process—PTHrP secretion here is from a biopsy-proven malignancy.

pearls: A suppressed PTH with hypercalcemia means a PTH-independent cause—think malignancy (PTHrP) until proven otherwise.
PTHrP-mediated (humoral) hypercalcemia is classic for squamous cell carcinoma (lung, head/neck) and renal cell carcinoma.
Treat severe hypercalcemia first with aggressive IV saline, then an IV bisphosphonate (± calcitonin for rapid bridging); avoid loop diuretics until euvolemic.
Hypercalcemia of malignancy reflects advanced disease and a poor prognosis—engage oncology and goals of care early.

cases.title: Tingling lips and hand spasms after an operation
cases.chiefComplaint: Tingling around the mouth and cramping spasms in the hands the day after thyroid surgery.
cases.history: A 47-year-old woman is on the surgical ward the day after a total thyroidectomy performed for a large multinodular goiter with compressive symptoms. Overnight she developed numbness and tingling around her mouth and in her fingertips, followed by painful cramping spasms of both hands. She feels anxious and reports mild muscle twitching. She denies chest pain, palpitations, seizures, or difficulty breathing, though she notes a slightly 'tight' throat. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications before surgery were only a multivitamin; postoperatively she is receiving acetaminophen and ondansetron. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for perioral and acral paresthesias and hand cramps; she denies prior episodes of tetany, kidney stones, or neck radiation. The operative note describes a difficult dissection of a large gland; parathyroid status during surgery was uncertain. She has had no prior neck surgery.
cases.exam: Anxious but comfortable woman with a clean, intact anterior neck incision and no expanding hematoma or significant swelling; voice is normal and there is no stridor. Tapping over the facial nerve anterior to the ear produces ipsilateral facial muscle twitching (positive Chvostek sign). Inflating a blood pressure cuff above systolic for several minutes elicits carpal spasm (positive Trousseau sign). Neurologic exam otherwise shows normal strength and sensation without focal deficit; mentation is clear. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. No tongue or lip swelling.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given perioral/acral paresthesias with positive Chvostek and Trousseau signs after total thyroidectomy, what is the most likely cause?
stages.explanation: Tetany with positive Chvostek and Trousseau signs after total thyroidectomy points to acute hypocalcemia from injury to or devascularization of the parathyroid glands (postsurgical hypoparathyroidism)—a classic, expected complication, particularly after difficult dissections. Anxiety/hyperventilation can transiently lower ionized calcium and cause tingling, but it would not explain true hypocalcemia in this surgical context and is a diagnosis of exclusion. Hypomagnesemia can impair PTH secretion and cause hypocalcemia, but ondansetron is not a typical cause; magnesium should still be checked. Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury causes hoarseness or airway issues, not tetany.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Total calcium 6.9 mg/dL (ref 8.5–10.5)
Ionized calcium 0.78 mmol/L (ref 1.12–1.32)
Albumin 4.0 g/dL
Intact PTH 6 pg/mL (ref 15–65)
Phosphate 5.2 mg/dL (ref 2.5–4.5)
Magnesium 1.9 mg/dL
ECG: prolonged QTc

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Low calcium, a low PTH, and high phosphate confirm the diagnosis. What ECG finding is the most concerning manifestation of this electrolyte abnormality?
options: Prolonged QT interval predisposing to arrhythmia
stages.explanation: Hypocalcemia prolongs the QT interval (by lengthening the ST segment), which predisposes to torsades de pointes and other ventricular arrhythmias—the dangerous cardiac manifestation requiring prompt correction. A shortened QT is characteristic of hyperCALCemia, the opposite derangement. Peaked T waves are a hallmark of hyperkalemia, not hypocalcemia. A delta wave indicates ventricular pre-excitation (WPW) and is unrelated to calcium. The combination of low calcium, low PTH, and high phosphate is the classic biochemical signature of hypoparathyroidism.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has symptomatic hypocalcemia with tetany and a prolonged QTc. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: IV calcium gluconate with cardiac monitoring
stages.explanation: Symptomatic or severe hypocalcemia—tetany, carpopedal spasm, or a prolonged QT—requires IV calcium gluconate with continuous cardiac monitoring for rapid correction. Once stabilized, transition to oral calcium plus active vitamin D (calcitriol), and check/replete magnesium. Oral calcium alone acts too slowly for acute tetany with QT prolongation. IV sodium bicarbonate would worsen hypocalcemia by lowering ionized calcium (alkalosis increases calcium binding to albumin). Phosphate supplementation is contraindicated—phosphate is already high, and giving more risks calcium-phosphate precipitation and further lowers calcium.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Her hypoparathyroidism persists beyond several weeks. What is the appropriate long-term outpatient regimen?
options: Thiazide diuretic plus a high-phosphate diet
Oral calcium plus active vitamin D (calcitriol), with monitoring of calcium and urine calcium
stages.explanation: Chronic postsurgical hypoparathyroidism is managed with oral calcium and active vitamin D (calcitriol), since absent PTH impairs renal 1-alpha-hydroxylation; the goal is low-normal serum calcium while avoiding hypercalciuria/nephrocalcinosis, so urine calcium is monitored (a thiazide can be added to reduce urinary calcium if needed). Levothyroxine is needed after total thyroidectomy for thyroid hormone replacement but does nothing for calcium homeostasis. A thiazide plus a high-phosphate diet is wrong—phosphate should be limited, and a thiazide is adjunctive, not the core therapy. Cinacalcet lowers PTH/calcium and would worsen hypocalcemia—exactly the wrong direction.

pearls: Tetany with positive Chvostek and Trousseau signs after total thyroidectomy is acute hypocalcemia from parathyroid injury until proven otherwise.
The biochemical triad of hypoparathyroidism is low calcium, low/inappropriately normal PTH, and high phosphate.
Hypocalcemia prolongs the QT interval; symptomatic or QT-prolonging hypocalcemia needs IV calcium gluconate with monitoring (and check magnesium).
Chronic hypoparathyroidism is treated with oral calcium plus calcitriol, targeting low-normal calcium while watching for hypercalciuria.

cases.title: Worsening lab abnormalities on dialysis
cases.chiefComplaint: Bone aches, itching, and worsening parathyroid hormone levels in a dialysis patient.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man with end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis three times weekly for four years presents for routine follow-up with new diffuse bone aches and persistent generalized itching. He admits he frequently skips his phosphate binders because of pill burden and does not always follow the renal diet. He denies fractures, but reports proximal muscle weakness making it hard to climb stairs. PMH includes type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and ESRD presumed diabetic. Medications: insulin glargine and aspart, amlodipine, sevelamer (inconsistently taken), and a renal multivitamin; he is not on any vitamin D analog or calcimimetic currently. No known allergies. He does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history includes diabetes and hypertension. Review of systems is positive for bone pain, pruritus, and weakness; he denies chest pain, dyspnea at rest, or recent fractures. He is adherent to dialysis sessions. His most recent labs prompted this focused visit and concern about his rising parathyroid hormone.
cases.exam: Chronically ill-appearing man in no acute distress with a left forearm arteriovenous fistula with a palpable thrill and audible bruit. Skin shows diffuse excoriations from scratching. There is mild diffuse bony tenderness over the ribs and proximal long bones and proximal muscle weakness (4/5) in the hip girdle. No focal neurologic deficit; reflexes intact. Neck is supple without a discrete palpable parathyroid mass. Cardiopulmonary exam reveals no JVD, clear lungs, and a regular rhythm without rub. No tetany; Chvostek and Trousseau signs are negative.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a long-term dialysis patient with bone pain and pruritus, which set of labs best characterizes the expected mineral-bone disorder?
options: Calcium, phosphate, intact PTH, and 25-hydroxyvitamin D
stages.explanation: CKD–mineral and bone disorder is assessed with serum calcium, phosphate, intact PTH, and 25-hydroxyvitamin D (with alkaline phosphatase as an adjunct). In CKD, phosphate retention and reduced renal 1-alpha-hydroxylation lower 1,25-vitamin D and calcium, and rising FGF23 further suppresses calcitriol—together driving secondary hyperparathyroidism, the cause of his bone pain and pruritus. TSH/free T4 evaluate thyroid disease, not this picture. SPEP and PTHrP target malignancy-related processes, which do not fit a dialysis patient with classic CKD-MBD. Cortisol/ACTH assess adrenal disease and are irrelevant to mineral-bone disorder.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Calcium 8.1 mg/dL (ref 8.5–10.5)
Phosphate 7.4 mg/dL (ref 2.5–4.5)
Intact PTH 980 pg/mL (ref 15–65)
25-hydroxyvitamin D 18 ng/mL
Alkaline phosphatase 220 U/L (elevated)
Bicarbonate 20 mEq/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Low calcium, very high phosphate, and markedly elevated PTH in a dialysis patient indicate which diagnosis and primary driving mechanism?
options: Primary hyperparathyroidism from a parathyroid adenoma
Secondary hyperparathyroidism driven by phosphate retention and low calcitriol
stages.explanation: Low calcium with high phosphate and a very high PTH is the signature of secondary hyperparathyroidism in CKD: phosphate retention, decreased renal calcitriol production, and consequent hypocalcemia chronically stimulate the parathyroids. Primary hyperparathyroidism causes HIGH calcium with low/normal phosphate—the opposite calcium-phosphate pattern. Tertiary hyperparathyroidism develops after prolonged secondary disease when the glands become autonomous and calcium becomes HIGH; his calcium is low, so he is not yet tertiary. Hypoparathyroidism would show a LOW PTH, not 980 pg/mL. Recognizing the calcium/phosphate/PTH pattern distinguishes these entities.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone first-line strategy to control this secondary hyperparathyroidism?
options: Control hyperphosphatemia (dietary phosphate restriction and phosphate binders) plus active vitamin D/calcimimetic as needed
High-dose oral calcium to push calcium up quickly
Stop dialysis and start a high-protein diet
stages.explanation: The foundation of therapy is controlling hyperphosphatemia—dietary phosphate restriction and adherent use of phosphate binders (preferably non-calcium-based like sevelamer to avoid calcium loading)—combined with active vitamin D (calcitriol or analogs) and/or a calcimimetic (cinacalcet/etelcalcetide) to lower PTH while keeping calcium and phosphate in range. His nonadherence to binders is a key reversible problem. Parathyroidectomy is reserved for severe, refractory, or tertiary disease, not first-line. Aggressive oral calcium risks vascular calcification, especially with his very high phosphate. Stopping dialysis is dangerous and would worsen everything.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His bone pain, proximal weakness, very high PTH, and elevated alkaline phosphatase reflect which high-turnover skeletal complication of his disease?
stages.explanation: Sustained PTH excess in CKD drives high-turnover bone disease—osteitis fibrosa cystica—characterized by increased osteoclastic resorption, marrow fibrosis, 'brown tumors,' bone pain, and a markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase, exactly his picture. Adynamic/osteomalacic disease is a LOW-turnover form (historically linked to aluminum or oversuppression of PTH) with low bone turnover and typically low/normal PTH—the opposite of his very high PTH and ALP. Paget disease is a focal idiopathic remodeling disorder unrelated to CKD-MBD. Postmenopausal estrogen-deficiency osteoporosis is a separate mechanism and does not explain his profoundly elevated PTH and phosphate.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
After 9 months of binders, calcitriol, and a calcimimetic, PTH remains 1,150 pg/mL
Calcium now 11.2 mg/dL
Phosphate 6.8 mg/dL
Parathyroid ultrasound: enlarged nodular glands
New calciphylaxis-type skin lesion on the thigh

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Despite maximal medical therapy his PTH stays >1,000 and calcium is now elevated with nodular gland enlargement. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue the same medical regimen indefinitely
Refer for parathyroidectomy (surgical management of refractory/tertiary hyperparathyroidism)
Begin high-dose calcium and vitamin D
stages.explanation: Persistently severe hyperparathyroidism refractory to maximal medical therapy—now with autonomous-appearing hypercalcemia, nodular gland enlargement, and a complication like calciphylaxis—signals progression toward tertiary hyperparathyroidism and is an indication for parathyroidectomy (subtotal or total with autotransplantation). Continuing the same failed regimen ignores refractory disease and ongoing tissue damage. A thiazide raises calcium and would worsen his now-elevated calcium. High-dose calcium and vitamin D would aggravate hypercalcemia and promote vascular/soft-tissue calcification, increasing calciphylaxis risk. Surgery is the definitive option here.

pearls: CKD secondary hyperparathyroidism: low calcium, HIGH phosphate, and high PTH—driven by phosphate retention and low calcitriol.
First-line therapy controls phosphate (diet + binders, prefer non-calcium binders) plus active vitamin D and/or calcimimetics; binder adherence is critical.
Tertiary hyperparathyroidism develops when chronically stimulated glands become autonomous, flipping calcium from low to HIGH.
Refractory disease with hypercalcemia, nodular glands, or calciphylaxis is an indication for parathyroidectomy.

cases.title: Weight gain and a rounder face on long-term medication
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive central weight gain, a rounder face, and easy bruising over the past year.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman presents with about a year of gradual weight gain concentrated in her abdomen and face, a 'rounder' appearance, easy bruising, and new purplish stretch marks on her abdomen. She also reports proximal muscle weakness, mild mood changes, and worsening blood sugars. Crucially, she has had rheumatoid arthritis for several years and has been on prednisone 10 mg daily for the last 14 months to control flares, in addition to methotrexate. Other medications: lisinopril and a recently started metformin for hyperglycemia; she takes calcium and vitamin D. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history is notable for type 2 diabetes. Review of systems is positive for the above plus thinning skin; she denies headaches, visual changes, or galactorrhea. She has not adjusted her steroid dose on her own. She lives independently and is concerned her appearance and weight are 'out of control' despite dieting.
cases.exam: Centrally obese woman with rounded, plethoric facies and increased dorsocervical and supraclavicular fat pads. Skin is thin with several ecchymoses and wide violaceous abdominal striae. There is proximal muscle weakness (4/5) in the hip flexors. The thyroid is normal and there is no cervical lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular exam reveals elevated blood pressure with a regular rhythm and no murmur. Hands show changes consistent with rheumatoid arthritis (mild ulnar deviation) without active synovitis. No visual field deficit on confrontation testing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her cushingoid features developed while taking daily prednisone. What is the most likely cause of her hypercortisolism?
options: Exogenous (iatrogenic) Cushing syndrome from chronic glucocorticoid therapy
Ectopic ACTH from a lung tumor
stages.explanation: The most common cause of Cushing syndrome overall is exogenous glucocorticoids; she has been on prednisone 10 mg daily for over a year, which fully explains her cushingoid phenotype. Recognizing the medication history avoids an unnecessary, expensive endogenous workup. Cushing disease (pituitary ACTH adenoma) is the most common ENDOGENOUS cause but is far less likely than iatrogenic disease given her clear steroid exposure. An adrenal cortisol-secreting adenoma is endogenous and would not be the first consideration with obvious exogenous steroid use. Ectopic ACTH (e.g., small cell lung cancer) is rarer and not suggested here; her exogenous steroids are the obvious culprit.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory pattern would you expect to confirm exogenous (iatrogenic) Cushing syndrome?
options: Low ACTH and low endogenous serum/urinary cortisol (with suppressed morning cortisol)
stages.explanation: Exogenous glucocorticoids suppress the HPA axis, so endogenous ACTH is low AND measured endogenous cortisol is low (synthetic steroids like prednisone are not detected as cortisol by many assays, and 24-hour urinary free cortisol is typically low/normal). This low-ACTH, low-endogenous-cortisol pattern distinguishes iatrogenic disease from endogenous causes. High ACTH with high cortisol suggests an ACTH-dependent endogenous cause (pituitary or ectopic). High ACTH with low cortisol suggests primary adrenal insufficiency, not Cushing. Normal ACTH with high aldosterone describes primary hyperaldosteronism, an unrelated entity.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Morning ACTH: low
24-hour urinary free cortisol: low-normal
Late-night salivary cortisol: low
Fasting glucose 168 mg/dL; HbA1c 7.6%
Bone density (DXA): osteopenia

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Iatrogenic Cushing syndrome is confirmed. What is the most appropriate management of her glucocorticoid therapy?
options: Gradually taper prednisone to the lowest effective dose (coordinating with rheumatology), using steroid-sparing agents
Increase prednisone to better control symptoms
stages.explanation: Treatment is to gradually taper the glucocorticoid to the lowest effective dose—or off if possible—while coordinating with rheumatology to optimize steroid-sparing agents (e.g., methotrexate or a biologic) so the rheumatoid arthritis stays controlled. A slow taper allows the suppressed HPA axis to recover. Abruptly stopping is dangerous: the chronically suppressed adrenal axis cannot mount cortisol, risking adrenal crisis. Increasing prednisone worsens the Cushing syndrome. Switching to high-dose dexamethasone (a more potent, longer-acting steroid) would intensify hypercortisolism and HPA suppression—the opposite of the goal.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During the taper she develops fatigue, nausea, dizziness, and hypotension. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Secondary adrenal insufficiency from HPA-axis suppression as steroids are reduced
stages.explanation: Tapering exogenous steroids too quickly can unmask secondary adrenal insufficiency, because long-term suppression leaves the HPA axis unable to produce adequate cortisol; symptoms include fatigue, nausea, dizziness, and hypotension. Management is to slow the taper and counsel on stress-dose steroids during illness/surgery until recovery. Worsening Cushing syndrome would cause more hypercortisol features, not hypotension and fatigue. A pheochromocytoma causes episodic hypertension, palpitations, and headaches—not this hypotensive, fatigued picture. Primary hyperaldosteronism causes hypertension and hypokalemia, not adrenal-insufficiency symptoms during a steroid taper.

pearls: Exogenous glucocorticoids are the most common overall cause of Cushing syndrome—always review the medication list first.
Iatrogenic Cushing shows LOW ACTH and LOW endogenous cortisol because synthetic steroids suppress the HPA axis.
Manage by tapering to the lowest effective dose with steroid-sparing agents—never stop chronic steroids abruptly.
Rapid withdrawal can precipitate secondary adrenal insufficiency; counsel on stress-dose steroids until the axis recovers.

cases.title: Weight Gain, Stretch Marks, and New Hypertension
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive central weight gain, easy bruising, and purple stretch marks over the past year.
cases.history: A 34-year-old woman presents with 14 kg of weight gain over the past year, concentrated in her abdomen and face, with thinning of her arms and legs. She reports purple stretch marks across her abdomen and thighs, easy bruising, proximal muscle weakness (difficulty rising from a chair), and worsening acne. She has noted new oligomenorrhea and low mood. PMH is notable for hypertension diagnosed 6 months ago, treated with amlodipine 10 mg daily, and prediabetes. She takes no glucocorticoids, denies inhaled or topical steroid use, and has no over-the-counter supplement use. Allergies: none. Social: drinks alcohol socially, never smoked, works as an accountant. Family history: type 2 diabetes in her father; no endocrine tumors. Review of systems is positive for facial rounding noticed by friends, increased fine facial hair, and decreased libido. She denies headaches or visual changes. She is frustrated that diet and exercise have not helped her weight.
cases.exam: The patient has a rounded, plethoric face and a dorsocervical fat pad with supraclavicular fullness. There is central obesity with thin extremities. Wide (>1 cm) violaceous striae are present on the abdomen and proximal thighs. Skin shows scattered ecchymoses and mild hirsutism on the chin. Proximal muscle strength is 4/5 in the hips and shoulders bilaterally; she cannot rise from a squat without using her hands. There is mild lower-extremity edema. Visual fields are full to confrontation. No goiter or thyroid nodules. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial test is most appropriate to screen for endogenous hypercortisolism in this patient?
stages.explanation: Late-night salivary cortisol is a first-line screening test for Cushing syndrome; it captures loss of the normal diurnal nadir in cortisol, one of the earliest abnormalities. Other validated first-line screens are the 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test and 24-hour urinary free cortisol. A random serum cortisol is uninterpretable because cortisol varies widely with stress and time of day. Morning serum ACTH is used to differentiate causes only after hypercortisolism is established, not to screen for it. The aldosterone-to-renin ratio screens for primary hyperaldosteronism, which causes hypertension and hypokalemia but not striae, central obesity, or proximal myopathy.

stages.label: SCREENING RESULTS
stages.content:
Late-night salivary cortisol: 0.42 µg/dL (normal <0.09)
24-hr urinary free cortisol: 310 µg/24h (normal <50)
1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression: 8 a.m. cortisol 14 µg/dL (normal suppression <1.8)
Fasting glucose: 138 mg/dL
HbA1c: 6.8%
Potassium: 3.8 mEq/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Hypercortisolism is confirmed. What is the best next test to determine whether the cause is ACTH-dependent or ACTH-independent?
stages.explanation: Once hypercortisolism is confirmed, plasma ACTH is the key branch point. A suppressed ACTH (<5 pg/mL) indicates an ACTH-independent (adrenal) cause, while a normal or elevated ACTH indicates an ACTH-dependent cause (pituitary or ectopic). Adrenal CT is appropriate only after ACTH is shown to be suppressed; imaging an incidentally nonfunctioning gland first risks misdiagnosis. Pituitary MRI is premature and can show incidental microadenomas in up to 10% of healthy people, so it should follow biochemical localization. High-dose dexamethasone testing helps distinguish pituitary from ectopic ACTH but only after ACTH dependence is established.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION STUDIES
stages.content:
Plasma ACTH: 78 pg/mL (normal 10–60) — ACTH-dependent
High-dose (8 mg) dexamethasone suppression: cortisol falls from 14 to 3 µg/dL (>50% suppression)
Pituitary MRI: 6 mm hypoenhancing lesion in the left side of the pituitary
Serum K: 3.9 mEq/L

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: The high-dose dexamethasone test shows >50% suppression and MRI shows a 6 mm lesion. Which finding would most strongly confirm a pituitary source over an ectopic ACTH source if the diagnosis remained uncertain?
options: A central-to-peripheral ACTH gradient on inferior petrosal sinus sampling
Failure of cortisol to suppress on the low-dose dexamethasone test
stages.explanation: Inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS) is the gold standard to distinguish a pituitary (Cushing disease) from an ectopic ACTH source when imaging and dynamic testing are equivocal. A central (petrosal)-to-peripheral ACTH gradient >2 at baseline (or >3 after CRH) confirms a pituitary source. Chromogranin A elevation suggests a neuroendocrine/ectopic tumor, arguing against pituitary disease. Failure to suppress on low-dose dexamethasone merely confirms Cushing syndrome and does not localize it. Severe hypokalemic alkalosis is characteristic of ectopic ACTH (very high cortisol overwhelming 11β-HSD2), pointing away from a pituitary source, not toward it.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The diagnosis is Cushing disease from a pituitary ACTH-secreting microadenoma. What is the first-line definitive treatment?
stages.explanation: Transsphenoidal selective adenomectomy is the first-line definitive treatment for Cushing disease, with remission rates of 70–90% for microadenomas in experienced hands. Bilateral adrenalectomy cures hypercortisolism but is reserved for refractory cases and risks Nelson syndrome (continued pituitary tumor growth). Ketoconazole and other steroidogenesis inhibitors are useful bridges to surgery or for inoperable disease but are not definitive. Radiosurgery is second-line, used for residual or recurrent tumor after surgery, and has a delayed effect requiring interim medical therapy. After successful surgery, patients require glucocorticoid replacement until the suppressed hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis recovers.

pearls: First-line screens for Cushing syndrome: late-night salivary cortisol, 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression, or 24-hour urinary free cortisol — use two abnormal tests to confirm.
Plasma ACTH is the branch point: suppressed = adrenal (ACTH-independent); normal/high = pituitary or ectopic (ACTH-dependent).
Cushing disease typically suppresses on high-dose but not low-dose dexamethasone; inferior petrosal sinus sampling is the gold standard when localization is uncertain.
Transsphenoidal adenomectomy is first-line; expect transient secondary adrenal insufficiency requiring glucocorticoid replacement postoperatively.

cases.title: Profound Weakness, Darkening Skin, and Low Potassium
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of progressive weakness, weight loss, and darkening of the skin.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man admitted for weakness reports 2 weeks of profound fatigue, muscle weakness, and a 6 kg weight loss. His wife notes his skin has darkened, especially the knuckles and creases of his palms. He has a chronic cough with occasional blood-tinged sputum for 3 months and worsening dyspnea. PMH: COPD and a 50-pack-year smoking history; he quit 2 years ago. Medications: tiotropium and albuterol inhalers. Allergies: none. Social: former heavy smoker, retired welder. Family history: noncontributory. On the ward he was found to have severe hypokalemia and hyperglycemia. He denies headaches, visual changes, striae, or easy bruising, and his weight has fallen rather than risen. Review of systems is positive for anorexia, peripheral edema, and proximal weakness making it hard to climb stairs. He reports no recent glucocorticoid use. The rapid onset of symptoms and his significant smoking history are notable.
cases.exam: The patient is cachectic with generalized hyperpigmentation, most prominent over the knuckles, palmar creases, and buccal mucosa. There is no facial plethora, dorsocervical fat pad, or violaceous striae. Lung exam reveals decreased breath sounds and a fixed wheeze at the right apex. Cardiac exam is normal aside from tachycardia. There is 2+ pitting edema of the lower extremities and proximal muscle weakness (4-/5 at hips). No goiter. Mild generalized muscle wasting is evident. Neurologic exam is otherwise nonfocal.

stages.label: ADMISSION LABS
stages.content:
Potassium: 2.6 mEq/L
Sodium: 146 mEq/L
Bicarbonate: 34 mEq/L (metabolic alkalosis)
Glucose: 268 mg/dL
Late-night salivary cortisol: markedly elevated
24-hr urinary free cortisol: 1,950 µg/24h (normal <50)
Chest X-ray: right hilar/apical mass

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Hypercortisolism is confirmed and his plasma ACTH returns at 240 pg/mL (normal 10–60). What test best distinguishes a pituitary from an ectopic source of ACTH in this case?
stages.explanation: The high-dose dexamethasone suppression test helps distinguish pituitary Cushing disease (which usually suppresses cortisol by >50% because the adenoma retains partial feedback sensitivity) from ectopic ACTH secretion (which typically does NOT suppress). The low-dose test only confirms the presence of hypercortisolism and does not localize the source. A morning serum cortisol adds nothing once hypercortisolism is established. Adrenal CT evaluates the adrenal glands but cannot differentiate a pituitary from an ectopic ACTH source; in ACTH-dependent disease both adrenals are typically hyperplastic. Inferior petrosal sinus sampling can confirm if needed.

stages.label: ADDITIONAL STUDIES
stages.content:
High-dose (8 mg) dexamethasone suppression: cortisol fails to suppress (<10% decrease)
Plasma ACTH: 240 pg/mL (normal 10–60)
Chest CT: 4.5 cm right hilar mass with mediastinal lymphadenopathy
Bronchoscopic biopsy: small cell carcinoma of the lung
DHEA-S: elevated

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why does this patient have severe hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis, features less common in classic Cushing disease?
options: Extremely high cortisol overwhelming renal 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2, producing mineralocorticoid effects
stages.explanation: In ectopic ACTH syndrome, cortisol levels are often extremely high — far higher than in pituitary Cushing disease. Cortisol can bind the mineralocorticoid receptor, but normally renal 11β-HSD2 converts cortisol to inactive cortisone to protect the receptor. When cortisol is massively elevated, this enzyme is overwhelmed, allowing cortisol to act as a potent mineralocorticoid, causing sodium retention, potassium wasting, and metabolic alkalosis. Coexisting primary hyperaldosteronism would show elevated aldosterone, not explain the cortisol findings. There is no diuretic use described. Renal tubular acidosis causes a non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, the opposite of his alkalosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While oncology plans treatment of the small cell lung cancer, what is the most appropriate immediate step to control his life-threatening hypercortisolism?
options: Start a steroidogenesis inhibitor such as ketoconazole or metyrapone
stages.explanation: Severe ectopic ACTH-driven hypercortisolism is life-threatening (severe hypokalemia, hyperglycemia, hypertension, immunosuppression, thromboembolism). Steroidogenesis inhibitors such as ketoconazole, metyrapone, or etomidate (IV, for the most severe cases) rapidly lower cortisol while the underlying tumor is treated. Physiologic hydrocortisone replacement is wrong — the patient has cortisol excess, not deficiency. Transsphenoidal surgery is irrelevant because the source is the lung, not the pituitary. Spironolactone could help his hypokalemia and hypertension by blocking the mineralocorticoid receptor but does not treat the underlying cortisol excess, which drives all his metabolic derangements and morbidity.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His severe hypokalemia must be corrected. Which combination best addresses the cause while replacing potassium?
options: IV potassium plus spironolactone, with concurrent cortisol-lowering therapy
Oral potassium plus a thiazide diuretic
Insulin infusion to drive potassium intracellularly
stages.explanation: The hypokalemia is driven by cortisol's mineralocorticoid effect, so the best approach combines potassium replacement, a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone or eplerenone) to block ongoing renal potassium wasting, AND cortisol-lowering therapy to address the root cause. Potassium replacement alone will be undermined by continued urinary losses. A thiazide worsens hypokalemia and is contraindicated. Insulin shifts potassium intracellularly and would acutely lower serum potassium further, dangerous in someone already at 2.6 mEq/L; it treats hyperkalemia, not hypokalemia. Correcting hypokalemia also improves his metabolic alkalosis.

cases.diagnosis: Ectopic ACTH syndrome from small cell lung carcinoma
pearls: Suspect ectopic ACTH when Cushing features develop rapidly with weight loss, severe hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, hyperpigmentation, and very high ACTH/cortisol — classically from small cell lung cancer.
Ectopic ACTH usually fails to suppress on high-dose dexamethasone, whereas pituitary Cushing disease usually suppresses by >50%.
Severe hypokalemia results from cortisol overwhelming renal 11β-HSD2, letting cortisol act on mineralocorticoid receptors.
Control life-threatening hypercortisolism rapidly with steroidogenesis inhibitors (ketoconazole, metyrapone, or IV etomidate) while treating the underlying tumor.

cases.title: Fatigue, Bronzed Skin, and Craving Salt
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of worsening fatigue, lightheadedness, and a strong craving for salty foods.
cases.history: A 38-year-old woman presents with 4 months of progressive fatigue, generalized weakness, and lightheadedness on standing. She has lost 5 kg unintentionally and reports intense salt cravings, occasionally eating salt directly. She has intermittent nausea and vague abdominal pain. Her husband says her skin and gums look darker. PMH: autoimmune hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, and vitiligo. Medications: levothyroxine only; no glucocorticoids. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, works as a teacher. Family history: mother with type 1 diabetes; sister with Hashimoto thyroiditis. Review of systems: dizziness worse in the morning and when standing, decreased libido, and amenorrhea for 2 months. She denies fever, vomiting, or recent illness. She has felt unusually cold-intolerant despite stable levothyroxine dosing, and her primary care visits showed a downward trend in blood pressure. The constellation of autoimmune conditions in her and her family is striking.
cases.exam: The patient appears thin and fatigued. Skin shows diffuse hyperpigmentation that is most prominent over the palmar creases, knuckles, elbows, and buccal/gingival mucosa; there are scattered depigmented vitiligo patches. There is orthostatic hypotension with a compensatory tachycardia. The thyroid is mildly firm without nodules. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. The abdomen is soft with mild diffuse tenderness and no organomegaly. There is no peripheral edema. Neurologic exam is nonfocal, though she has generalized muscle weakness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the clinical picture, which laboratory pattern would you most expect on initial chemistry testing?
stages.explanation: Primary adrenal insufficiency causes deficiency of both cortisol and aldosterone. Aldosterone deficiency leads to renal sodium wasting (hyponatremia) and potassium retention (hyperkalemia), along with volume depletion and orthostatic hypotension. Cortisol deficiency also impairs free-water excretion, worsening hyponatremia. Hypernatremia/hypokalemia is the pattern of mineralocorticoid EXCESS (e.g., hyperaldosteronism), the opposite. Hypernatremia with hyperkalemia is not a coherent adrenal pattern. Hyponatremia with hypokalemia would be more typical of vomiting or SIADH, not the aldosterone deficiency that defines primary (as opposed to secondary) adrenal insufficiency, where hyperkalemia is the hallmark.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Sodium: 129 mEq/L
Potassium: 5.6 mEq/L
Glucose: 64 mg/dL
BUN/Cr: 28/1.1 mg/dL
8 a.m. serum cortisol: 2.8 µg/dL (low)
Plasma ACTH: 620 pg/mL (markedly elevated)
TSH: 4.0 µIU/mL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Cortisol is low and ACTH is markedly elevated. Which confirmatory test best establishes primary adrenal insufficiency?
stages.explanation: The cosyntropin (ACTH) stimulation test confirms adrenal insufficiency: 250 mcg of synthetic ACTH is given and cortisol measured at 30–60 minutes. A subnormal cortisol response (peak <18 µg/dL) confirms inadequate adrenal reserve. In primary disease the diseased adrenals cannot respond even to exogenous ACTH. The dexamethasone suppression test screens for cortisol EXCESS, not deficiency. The insulin tolerance test assesses the HPA axis but is risky (hypoglycemia) and reserved for select secondary cases. 24-hour urinary free cortisol is used to evaluate hypercortisolism, not insufficiency. Here, the high ACTH with low cortisol already strongly indicates a primary (adrenal-level) defect.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: Given her vitiligo, autoimmune hypothyroidism, and family history, what is the most likely cause of her primary adrenal insufficiency, and which test would confirm it?
options: Tuberculous adrenalitis — confirm with adrenal CT and interferon-gamma release assay
Metastatic disease — confirm with whole-body PET
Adrenal hemorrhage — confirm with adrenal MRI
stages.explanation: In developed countries, autoimmune adrenalitis (Addison disease) is the most common cause of primary adrenal insufficiency, especially in a patient with other autoimmune conditions (autoimmune polyglandular syndrome type 2: autoimmune thyroid disease, type 1 diabetes, vitiligo). 21-hydroxylase autoantibodies confirm autoimmune etiology. Tuberculous adrenalitis remains the leading cause worldwide and warrants consideration, but her autoimmune background makes autoimmune disease far more likely. Metastatic disease and adrenal hemorrhage typically present more acutely or with a known primary/anticoagulation and would show adrenal enlargement on imaging rather than the autoimmune destruction implied here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate long-term replacement regimen for this patient?
stages.explanation: Primary adrenal insufficiency requires replacement of BOTH cortisol and aldosterone: a glucocorticoid (hydrocortisone, typically split dosing to mimic the diurnal rhythm) PLUS a mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone) to correct sodium wasting, hyperkalemia, and orthostasis. Glucocorticoid alone leaves the mineralocorticoid deficiency uncorrected. Fludrocortisone alone does not replace cortisol. High-dose dexamethasone provides glucocorticoid but no meaningful mineralocorticoid activity and is not the standard maintenance agent. Patients also need education on stress dosing (doubling/tripling glucocorticoid during illness) and an emergency injectable hydrocortisone kit to prevent adrenal crisis.

pearls: Primary adrenal insufficiency = cortisol AND aldosterone deficiency → hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, orthostasis, and salt craving.
Hyperpigmentation (palmar creases, buccal mucosa, knuckles) reflects high ACTH/POMC stimulation of melanocytes — distinguishes primary from secondary disease.
Confirm with a cosyntropin stimulation test; 21-hydroxylase antibodies identify autoimmune etiology, often as part of autoimmune polyglandular syndrome type 2.
Treat with both a glucocorticoid and fludrocortisone, plus sick-day stress dosing and an emergency hydrocortisone injection kit.

cases.title: Collapse and Refractory Shock After Stopping a Medication
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe weakness, vomiting, and collapse with hypotension not responding to IV fluids.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man is brought to the ED after collapsing at home. For 3 days he has had nausea, vomiting, diffuse abdominal pain, and profound weakness, with worsening lightheadedness. He has a viral upper respiratory illness with fever. PMH: rheumatoid arthritis on prednisone 15 mg daily for the past 8 months, which he abruptly stopped 1 week ago because he ran out and 'felt fine.' Other medications: methotrexate weekly, folic acid. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, rare alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. In the ED he received 3 liters of normal saline with minimal improvement in blood pressure and remains hypotensive and confused. Review of systems is positive for myalgias, fever, and reduced urine output. He has no chest pain, melena, or hematochezia, and his abdomen, while diffusely tender, is soft without peritoneal signs. The combination of chronic steroid use, abrupt cessation, and an intercurrent febrile illness is the critical thread in his history.
cases.exam: The patient is ill-appearing, lethargic, and confused but arousable. He is hypotensive and tachycardic with cool, mottled extremities and delayed capillary refill. Mucous membranes are dry. There is NO hyperpigmentation (consistent with secondary/iatrogenic suppression rather than primary disease). Heart sounds are tachycardic without murmurs; lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft with diffuse mild tenderness and no rebound or guarding. There is no rash or peripheral edema. Neurologic exam shows no focal deficits aside from his depressed mental status.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His hypotension is refractory to 3 L of crystalloid. While continuing resuscitation, which intervention is most urgent and should NOT be delayed for confirmatory testing?
stages.explanation: This is an adrenal crisis precipitated by abrupt withdrawal of chronic glucocorticoids during a febrile illness in a patient with HPA-axis suppression. The single most life-saving step is immediate empiric IV hydrocortisone (e.g., 100 mg) — it should NOT be delayed for confirmatory cortisol testing, as untreated crisis is fatal. Hypotension in adrenal crisis is often refractory to fluids and vasopressors until glucocorticoid is given. Antibiotics are important if infection is suspected but will not reverse the crisis. Vasopressors alone are frequently ineffective without cortisol. There is no evidence of hemorrhage to justify transfusion. Draw a cortisol/ACTH before steroids only if it does not delay treatment.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Sodium: 131 mEq/L
Potassium: 4.4 mEq/L (relatively normal — suggests intact mineralocorticoid/secondary cause)
Glucose: 52 mg/dL
BUN/Cr: 34/1.4 mg/dL
Random cortisol (drawn before steroids): 3.1 µg/dL (inappropriately low for stress)
Lactate: 3.2 mmol/L
WBC: 13.5 ×10³/µL

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: His potassium is normal (4.4 mEq/L) rather than elevated. Why does this patient have hyponatremia and hypoglycemia but NOT hyperkalemia?
options: He has primary adrenal failure with preserved aldosterone
His adrenal insufficiency is secondary/iatrogenic, so the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis and mineralocorticoid production are intact
He is taking a potassium-binding resin
Concurrent diabetic ketoacidosis is masking hyperkalemia
stages.explanation: Chronic exogenous glucocorticoids suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis (CRH/ACTH), causing SECONDARY adrenal insufficiency. ACTH does not regulate aldosterone — aldosterone is controlled by the renin-angiotensin system and potassium, which remain intact. Therefore mineralocorticoid function is preserved and potassium stays normal, unlike primary adrenal insufficiency where hyperkalemia is expected. Cortisol deficiency still causes hypoglycemia (loss of gluconeogenic/counter-regulatory effect) and hyponatremia (impaired free-water excretion plus hypovolemia). He does not have primary failure, is not on a binder, and has no evidence of DKA (glucose is low, not high).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond IV hydrocortisone, which combination of measures is most appropriate for his acute management?
options: Aggressive IV normal saline with dextrose and treatment of the precipitating infection
Immediate fludrocortisone as the primary intervention
stages.explanation: Adrenal crisis management is glucocorticoid plus aggressive volume resuscitation with isotonic saline; adding dextrose corrects his hypoglycemia, and identifying/treating the precipitant (here, the febrile viral illness ± secondary infection) is essential. Fluid restriction is wrong — he is volume-depleted and hyponatremic from hypovolemia, needing saline. IV insulin would worsen his hypoglycemia, and a beta-blocker is dangerous in shock. Fludrocortisone is unnecessary acutely because high-dose hydrocortisone provides sufficient mineralocorticoid activity, and he has a secondary (ACTH-mediated) cause with intact aldosterone. Once stabilized, taper to maintenance and counsel never to abruptly stop chronic steroids.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After recovery, what is the most important counseling point to prevent recurrence?
options: Switch all future steroid courses to inhaled formulations
Never stop chronic glucocorticoids abruptly; taper slowly and use stress-dose steroids during illness, surgery, or trauma
Permanently discontinue glucocorticoids to allow axis recovery
stages.explanation: Patients on chronic glucocorticoids (>3 weeks at suppressive doses) develop HPA-axis suppression and cannot mount an endogenous cortisol response to stress. The key teaching is to never stop abruptly — taper gradually — and to increase (stress-dose) glucocorticoids during febrile illness, surgery, or major stress, plus carry medical alert identification and an emergency injectable hydrocortisone. Inhaled steroids do not provide systemic replacement and are irrelevant to his rheumatoid arthritis needs. Avoiding vaccines is unsafe and unnecessary. Permanently and abruptly discontinuing steroids is exactly what precipitated this crisis; any wean must be slow and guided to allow gradual axis recovery.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If his blood pressure had remained profoundly low despite hydrocortisone and several liters of saline, what should be added next?
options: Norepinephrine vasopressor support while continuing steroids and fluids
Stop the hydrocortisone, as it may be causing hypotension
stages.explanation: If hypotension persists after adequate glucocorticoid and fluid resuscitation, vasopressor support with norepinephrine is appropriate while continuing steroids and saline; glucocorticoids restore vascular responsiveness to catecholamines, so vasopressors work better once cortisol is on board. Stopping hydrocortisone would be catastrophic — cortisol deficiency is the cause of the refractory shock, not a contributor to it. Therapeutic phlebotomy and loop diuretics both reduce intravascular volume and would dangerously worsen hypotension in a hypovolemic, vasodilated patient. Ongoing monitoring for the precipitating infection and electrolyte/glucose correction continues in parallel.

cases.diagnosis: Adrenal crisis from abrupt glucocorticoid withdrawal (secondary adrenal insufficiency)
pearls: Adrenal crisis = hypotension refractory to fluids/pressors; give empiric IV hydrocortisone immediately — do NOT wait for confirmatory cortisol.
Abrupt cessation of chronic glucocorticoids during stress/illness is a classic precipitant in patients with a suppressed HPA axis.
Secondary (ACTH-mediated) insufficiency spares aldosterone, so potassium is normal — unlike primary disease where hyperkalemia is expected; both cause hyponatremia and hypoglycemia.
Prevent recurrence with gradual steroid tapers, sick-day stress dosing, medical alert ID, and an emergency injectable hydrocortisone kit.

cases.title: Persistent Fatigue and Low Blood Pressure After Brain Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Ongoing fatigue, lightheadedness, and nausea several weeks after pituitary surgery.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman presents for follow-up 6 weeks after transsphenoidal resection of a nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma that had caused bitemporal visual field loss. Since surgery she has had persistent fatigue, lightheadedness, nausea, poor appetite, and a 3 kg weight loss. She feels 'washed out' and has had two near-fainting episodes. PMH: the pituitary adenoma; otherwise healthy. Medications: none currently; she was told her postoperative hormone testing was pending. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, social drinker, accountant. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: cold intolerance, dry skin, constipation, and amenorrhea (she is perimenopausal). She denies polyuria or excessive thirst (no diabetes insipidus symptoms currently). Importantly, she has NO skin darkening. She has not been on any glucocorticoids. The recent pituitary surgery and the absence of hyperpigmentation are the key features pointing toward a central rather than adrenal-level problem.
cases.exam: The patient appears fatigued with pale skin and NO hyperpigmentation of the palmar creases, knuckles, or buccal mucosa (a key contrast with primary adrenal insufficiency). There is mild orthostatic hypotension. The skin is dry and cool. Visual fields have improved postoperatively and are now near-full to confrontation. The thyroid is normal in size without nodules. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are unremarkable. The abdomen is soft and nontender. There is no peripheral edema. Neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which laboratory pattern best distinguishes secondary from primary adrenal insufficiency in this postoperative patient?
options: Low cortisol with high ACTH and hyperkalemia
Low cortisol with low or inappropriately normal ACTH and normal potassium
Low cortisol with high renin and hyponatremia
stages.explanation: Secondary adrenal insufficiency results from pituitary ACTH deficiency, so cortisol is low while ACTH is low or inappropriately normal — the pituitary cannot drive the adrenal. Because aldosterone is regulated by renin-angiotensin (not ACTH), mineralocorticoid function is preserved and potassium stays normal. In contrast, PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency shows low cortisol with HIGH ACTH (driving hyperpigmentation) and hyperkalemia from aldosterone deficiency. High cortisol with low ACTH describes adrenal Cushing, not insufficiency. Low cortisol with high renin/hyponatremia from aldosterone loss again points to primary disease. Her recent pituitary surgery and lack of hyperpigmentation strongly favor a secondary (central) cause.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
8 a.m. serum cortisol: 3.5 µg/dL (low)
Plasma ACTH: 9 pg/mL (low/inappropriately normal)
Sodium: 133 mEq/L
Potassium: 4.2 mEq/L (normal)
Free T4: 0.6 ng/dL (low)
TSH: 1.1 µIU/mL (inappropriately normal)
IGF-1: low
Prolactin: mildly low

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her labs suggest deficiency of multiple pituitary hormones. Before starting levothyroxine for her central hypothyroidism, what must be done first?
options: Start levothyroxine immediately at full dose
Replace glucocorticoid (hydrocortisone) before or together with thyroid hormone
stages.explanation: In a patient with combined secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism, glucocorticoid must be replaced BEFORE (or at least concurrently with) levothyroxine. Thyroid hormone increases the metabolic clearance of cortisol and raises metabolic demand; giving it first to a cortisol-deficient patient can precipitate an adrenal crisis. Starting full-dose levothyroxine alone is therefore dangerous. Antithyroid antibodies are irrelevant here — this is central (pituitary) hypothyroidism with a low free T4 and inappropriately normal TSH, not autoimmune thyroiditis. Growth hormone replacement is considered later and is never the priority over life-sustaining cortisol and thyroid hormone.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What replacement does she need for her adrenal axis, and does she require fludrocortisone?
options: Hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone, like primary adrenal insufficiency
Hydrocortisone alone; fludrocortisone is not needed because aldosterone production is intact
No replacement; the axis will recover on its own
stages.explanation: Secondary adrenal insufficiency requires glucocorticoid replacement (hydrocortisone) but NOT a mineralocorticoid, because aldosterone is regulated by the renin-angiotensin system and remains intact when only ACTH is deficient. This is a key contrast with primary adrenal insufficiency, which needs both hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone. Fludrocortisone alone would leave the cortisol deficiency untreated. Withholding all replacement is unsafe given her symptomatic hypocortisolism with orthostasis and near-syncope. She also needs education on sick-day stress dosing and an emergency hydrocortisone injection, identical to other forms of adrenal insufficiency.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which additional monitoring is most important given her pituitary surgery and panhypopituitarism workup?
options: Screen for diabetes insipidus and monitor serum sodium
stages.explanation: After transsphenoidal pituitary surgery, posterior pituitary dysfunction can occur, so monitoring for diabetes insipidus (polyuria, hypernatremia) and the SIADH that can follow in a triphasic pattern is important — serum sodium surveillance catches both. She should also have ongoing assessment of all anterior pituitary axes (thyroid, adrenal, gonadal, growth hormone). Bone marrow biopsy and coronary CT angiography are unrelated to her pituitary pathology. Daily urinary free cortisol is used to evaluate hypercortisolism, not to monitor replacement in adrenal insufficiency; replacement is titrated clinically, not by urinary cortisol.

pearls: Secondary adrenal insufficiency: low cortisol with low/inappropriately normal ACTH, no hyperpigmentation, and normal potassium (aldosterone is spared).
Always replace glucocorticoid before levothyroxine in combined deficiency — thyroid hormone can precipitate adrenal crisis in a cortisol-deficient patient.
Unlike primary disease, secondary adrenal insufficiency does NOT require fludrocortisone because the renin-aldosterone axis is intact.
After pituitary surgery, monitor all anterior pituitary axes and watch for diabetes insipidus/SIADH via serum sodium.

cases.title: Spells of Pounding Headache, Racing Heart, and Drenching Sweats
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of severe headache, palpitations, and sweating with very high blood pressure.
cases.history: A 44-year-old man presents with 6 months of episodic, abrupt attacks of pounding headache, palpitations, drenching diaphoresis, and a sense of impending doom, each lasting 15–45 minutes. Episodes are sometimes triggered by bending over, exertion, or straining. Between attacks he feels well. He has lost 5 kg despite a normal appetite and reports tremor and anxiety during spells. PMH: hypertension diagnosed 1 year ago, poorly controlled on three agents (amlodipine, lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide). Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, minimal alcohol, electrician. Family history: a maternal uncle had a 'thyroid cancer and adrenal tumor,' and his mother had hypertension and parathyroid surgery — raising concern for a familial syndrome. Review of systems: pallor during spells, palpitations, and constipation. He denies flushing (more typical of carcinoid). His blood pressure has spiked into the 200s during episodes recorded at home. The paroxysmal nature and family history are central clues.
cases.exam: The patient appears anxious with a fine resting tremor. He is hypertensive and tachycardic. The skin is warm but he reports episodic pallor rather than flushing. There is no goiter, but careful neck exam is documented given the family history. Cardiac exam reveals a forceful apical impulse without murmurs. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft; no bruit or palpable mass. There are no neurocutaneous stigmata such as café-au-lait macules or neurofibromas. Fundoscopic exam shows mild hypertensive changes. Neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate initial biochemical test to evaluate for a catecholamine-secreting tumor?
