options: Plasma free metanephrines (or 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines)
Random serum catecholamines during an asymptomatic period
stages.explanation: Plasma free metanephrines or 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines are the most sensitive screening tests for pheochromocytoma because catecholamines are metabolized to metanephrines continuously within the tumor, even between secretory bursts — giving high sensitivity. Random serum catecholamines fluctuate dramatically and are insensitive between spells. Chromogranin A is a nonspecific neuroendocrine marker and is not adequate as a primary screen. The aldosterone-to-renin ratio screens for primary hyperaldosteronism, which causes hypertension and hypokalemia but not the paroxysmal headache/palpitation/diaphoresis triad. Proper sampling (supine, avoiding interfering drugs) is important to limit false positives.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Plasma free metanephrines: 4.8 nmol/L (normal <0.5)
Plasma free normetanephrines: 6.2 nmol/L (markedly elevated)
24-hr urinary fractionated metanephrines: markedly elevated
CT abdomen: 4.2 cm heterogeneous right adrenal mass with high unenhanced attenuation (>20 HU)
Calcium: 10.8 mg/dL (mildly elevated)
Calcitonin: elevated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Surgical resection is planned. What is the correct preoperative pharmacologic preparation?
options: Start a beta-blocker first, then add an alpha-blocker
Start alpha-blockade (e.g., phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) first, then add a beta-blocker if needed
Begin an ACE inhibitor and proceed to surgery within 24 hours
Use a calcium channel blocker alone the morning of surgery
stages.explanation: The cardinal rule is alpha-blockade FIRST (phenoxybenzamine or selective alpha-1 blockers like doxazosin) for 10–14 days to control blood pressure and allow volume re-expansion, with a beta-blocker added only AFTER adequate alpha-blockade to control reflex tachycardia. Starting a beta-blocker first is dangerous: blocking beta-2-mediated vasodilation leaves unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction, which can precipitate a hypertensive crisis. An ACE inhibitor with rushed surgery does not provide the necessary alpha-blockade or volume repletion. A calcium channel blocker alone is insufficient preparation. Liberal salt/fluid intake during alpha-blockade helps restore intravascular volume before resection.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: His elevated calcium, elevated calcitonin, and family history of thyroid cancer plus parathyroid disease suggest a hereditary syndrome. Which is most likely?
stages.explanation: MEN2A (RET proto-oncogene mutation) comprises medullary thyroid carcinoma (elevated calcitonin), pheochromocytoma, and primary hyperparathyroidism (his elevated calcium and the uncle's parathyroid surgery) — fitting this family perfectly. MEN1 involves the 'three Ps' (parathyroid, pancreatic/enteric, pituitary) and does NOT include pheochromocytoma or medullary thyroid cancer. Von Hippel-Lindau causes pheochromocytoma with hemangioblastomas, renal cell carcinoma, and pancreatic cysts, but not medullary thyroid cancer or hyperparathyroidism. Neurofibromatosis type 1 is associated with pheochromocytoma but presents with café-au-lait macules and neurofibromas, which he lacks. Genetic counseling and RET testing are indicated.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During induction of anesthesia the patient develops an acute hypertensive crisis (BP 240/130). What is the best agent to control it?
stages.explanation: Intraoperative pheochromocytoma crises are managed with rapidly titratable agents: IV phentolamine (a short-acting alpha-blocker) or sodium nitroprusside, sometimes with magnesium. These directly counter catecholamine-driven vasoconstriction. Labetalol has more beta than alpha activity, so as monotherapy it risks unopposed alpha effect and is not ideal for acute pheochromocytoma crisis. Esmolol alone (pure beta-blockade) is dangerous for the same unopposed-alpha reason and is used only to control tachyarrhythmias after alpha control. Sublingual nifedipine causes unpredictable, uncontrolled drops in blood pressure and is not appropriate for a tightly managed intraoperative crisis. Manipulation of the tumor commonly triggers these surges.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful adrenalectomy, what is the most important early postoperative complication to anticipate?
stages.explanation: After resection of a pheochromocytoma, the sudden withdrawal of catecholamine excess — combined with residual alpha-blockade and the pre-existing volume contraction — commonly causes HYPOTENSION, which is treated with fluids and, if needed, vasopressors. Rebound HYPOGLYCEMIA also occurs because catecholamines had been suppressing insulin; their abrupt removal leads to a surge of insulin secretion, so glucose should be monitored. A hypertensive emergency is the intraoperative concern, not the typical immediate postoperative one once the tumor is removed. Acute hyperkalemia is not characteristic. Thyroid storm is unrelated to adrenalectomy. Lifelong biochemical surveillance for recurrence/metastasis and genetic screening of relatives are also required.

pearls: The classic triad is episodic headache, palpitations, and diaphoresis with paroxysmal hypertension; screen with plasma free or 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines.
Always give alpha-blockade FIRST, then add beta-blockade — never beta-block first (unopposed alpha causes hypertensive crisis).
Pheochromocytoma + medullary thyroid cancer + hyperparathyroidism = MEN2A (RET mutation); offer genetic testing and family screening.
After resection, anticipate hypotension and rebound hypoglycemia; control intraoperative crises with phentolamine or nitroprusside.

cases.title: Hard-to-Control Blood Pressure with Persistently Low Potassium
cases.chiefComplaint: High blood pressure despite three medications, plus muscle cramps and low potassium.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man is referred for resistant hypertension. His blood pressure remains elevated despite three antihypertensives at adequate doses (amlodipine 10 mg, lisinopril 40 mg, and hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg). He reports muscle cramps, fatigue, polyuria, and nocturia. Repeated labs show low potassium, sometimes requiring supplementation. PMH: hypertension for 6 years, increasingly difficult to control. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, moderate alcohol, sedentary office worker. Family history: hypertension in both parents; no known endocrine tumors. Review of systems: occasional headaches, muscle weakness, and increased thirst; no episodic palpitations, sweating, or flushing to suggest pheochromocytoma. He has no edema. He is frustrated that adding medications has not helped, and he was hospitalized once for a potassium of 2.8 mEq/L. The combination of resistant hypertension and spontaneous or easily provoked hypokalemia is the central clue.
cases.exam: The patient is overweight and well-appearing. He is hypertensive without orthostatic changes. There is no Cushingoid habitus, striae, or hyperpigmentation. The thyroid is normal. Cardiac exam shows a normal S1/S2 with an S4 gallop consistent with chronic hypertension; no murmurs or bruits. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft without an audible bruit. There is NO peripheral edema (a clue that aldosterone-mediated sodium retention causes 'escape' rather than edema). Mild proximal muscle weakness is noted, consistent with hypokalemia. Neurologic exam is otherwise normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which screening test is most appropriate to evaluate this resistant hypertension with hypokalemia?
stages.explanation: Resistant hypertension with hypokalemia is a classic indication to screen for primary hyperaldosteronism using the plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR). In Conn syndrome aldosterone is high and renin is suppressed, producing an elevated ratio. Urinary metanephrines screen for pheochromocytoma, which causes paroxysmal symptoms he lacks. The dexamethasone suppression test screens for Cushing syndrome, but he has no Cushingoid features. Renal artery duplex evaluates renovascular hypertension, in which renin is typically HIGH (not suppressed) — the opposite of the suppressed renin expected here. Ideally interfering drugs (especially mineralocorticoid antagonists) are managed before testing, and hypokalemia is corrected since it can falsely lower aldosterone.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Potassium: 3.0 mEq/L
Sodium: 144 mEq/L
Bicarbonate: 30 mEq/L (mild metabolic alkalosis)
Plasma aldosterone: 28 ng/dL (high)
Plasma renin activity: 0.2 ng/mL/h (suppressed)
Aldosterone-to-renin ratio: markedly elevated (>30)
eGFR: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The ARR is markedly elevated. What is the appropriate next step to confirm autonomous aldosterone secretion?
options: Perform a confirmatory test such as oral sodium loading or saline infusion to demonstrate non-suppressible aldosterone
Start spironolactone empirically and stop the workup
stages.explanation: An elevated ARR is a screening result that must be confirmed with a dynamic suppression test — oral sodium loading, saline infusion, fludrocortisone suppression, or captopril challenge — showing that aldosterone fails to suppress, confirming autonomous secretion. Proceeding directly to surgery is inappropriate before confirming the diagnosis and determining laterality. Starting spironolactone empirically without confirmation and subtyping forgoes a potentially curable surgical option and interferes with further testing. A pituitary MRI is irrelevant — aldosterone is produced in the adrenal zona glomerulosa, not the pituitary. After biochemical confirmation, adrenal CT and often adrenal venous sampling determine whether disease is unilateral (adenoma) or bilateral (hyperplasia).

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After biochemical confirmation, adrenal CT shows a 1.2 cm left adrenal nodule. The patient is 48 and a surgical candidate. What is the best next step to guide treatment?
options: Proceed to left adrenalectomy based on CT alone
Adrenal venous sampling to confirm lateralization before considering surgery
Start lifelong spironolactone without further imaging
Repeat the ARR in one year
stages.explanation: Adrenal venous sampling (AVS) is the gold standard to determine whether aldosterone excess is unilateral (surgically curable adenoma) or bilateral (managed medically) — especially important because small nonfunctioning adrenal nodules are common and CT can be misleading, while bilateral hyperplasia may be missed. Proceeding to adrenalectomy on CT alone risks removing a nonfunctioning incidentaloma while leaving the true contralateral or bilateral source. Lifelong spironolactone without lateralization denies a potentially curative surgery to a younger surgical candidate with a discrete lesion. Repeating the ARR in a year delays definitive management of confirmed disease. AVS findings then direct unilateral adrenalectomy vs medical therapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: AVS confirms bilateral adrenal hyperplasia rather than a unilateral source. What is the preferred long-term treatment?
options: A mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone or eplerenone)
stages.explanation: Bilateral adrenal hyperplasia (idiopathic hyperaldosteronism) is treated medically with a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist — spironolactone or eplerenone — which controls blood pressure and corrects hypokalemia by blocking aldosterone's effects. Bilateral adrenalectomy would cause lifelong adrenal insufficiency and is not warranted for bilateral disease, which responds well to medication. Potassium supplementation alone treats only one consequence and ignores the hypertension and underlying excess. An ACE inhibitor as monotherapy is inadequate, as primary hyperaldosteronism is driven by autonomous aldosterone rather than the renin-angiotensin cascade. Eplerenone is preferred when antiandrogenic side effects of spironolactone (gynecomastia, erectile dysfunction) are problematic.

cases.diagnosis: Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn syndrome) — bilateral adrenal hyperplasia
pearls: Screen for primary hyperaldosteronism in resistant hypertension with hypokalemia (or an incidentaloma) using the aldosterone-to-renin ratio: high aldosterone, suppressed renin.
An elevated ARR is only a screen — confirm with a suppression test (saline infusion, oral sodium load, fludrocortisone, or captopril) showing non-suppressible aldosterone.
Use adrenal venous sampling to lateralize: unilateral adenoma → adrenalectomy; bilateral hyperplasia → mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist.
Aldosterone escape explains hypertension and hypokalemia WITHOUT edema; correct hypokalemia and manage interfering drugs before ARR testing.

cases.title: Newborn with Vomiting, Poor Feeding, and Atypical Genitalia
cases.chiefComplaint: Two-week-old with vomiting, lethargy, poor weight gain, and atypical genital appearance.
cases.history: A 2-week-old infant is brought to the ED for 3 days of vomiting, poor feeding, lethargy, and inadequate weight gain (now below birth weight). The infant was born at term at home without newborn screening completed. The parents note the genitalia 'looked unusual' at birth, prompting uncertainty about sex assignment. There is no diarrhea or fever reported initially. PMH: uncomplicated pregnancy; no prenatal care after the first trimester. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Family history: parents are first cousins (consanguinity); a maternal aunt had an infant who died in the neonatal period of an unknown cause. Social: home birth, no completed state newborn metabolic screen — a critical gap. Review of systems: progressive lethargy, decreased wet diapers, and one episode of an apneic spell at home. The combination of a salt-wasting presentation, atypical genitalia, consanguinity, and a missed newborn screen is the key thread.
cases.exam: The infant is lethargic and mottled with delayed capillary refill and sunken fontanelle, indicating dehydration and shock. The genital exam shows clitoromegaly with partially fused, rugated, hyperpigmented labioscrotal folds and no palpable gonads — atypical/virilized genitalia in a phenotypically ambiguous infant. There is diffuse skin hyperpigmentation, most notable in the genital area and areolae. The abdomen is soft. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia without murmur. Lungs are clear with mild tachypnea. The anterior fontanelle is depressed. Neurologic exam shows hypotonia and decreased activity.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
Sodium: 122 mEq/L
Potassium: 7.4 mEq/L
Glucose: 38 mg/dL
Bicarbonate: 14 mEq/L (metabolic acidosis)
17-hydroxyprogesterone: markedly elevated
ECG: peaked T waves
Karyotype (pending): 46,XX

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: This salt-wasting, virilized 46,XX infant has a markedly elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone. What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Classic salt-wasting congenital adrenal hyperplasia from 21-hydroxylase deficiency is the most common cause (>90% of CAH). The enzyme block impairs cortisol AND aldosterone synthesis while shunting precursors into androgens — causing salt-wasting crisis (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypotension, hypoglycemia) plus virilization of a 46,XX infant. The hallmark lab is a markedly elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone, the substrate just above the block. 11β-hydroxylase deficiency causes virilization but with HYPERtension and hypokalemia (from deoxycorticosterone excess), not salt wasting. 17α-hydroxylase deficiency causes hypertension and UNDER-virilization. Aromatase deficiency causes maternal/fetal virilization but not this adrenal salt-wasting biochemical picture.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The infant is in a salt-wasting adrenal crisis with hyperkalemia and hypoglycemia. What is the most urgent first intervention?
options: Oral hydrocortisone and follow-up in clinic
IV isotonic saline with dextrose plus IV hydrocortisone, and treat the hyperkalemia
stages.explanation: This is a life-threatening salt-wasting adrenal crisis. Emergent management is IV isotonic saline (for shock and hyponatremia) WITH dextrose (for hypoglycemia) and IV hydrocortisone (stress-dose glucocorticoid, which at high dose also provides mineralocorticoid activity), plus treatment of dangerous hyperkalemia (calcium gluconate for cardiac membrane stabilization, insulin-with-dextrose, etc.). Oral hydrocortisone with outpatient follow-up is grossly inadequate for a shocked infant. Giving IV potassium would be lethal given a potassium of 7.4. Fludrocortisone alone does not address the acute cortisol deficiency, hypoglycemia, or shock. Insulin WITHOUT dextrose would dangerously worsen the existing hypoglycemia.

stages.label: MAINTENANCE
stages.question: After stabilization, what is the appropriate long-term treatment for classic salt-wasting CAH?
options: Glucocorticoid (hydrocortisone) plus mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone), with sodium chloride supplementation in infancy
stages.explanation: Classic salt-wasting CAH requires lifelong glucocorticoid (hydrocortisone) to replace cortisol and suppress excess ACTH-driven androgen production, PLUS mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone) to replace aldosterone and prevent salt wasting. Infants additionally need sodium chloride (salt) supplementation because of high salt requirements and renal immaturity. Hydrocortisone alone leaves the aldosterone deficiency untreated, risking recurrent salt-wasting crises. Fludrocortisone alone does not provide cortisol or suppress androgen excess (which would otherwise cause progressive virilization and growth/bone-age abnormalities). Spironolactone is an aldosterone ANTAGONIST and would dangerously worsen salt wasting and hyperkalemia. Families also need stress-dosing education for illness and surgery.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The karyotype confirms 46,XX. What is the appropriate counseling regarding the virilized external genitalia?
options: Immediate surgical correction is mandatory before discharge
Multidisciplinary evaluation; the child is a genetic female with functional ovaries/uterus, and decisions about any genital surgery are individualized and ideally deferred
Reassign the infant as male given the virilization
No further evaluation is needed once hormones are replaced
stages.explanation: A 46,XX infant with 21-hydroxylase deficiency has normal internal female anatomy (ovaries and uterus) and is virilized externally by excess prenatal androgens. Management requires a multidisciplinary team (endocrinology, urology/surgery, psychology, genetics) with thoughtful family counseling; the modern approach individualizes and often DEFERS irreversible genital surgery when feasible, given evolving ethical standards and the goal of preserving autonomy. Mandatory immediate surgery before discharge is not appropriate. Reassigning the genetic female as male is incorrect — she has female internal organs and fertility potential with proper treatment. Saying no further evaluation is needed ignores the essential multidisciplinary and genetic counseling and ongoing monitoring of growth and androgen control.

cases.diagnosis: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia, classic salt-wasting 21-hydroxylase deficiency
pearls: Classic salt-wasting 21-hydroxylase deficiency: salt-wasting crisis (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, shock) + virilization of a 46,XX infant + markedly elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone.
21-hydroxylase deficiency causes salt wasting; 11β-hydroxylase and 17α-hydroxylase deficiencies cause HYPERTENSION instead.
Acute crisis: IV isotonic saline + dextrose + stress-dose IV hydrocortisone, and emergently treat hyperkalemia; never give potassium or insulin without dextrose.
Lifelong therapy is hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone (+ salt in infancy); manage virilized genitalia with a multidisciplinary team and individualized, often deferred, surgical decisions.

cases.title: An Unexpected Adrenal Mass Found on a Scan
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred after a CT done for abdominal pain incidentally revealed an adrenal mass.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man is referred after a CT scan ordered for evaluation of self-limited abdominal pain (later attributed to gastroenteritis) incidentally revealed a left adrenal mass. He feels generally well and is asymptomatic. He denies episodic headache, palpitations, sweating, or spells. He has no easy bruising, striae, weight gain, or muscle weakness. PMH: hypertension on amlodipine and well-controlled type 2 diabetes on metformin. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, accountant. Family history: no known endocrine tumors or adrenal cancer; father had prostate cancer. Review of systems is largely negative. He has no history of any prior malignancy, which lowers (but does not eliminate) the pre-test probability of metastasis. He is anxious about whether the mass is cancer. The task is a systematic functional and malignancy assessment of an incidentally discovered adrenal mass.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. There is no Cushingoid habitus, no facial plethora, no dorsocervical fat pad, and no violaceous striae. The skin shows no hyperpigmentation or bruising. There is no tremor or thyromegaly. Cardiac exam is normal without murmurs; lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft and nontender without palpable mass or bruit. There is no peripheral edema and no orthostatic change in blood pressure. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. Overall the exam is notable for the ABSENCE of stigmata of hormone excess.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For any incidentally discovered adrenal mass, the two fundamental questions are whether it is functional and whether it is malignant. Which functional workup should be done in essentially ALL patients?
options: 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test, plasma free metanephrines, and (if hypertensive/hypokalemic) an aldosterone-to-renin ratio
No biochemical testing unless symptoms are present
stages.explanation: Every adrenal incidentaloma needs screening for autonomous cortisol secretion (1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test) and for pheochromocytoma (plasma free or 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines). An aldosterone-to-renin ratio is added when the patient has hypertension and/or hypokalemia — both present here. A single urinary free cortisol is insufficient and misses subclinical (mild autonomous) cortisol secretion better detected by dexamethasone suppression. Prolactin and IGF-1 evaluate pituitary disease, not adrenal masses. Withholding biochemical testing because the patient is asymptomatic is wrong — subclinical hormone excess (especially mild autonomous cortisol and silent pheochromocytoma) is common and clinically important to detect before any intervention.

stages.label: WORKUP RESULTS
stages.content:
Unenhanced CT: 2.6 cm left adrenal mass, 8 Hounsfield units, smooth margins, homogeneous
1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression: 8 a.m. cortisol 1.2 µg/dL (suppresses normally, <1.8)
Plasma free metanephrines: normal
Aldosterone-to-renin ratio: normal
Potassium: 4.2 mEq/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The mass measures 2.6 cm with 8 Hounsfield units on unenhanced CT and homogeneous, smooth margins. What does this imaging phenotype indicate?
options: Consistent with a benign, lipid-rich adrenal adenoma
stages.explanation: An adrenal mass with low unenhanced attenuation (≤10 Hounsfield units), small size, smooth margins, and homogeneity is the classic appearance of a benign, lipid-rich adrenal adenoma — the low HU reflects intracellular fat. Adrenocortical carcinoma is suggested by large size (>4 cm, and especially >6 cm), high unenhanced attenuation, heterogeneity, irregular margins, and rapid growth — none present here. Pheochromocytoma typically shows high attenuation (>20 HU) and avid, prolonged enhancement, and his metanephrines are normal. Metastases and other malignant lesions also tend to be lipid-poor with higher attenuation. The combination of nonfunctioning biochemistry and a benign imaging phenotype is reassuring.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Should a biopsy be performed to rule out malignancy in this lesion?
options: Yes — biopsy every adrenal mass to exclude cancer
No — adrenal biopsy is generally avoided; first ensure pheochromocytoma is excluded biochemically, and imaging characterizes most benign lesions
Yes — but only after starting alpha-blockade
Biopsy is the gold standard for distinguishing adenoma from carcinoma
stages.explanation: Adrenal biopsy is generally AVOIDED. It rarely changes management for a typical benign-appearing, nonfunctioning adenoma, and critically, biopsying an unsuspected pheochromocytoma can precipitate a hypertensive crisis — so pheochromocytoma must always be biochemically excluded first. Biopsy is poor at distinguishing a benign adrenal adenoma from adrenocortical carcinoma (it cannot reliably make that distinction histologically) and is reserved mainly for suspected metastasis in a patient with a known extra-adrenal primary after pheochromocytoma is ruled out. Routinely biopsying every adrenal mass is incorrect and potentially dangerous. Here, with benign imaging and negative hormonal workup, no biopsy is needed.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The 2.6 cm mass is nonfunctioning with benign imaging features. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassurance with conservative monitoring; surgery is reserved for functional tumors or imaging features concerning for malignancy
Repeat full hormonal and imaging workup every month indefinitely
stages.explanation: A small (<4 cm), nonfunctioning adrenal mass with clearly benign imaging features can be managed conservatively. Many guidelines now consider no further follow-up reasonable for a clearly benign, nonfunctioning lesion, while others recommend limited interval reassessment; surgery (adrenalectomy) is reserved for functional tumors, lesions >4 cm, or imaging features concerning for malignancy. Immediate adrenalectomy is unnecessary for this benign-appearing, nonfunctioning lesion. Mitotane is a treatment for adrenocortical carcinoma, which this is not. Monthly indefinite repeat full workups are excessive, costly, and not guideline-supported. Reassurance with judicious, limited follow-up is the appropriate, value-conscious approach.

pearls: Every adrenal incidentaloma needs two questions answered: is it functional (1 mg dexamethasone suppression + metanephrines, plus ARR if hypertensive/hypokalemic) and is it malignant (imaging)?
Benign adenoma imaging: small, smooth, homogeneous, ≤10 unenhanced Hounsfield units (lipid-rich); concerning features are >4 cm, high HU, heterogeneity, and rapid growth.
Avoid adrenal biopsy — always exclude pheochromocytoma first (biopsy can trigger a crisis), and biopsy cannot reliably distinguish adenoma from adrenocortical carcinoma.
Operate for functional tumors or imaging suspicious for malignancy; small, nonfunctioning, benign-appearing lesions can be managed conservatively.

cases.title: Bigger Hands, Changing Face, and Rings That No Longer Fit
cases.chiefComplaint: Gradual enlargement of the hands and feet with coarsening facial features over several years.
cases.history: A 46-year-old man presents because his wedding ring no longer fits and he has had to buy larger shoes twice in the past 3 years. He notes his facial features have coarsened, his jaw seems more prominent, and his voice has deepened. He has headaches, excessive sweating, and joint pain in the hands and knees. PMH: hypertension on lisinopril, type 2 diabetes diagnosed last year on metformin, and obstructive sleep apnea. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, minimal alcohol, mechanic. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: increased spacing between his teeth, deepening voice, decreased libido, and tingling in the hands (carpal tunnel symptoms). He denies visual changes but has had a few episodes of bumping into door frames. The slowly progressive acral and facial changes, new diabetes, hypertension, sleep apnea, and carpal tunnel are the unifying clues pointing to chronic growth hormone excess.
cases.exam: The patient has coarse facial features with frontal bossing, a prominent supraorbital ridge, an enlarged nose, and prognathism with increased interdental spacing. The hands are large and 'spade-like' with thickened, doughy soft tissue; Tinel and Phalen signs are positive bilaterally. The feet are enlarged. There is mild thyromegaly and several skin tags. The skin is oily with excessive perspiration. Cardiac exam is normal aside from hypertension. Visual field testing by confrontation suggests subtle bitemporal peripheral loss. There is mild proximal weakness. The tongue appears enlarged (macroglossia).

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which is the best initial screening test for acromegaly?
options: Growth hormone level after insulin-induced hypoglycemia
stages.explanation: Serum IGF-1 is the best screening test for acromegaly because IGF-1 (produced by the liver in response to growth hormone) integrates GH secretion over time and is stable throughout the day, unlike GH, which is secreted in pulses. A random GH level is unreliable because it fluctuates widely and can be normal or even low between pulses. Morning cortisol assesses the adrenal axis, not GH. Insulin-induced hypoglycemia is used to test for GH DEFICIENCY (stimulation), not excess. An age- and sex-adjusted elevated IGF-1 strongly supports acromegaly and prompts confirmatory dynamic testing.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
IGF-1: markedly elevated for age/sex
Fasting glucose: 152 mg/dL
HbA1c: 7.4%
Phosphorus: high-normal
Prolactin: mildly elevated
Free T4 and TSH: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: IGF-1 is elevated. What is the confirmatory test for acromegaly?
options: Oral glucose tolerance test with growth hormone measurement (failure of GH to suppress)
stages.explanation: The confirmatory test for acromegaly is the oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) with serial GH measurement: in normal individuals, a glucose load suppresses GH to a low nadir, whereas in acromegaly GH fails to suppress appropriately. This confirms autonomous GH secretion. A pituitary MRI is the next step to LOCALIZE the tumor AFTER biochemical confirmation, not to make the biochemical diagnosis. A repeat random GH is unhelpful because of pulsatile secretion. The dexamethasone suppression test evaluates cortisol excess (Cushing syndrome), not GH. The mildly elevated prolactin may reflect co-secretion or stalk effect from the adenoma.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
OGTT: GH fails to suppress (nadir GH remains elevated)
Pituitary MRI: 1.4 cm pituitary macroadenoma with suprasellar extension abutting the optic chiasm
Formal visual fields: bitemporal hemianopia
Colonoscopy recommended due to increased polyp/cancer risk

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: MRI confirms a GH-secreting pituitary macroadenoma abutting the optic chiasm with a visual field defect. What is the first-line treatment?
options: Medical therapy with a somatostatin analog alone
Transsphenoidal surgical resection of the adenoma
stages.explanation: Transsphenoidal surgical resection is first-line for most GH-secreting pituitary adenomas, particularly here where a macroadenoma compresses the optic chiasm and causes a visual field defect — surgery both controls GH and decompresses the chiasm. Medical therapy with somatostatin analogs (octreotide, lanreotide) is used preoperatively in select cases, for residual/persistent disease after surgery, or when surgery is not feasible, but it is not first-line for a chiasm-compressing tumor needing decompression. Radiosurgery has a delayed effect and is reserved for residual or recurrent disease. Observation is inappropriate given active disease, the metabolic complications, and the threatened vision.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Acromegaly carries significant long-term morbidity and mortality. Which is the leading cause of death in untreated acromegaly, and what screening is indicated?
options: Cardiovascular disease; screen with echocardiography and aggressive blood pressure/glucose control
Adrenal crisis; screen with cosyntropin testing
stages.explanation: Cardiovascular disease (hypertension, cardiomyopathy, heart failure, arrhythmias) is the leading cause of mortality in acromegaly, so management includes aggressive control of blood pressure and glucose and cardiac evaluation such as echocardiography. Patients also need screening for colon polyps/cancer (increased risk — hence the recommended colonoscopy), evaluation for obstructive sleep apnea, and monitoring for diabetes and arthropathy. Adrenal crisis is not the characteristic risk of GH excess (though hypopituitarism can follow treatment). Acromegaly is not primarily associated with hyperthyroidism — diffuse thyromegaly may occur but it is usually euthyroid. While bone/joint disease occurs, isolated DEXA screening does not address the dominant cardiovascular and colorectal risks.

pearls: Screen for acromegaly with IGF-1 (stable, integrates GH secretion); a random GH is unreliable due to pulsatility.
Confirm with an oral glucose tolerance test showing failure of GH to suppress, then obtain pituitary MRI to localize the adenoma.
Transsphenoidal surgery is first-line, especially with chiasmal compression; somatostatin analogs and radiosurgery are adjuncts for residual/inoperable disease.
Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death; also screen for colon cancer, sleep apnea, and diabetes and treat metabolic complications aggressively.

cases.title: Milky Discharge and Missed Periods in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Milky breast discharge and absent periods for the past five months.
cases.history: A 28-year-old nulligravid woman presents with bilateral milky nipple discharge that occurs spontaneously and with minimal compression. She has not had a menstrual period in five months; previously her cycles were regular every 28 days. She also reports decreased libido and dyspareunia from vaginal dryness. Over the past two months she has noticed dull bifrontal headaches and difficulty seeing cars approaching from the sides while driving, twice nearly sideswiping vehicles. She denies recent pregnancy and a urine pregnancy test at home was negative. Past medical history is unremarkable. Her only medication is an over-the-counter multivitamin; she takes no antipsychotics, antiemetics, or opioids. She has no known drug allergies. She drinks alcohol socially, does not smoke, and works as a graphic designer. There is no family history of pituitary or endocrine tumors. Review of systems is negative for cold intolerance, constipation, polyuria, and skin changes. She has not been exercising excessively and has had no significant weight change.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, in no distress. Visual field testing by confrontation reveals a bitemporal hemianopsia. Extraocular movements are intact and pupils are equal and reactive. Thyroid is normal in size without nodules. Breast exam demonstrates expressible bilateral milky discharge with no masses, skin changes, or lymphadenopathy. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are unremarkable. There is no acne, hirsutism, striae, or acanthosis nigricans. Neurologic exam is otherwise nonfocal with normal strength, sensation, and reflexes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After confirming the negative pregnancy test, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Measure serum prolactin, TSH, and renal function
Order an immediate brain MRI without laboratory testing
Reassure and repeat menstrual history in three months
stages.explanation: Amenorrhea with galactorrhea points to hyperprolactinemia, so the first step is to measure serum prolactin and exclude common secondary causes such as hypothyroidism (TSH) and renal failure; pregnancy is already excluded. Imaging without labs is premature and may miss medication or systemic causes. Empiric dopamine agonist therapy treats before confirming a diagnosis and could mask a workable etiology. Surgery is never first-line for prolactinoma and is reserved for refractory or apoplectic cases. Reassurance ignores red-flag visual symptoms suggesting a mass and is inappropriate.

stages.label: LABORATORY RESULTS
stages.content:
Prolactin: 312 ng/mL (normal <25)
TSH: 1.8 µIU/mL
Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL
Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL
β-hCG: negative
LH: 2.1 IU/L
FSH: 3.0 IU/L
Estradiol: 18 pg/mL (low)

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: The prolactin level of 312 ng/mL with normal thyroid and renal function most strongly supports which conclusion?
options: A drug-induced cause is most likely
The elevation is consistent with stalk-effect compression alone
A prolactin-secreting macroadenoma is the most likely diagnosis
The value reflects the hook effect and is falsely low
stages.explanation: Prolactin levels above roughly 200-250 ng/mL are almost always due to a prolactin-secreting adenoma, and with bitemporal hemianopsia a macroadenoma (>10 mm) is most likely. Drug-induced and stalk-effect hyperprolactinemia typically produce values under 100-150 ng/mL; she takes no offending drugs. The hook effect causes a falsely LOW reading in very large tumors, but here the value is already markedly high, and if suspected you dilute the sample. Thyroid function is normal, excluding hypothyroidism.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging study should be obtained to confirm and characterize the lesion?
options: MRI of the pituitary with and without gadolinium
stages.explanation: MRI of the pituitary with and without gadolinium is the gold standard for visualizing pituitary adenomas and assessing optic chiasm compression and cavernous sinus invasion. Non-contrast CT lacks soft-tissue resolution for the sella and chiasm. Octreotide scintigraphy is used for somatostatin-receptor tumors, not standard prolactinoma workup. Skull radiographs are obsolete for this purpose. PET is not indicated for a suspected benign pituitary adenoma and adds no useful detail over MRI.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: MRI confirms a 14 mm pituitary macroadenoma compressing the optic chiasm. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
stages.explanation: Even with chiasmal compression, dopamine agonists such as cabergoline are first-line for prolactinomas; they shrink the tumor and normalize prolactin in the large majority of patients, often restoring vision. Cabergoline is preferred over bromocriptine for efficacy and tolerability. Surgery is reserved for tumors resistant or intolerant to medical therapy, or acute apoplexy with progressive visual loss. Radiosurgery is third-line. Observation is unsafe with active chiasmal compression. Estrogen treats hypogonadal symptoms but does not address the tumor and may stimulate it.

pearls: Prolactin levels >200 ng/mL are almost always due to a prolactinoma; mild elevations suggest drugs, hypothyroidism, renal failure, or stalk effect.
Always exclude pregnancy, hypothyroidism, and offending medications before imaging in hyperprolactinemia.
Dopamine agonists (cabergoline preferred) are first-line even for macroadenomas with chiasmal compression—surgery is reserved for refractory disease.
Suspect the hook effect (falsely low prolactin) when a large sellar mass shows only mildly elevated prolactin; repeat with serial dilution.

cases.title: Relentless Thirst and Urination After a Fall
cases.chiefComplaint: Excessive urination and intense thirst following a recent head injury.
cases.history: A 30-year-old man is admitted to the neurosurgery ward after a motorcycle collision four days ago that caused a basilar skull fracture and a small frontal contusion. Over the past 48 hours nursing staff note he is producing very large volumes of dilute urine, more than 6 liters in the last 24 hours, and he complains of unquenchable thirst, repeatedly asking for water. He feels weak and lightheaded when standing. He has no history of diabetes mellitus, kidney disease, or psychiatric illness, and takes no diuretics, lithium, or other chronic medications. His only current medications are acetaminophen and a stool softener. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol occasionally and does not use recreational drugs. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for fever, dysuria, and visual changes. His mental status has been at baseline since admission. Intake and output records confirm urine output consistently exceeding 300 mL/hr, and he has not been receiving large volumes of intravenous fluids.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented but appears mildly dehydrated with dry mucous membranes and decreased skin turgor. There is orthostatic drop in blood pressure on standing. Pupils are equal and reactive; extraocular movements intact; no visual field defect. There is periorbital ecchymosis consistent with the basilar skull fracture. Cardiopulmonary exam reveals tachycardia without murmurs or rales. Abdomen is soft and nontender; the bladder is not distended. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with normal strength and sensation. No peripheral edema is present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which set of initial laboratory studies best evaluates this polyuria?
options: Serum and urine osmolality, serum sodium, and serum glucose
Complete blood count and blood cultures
Thyroid panel and morning cortisol only
stages.explanation: Polyuria with intense thirst after head trauma raises concern for central diabetes insipidus. The key initial labs are paired serum and urine osmolality, serum sodium, and glucose to characterize free-water loss and exclude osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia. CBC and cultures address infection, not polyuria. A thyroid/cortisol panel is part of broader pituitary assessment but does not directly characterize the diuresis. Urinalysis with culture evaluates infection. A 24-hour protein/clearance study assesses glomerular disease, which is not suggested here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum sodium: 152 mEq/L
Serum osmolality: 312 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality: 110 mOsm/kg
Urine specific gravity: 1.003
Serum glucose: 96 mg/dL
Urine output: 320 mL/hr
MRI pituitary: loss of normal posterior pituitary bright spot; stalk intact

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: These results showing hypernatremia with inappropriately dilute urine are most consistent with which diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Hypernatremia with high serum osmolality but inappropriately dilute urine (osmolality 110 mOsm/kg, specific gravity 1.003) indicates the kidney is failing to conserve water despite a strong osmotic stimulus—classic for diabetes insipidus. Primary polydipsia causes hyponatremia or normal sodium because excess water intake drives the process, not hypernatremia. Glucose is normal, excluding osmotic diuresis. SIADH causes hyponatremia with concentrated urine—the opposite picture. ATN typically causes oliguria or isosthenuria with a rising creatinine, not free-water diuresis.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: To distinguish central from nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Restrict all oral fluids for 24 hours without monitoring
Administer desmopressin and measure the change in urine osmolality
Start a thiazide diuretic and observe response
Give a large normal saline bolus and recheck sodium
Measure serum copeptin only and stop the workup
stages.explanation: After confirming hypotonic polyuria, giving desmopressin (a vasopressin analog) and measuring the urine osmolality response differentiates the two: a substantial rise in urine osmolality indicates central DI (the kidney can respond to ADH), whereas little or no rise indicates nephrogenic DI (renal resistance). Unmonitored fluid restriction is dangerous in a hypernatremic, dehydrated patient. A thiazide treats nephrogenic DI but is not diagnostic here. A saline bolus worsens hypernatremia and does not localize the defect. Copeptin alone can aid diagnosis but the desmopressin trial directly answers the central-versus-nephrogenic question.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After desmopressin, urine osmolality rises from 110 to 480 mOsm/kg and urine output falls. What is the appropriate ongoing management?
options: Scheduled desmopressin with sodium monitoring and free-water access
stages.explanation: A robust rise in urine osmolality after desmopressin confirms central DI, treated with desmopressin replacement, careful serum sodium monitoring, and ensuring access to water to correct the free-water deficit gradually. Post-traumatic central DI may be transient, so reassessment is warranted. Thiazides are used for nephrogenic DI, not central. Free-water restriction would worsen hypernatremia. Demeclocycline induces nephrogenic DI to treat SIADH—exactly the wrong direction here. Hypertonic saline would dangerously aggravate his hypernatremia.

pearls: Central DI presents with hypotonic polyuria, hypernatremia, and high serum osmolality with inappropriately dilute urine; intact thirst keeps many patients near-eunatremic until water access is limited.
Loss of the posterior pituitary bright spot on T1 MRI supports central DI.
A brisk rise in urine osmolality (>50%) after desmopressin confirms central rather than nephrogenic DI.
Post-traumatic and post-surgical central DI can be transient or follow a triphasic pattern—monitor sodium closely and avoid overcorrection.

cases.title: Years of Heavy Urination in a Man on Long-Term Medication
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent excessive urination and thirst for over a year.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents with more than a year of polyuria and constant thirst, producing an estimated 5-6 liters of dilute urine daily including frequent nocturia waking him three to four times nightly. He has bipolar disorder, well controlled on lithium carbonate 900 mg daily for the past twelve years, with a recent lithium level in the therapeutic range. He also has hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg daily and type 2 diabetes well controlled on metformin, with a recent HbA1c of 6.4%. He has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke and drinks minimal alcohol. Family history is notable for hypertension. Review of systems is negative for fever, dysuria, hematuria, and weight loss. He reports no headaches or visual changes. He has tried drinking less but becomes uncomfortably thirsty and lightheaded. His primary physician already documented a normal fasting glucose on this visit and a normal serum calcium. He has not changed his lithium dose recently and is adherent to all medications.
cases.exam: Alert, well-nourished man in no acute distress. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. No orthostatic changes today. Thyroid is normal. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal without murmurs or rales. Abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly; bladder is not distended. There is no peripheral edema. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with no tremor, normal reflexes, and intact sensation. Visual fields are full to confrontation. Skin is warm and dry without rashes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given his polyuria with controlled diabetes, what is the most appropriate first step to characterize the problem?
options: Check serum and urine osmolality, serum sodium, glucose, and calcium
Increase the lithium dose to improve control
Begin a fluid restriction trial at home
stages.explanation: Chronic polyuria requires characterizing the urine and ruling out osmotic causes: serum and urine osmolality, sodium, glucose, and calcium are the appropriate first labs. His HbA1c and fasting glucose are already controlled, lowering the likelihood of glucosuric diuresis, but confirmation and exclusion of hypercalcemia (another cause of nephrogenic DI) are important. Raising lithium would worsen any lithium-induced renal effect. Immediate MRI is premature without localizing the defect. Empiric desmopressin skips diagnosis. An unsupervised home fluid restriction is unsafe and uninformative.

stages.label: LABORATORY RESULTS
stages.content:
Serum sodium: 146 mEq/L
Serum osmolality: 298 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality: 180 mOsm/kg
Serum glucose: 102 mg/dL
Serum calcium: 9.4 mg/dL
Serum potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
Creatinine: 1.3 mg/dL
Lithium level: 0.8 mEq/L (therapeutic)
Urine specific gravity: 1.005

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After confirming hypotonic polyuria, a desmopressin challenge is performed. Urine osmolality rises only from 180 to 200 mOsm/kg. How is this best interpreted?
stages.explanation: A minimal rise in urine osmolality after desmopressin (here only ~11%, far below the ~50% expected) indicates the kidney cannot respond to ADH—nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. In central DI, urine osmolality would rise substantially because exogenous ADH replaces the deficient hormone. Primary polydipsia would already concentrate urine with water deprivation and presents with low-normal sodium, not the mild hypernatremia seen here. Osmotic diuresis is excluded by normal glucose and the dilute urine. A nonresponsive, persistently dilute urine is clearly not normal concentrating ability.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely underlying cause of his nephrogenic diabetes insipidus?
stages.explanation: Long-term lithium is the classic acquired cause of nephrogenic DI; lithium enters principal cells via the epithelial sodium channel and downregulates aquaporin-2 water channels, impairing ADH responsiveness even at therapeutic levels and after years of use. His calcium is normal, excluding hypercalcemia. There is no sicca picture to suggest Sjögren. Amlodipine is not associated with nephrogenic DI. His diabetes is well controlled with a normal glucose and HbA1c, so osmotic diuresis does not explain his dilute urine.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: In coordination with psychiatry, what is the most appropriate management of his lithium-induced nephrogenic DI if lithium must continue?
options: Amiloride and/or a thiazide diuretic with sodium restriction
stages.explanation: When lithium cannot be stopped, amiloride is particularly useful because it blocks lithium entry into principal cells via ENaC, and thiazides with mild sodium/volume restriction induce proximal sodium and water reabsorption, paradoxically reducing urine output in nephrogenic DI. Desmopressin is largely ineffective because the defect is renal resistance. Demeclocycline actually causes nephrogenic DI and is used to treat SIADH, the wrong direction. Loop diuretics impair concentrating ability and worsen polyuria. Hypertonic saline is not a treatment and would aggravate hypernatremia. Adequate water access remains essential.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which additional long-term concern should prompt monitoring in patients on chronic lithium?
stages.explanation: Chronic lithium can cause progressive chronic tubulointerstitial nephropathy and declining GFR, so renal function should be monitored periodically; his creatinine of 1.3 mg/dL already hints at early impairment. Lithium is also linked to hypothyroidism and hyperparathyroidism, but not to pulmonary fibrosis, hemolytic anemia, optic neuritis, or adrenal insufficiency. Recognizing renal risk guides the decision about continuing versus changing the mood stabilizer in collaboration with psychiatry.

cases.diagnosis: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus due to chronic lithium therapy
pearls: Lithium is the most common acquired cause of nephrogenic DI, downregulating aquaporin-2 via principal-cell ENaC entry—often persisting even after lithium is stopped.
Nephrogenic DI shows little or no rise in urine osmolality after desmopressin, distinguishing it from central DI.
Amiloride blocks lithium uptake by principal cells and, with thiazides plus sodium restriction, reduces urine output in lithium-induced nephrogenic DI.
Monitor for lithium-associated chronic kidney disease, hypothyroidism, and hyperparathyroidism over time.

cases.title: Confusion and Low Sodium in a Man with Lung Cancer
cases.chiefComplaint: New confusion and lethargy found to have low blood sodium.
cases.history: A 67-year-old man admitted for workup of a newly discovered lung mass develops increasing lethargy, mild confusion, and a headache over two days. He has a 50 pack-year smoking history and was recently told a biopsy showed small cell lung cancer. He denies vomiting, diarrhea, or recent diuretic use, and he has not restricted his salt or water intake. He has hypertension treated with lisinopril 10 mg daily and osteoarthritis for which he occasionally takes acetaminophen. He takes no thiazides, SSRIs, or carbamazepine. He has no known drug allergies. He lives with his wife, drinks alcohol rarely, and is a former heavy smoker who quit two weeks ago. Family history is unremarkable. Nursing notes document normal blood pressure without orthostasis and good oral intake. He has had no fevers. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pain, dyspnea at rest, edema, and polyuria. His mental status has been progressively duller but he remains arousable and follows commands.
cases.exam: Drowsy but arousable man who is oriented to person and place but not the date. Mucous membranes are moist and there is no axillary dryness. There is no jugular venous distention and no peripheral edema; capillary refill is normal and there are no orthostatic vital sign changes. Lungs have decreased breath sounds at the right base. Cardiac exam is normal. Abdomen is soft and nontender without ascites. Neurologic exam shows no focal deficits but mild generalized slowing; deep tendon reflexes are normal and there is no asterixis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum sodium: 118 mEq/L
Serum osmolality: 248 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality: 540 mOsm/kg
Urine sodium: 62 mEq/L
Serum potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
Serum glucose: 98 mg/dL
TSH: 2.1 µIU/mL
Morning cortisol: 16 µg/dL
Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL
Chest CT: 4 cm right hilar mass with mediastinal adenopathy

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the volume status and laboratory data, what is the patient's most likely volume status and the best classification of his hyponatremia?
stages.explanation: He is clinically euvolemic—no edema, no JVD, moist membranes, no orthostasis—with low serum osmolality (248 mOsm/kg), confirming true hypotonic hyponatremia. Hypovolemic hyponatremia would show dry membranes, orthostasis, and typically low urine sodium (<20-30). Hypervolemic hyponatremia would show edema or JVD. Glucose is normal, excluding hyperglycemia-driven hypertonic hyponatremia. Serum osmolality is genuinely low, so isotonic pseudohyponatremia from paraproteins or lipids is excluded. The euvolemic, hypotonic picture is the setup for SIADH.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The concentrated urine (540 mOsm/kg) with urine sodium 62 mEq/L in a euvolemic, hypotonic patient with normal thyroid and cortisol best supports which diagnosis?
options: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion
stages.explanation: Inappropriately concentrated urine (>100 mOsm/kg, here 540) with elevated urine sodium (>30-40 mEq/L) in a euvolemic, hypotonic patient with normal thyroid and adrenal function defines SIADH—here a paraneoplastic effect of small cell lung cancer. Primary polydipsia produces maximally dilute urine (<100 mOsm/kg). Cerebral salt wasting presents with hypovolemia and a clear cerebral insult. Hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency are excluded by the normal TSH and morning cortisol, and these must be ruled out before diagnosing SIADH.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given his confusion and sodium of 118 mEq/L with only moderate symptoms, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Rapid correction with bolus 3% hypertonic saline to normal sodium within hours
Fluid restriction, with cautious hypertonic saline if symptoms worsen, correcting no more than 6-8 mEq/L in 24 hours
Aggressive normal saline at high rate
Free water administration to dilute concentrated urine
stages.explanation: For SIADH with mild-to-moderate symptoms, fluid restriction is the cornerstone, with judicious hypertonic saline reserved for worsening neurologic status, while limiting correction to roughly 6-8 mEq/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination. Rapid full correction risks osmotic demyelination syndrome. Normal saline can paradoxically worsen SIADH hyponatremia because the kidney retains free water and excretes the salt. Free water would deepen the hyponatremia. Tolvaptan combined with desmopressin is not standard initial therapy and risks erratic correction.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond correcting the sodium, what is the most important step to address the underlying cause?
options: Treat the small cell lung cancer with chemotherapy
stages.explanation: Because the SIADH is paraneoplastic, treating the small cell lung cancer with chemotherapy addresses the root cause and often improves the hyponatremia. Salt tablets and fluid restriction help manage the sodium but do not treat the malignancy. Chronic loop diuretics can adjunctively increase free-water excretion but are not the definitive fix. Lifelong desmopressin is a treatment for DI and would worsen SIADH. Sodium restriction is counterproductive and would aggravate hyponatremia.

cases.diagnosis: SIADH secondary to small cell lung cancer (paraneoplastic)
pearls: SIADH is euvolemic hypotonic hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine (>100 mOsm/kg) and urine sodium typically >30-40 mEq/L.
Always exclude hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency before diagnosing SIADH.
Correct sodium no faster than ~6-8 mEq/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome; fluid restriction is first-line for chronic SIADH.
Small cell lung cancer is a classic paraneoplastic cause of SIADH—treating the tumor treats the hyponatremia.

cases.title: A New Mother Who Cannot Breastfeed and Has No Periods
cases.chiefComplaint: Inability to produce breast milk and absent periods since a difficult delivery.
cases.history: A 33-year-old woman presents eight months after the birth of her second child. Her delivery was complicated by severe postpartum hemorrhage requiring multiple blood transfusions and a brief period of hypotension. Since then she has been unable to breastfeed because no milk came in, and her menstrual periods have not resumed despite stopping breastfeeding attempts months ago. She also reports persistent fatigue, cold intolerance, dry skin, constipation, loss of axillary and pubic hair, decreased appetite, occasional lightheadedness on standing, and low libido. She has no chronic medical conditions and takes only a prenatal vitamin. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke or drink. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for headache, visual changes, polyuria, and galactorrhea. She notes she has felt progressively more sluggish and depressed over the months and has lost a small amount of weight. She has not sought care until now because she attributed her symptoms to the stress of new motherhood.
cases.exam: Thin, pale, fatigued-appearing woman. Skin is dry and cool with fine texture; there is sparse axillary and pubic hair. There is mild periorbital puffiness. Thyroid is non-enlarged. Breasts show no galactorrhea and have involuted. There is a mild orthostatic drop in blood pressure. Cardiopulmonary exam reveals bradycardia without murmurs. Abdomen is soft and nontender. Pelvic exam shows atrophic vaginal mucosa. Neurologic exam reveals delayed relaxation of deep tendon reflexes. Visual fields are full to confrontation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the postpartum hemorrhage and multisystem symptoms, which pattern of hormone testing should be obtained first?
options: TSH alone, since hypothyroidism explains everything
A combined anterior pituitary panel: TSH and free T4, 8 AM cortisol, prolactin, LH, FSH, estradiol, and IGF-1
Pelvic ultrasound to assess the ovaries
stages.explanation: Failure to lactate plus amenorrhea after a hemorrhagic delivery suggests Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary infarction), which causes deficiencies across multiple anterior pituitary axes. A comprehensive panel—free T4 with TSH, an 8 AM cortisol, prolactin, gonadotropins with estradiol, and IGF-1—is needed because each axis can fail. TSH alone misses central hypothyroidism, where TSH is low or inappropriately normal. A random GH is uninterpretable. Pelvic ultrasound does not address the central process. Urine cortisol assesses excess, not the deficiency suspected here.

stages.label: LABORATORY RESULTS
stages.content:
Free T4: 0.5 ng/dL (low)
TSH: 0.8 µIU/mL (inappropriately normal/low)
8 AM cortisol: 2.4 µg/dL (low)
ACTH: 9 pg/mL (low-normal)
Prolactin: 2 ng/mL (low)
LH: 0.8 IU/L
FSH: 1.2 IU/L
Estradiol: 8 pg/mL (low)
IGF-1: low for age
Sodium: 132 mEq/L

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: How are these results best interpreted?
options: Panhypopituitarism with central deficiencies of multiple axes
Primary hypothyroidism and primary ovarian failure
stages.explanation: Low free T4 with a non-elevated TSH (central hypothyroidism), low cortisol with low-normal ACTH (secondary adrenal insufficiency), low prolactin, and low gonadotropins with low estradiol together indicate failure of multiple anterior pituitary axes—panhypopituitarism, here from Sheehan syndrome. Primary thyroid/ovarian failure would show HIGH TSH and HIGH FSH/LH. Isolated adrenal insufficiency ignores the thyroid, gonadal, and lactotroph deficits. Cushing is hormone excess. PCOS features androgen excess and normal-to-high LH, the opposite of this hypogonadotropic picture.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: When initiating hormone replacement, which principle is most important?
options: Begin estrogen replacement first to restore menses
Start growth hormone before any other hormone
Replace all hormones simultaneously at full doses
stages.explanation: Glucocorticoids must be replaced before levothyroxine because starting thyroid hormone first raises metabolic rate and cortisol clearance, which can precipitate an adrenal crisis in a patient with coexisting secondary adrenal insufficiency. Giving levothyroxine first is therefore dangerous. Estrogen and growth hormone are important but not the immediate life-threatening priority. Starting everything at once at full doses risks the same crisis and is not how replacement is sequenced; cortisol comes first, then thyroid, then gonadal steroids.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging finding would best support the suspected underlying diagnosis?
options: An empty or partially empty sella on MRI
stages.explanation: Sheehan syndrome results from ischemic necrosis of the enlarged peripartum pituitary; over time MRI often shows an empty or partially empty sella as the infarcted gland involutes. A macroadenoma would suggest a tumor causing hypopituitarism, not postpartum infarction. Adrenal masses would indicate a primary adrenal process. Polycystic ovaries describe PCOS. Thyroid enlargement reflects primary thyroid disease. The empty sella fits the clinical history of postpartum pituitary necrosis.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She develops vomiting and hypotension during an intercurrent febrile illness. What is the most urgent intervention?
options: Administer stress-dose intravenous hydrocortisone and IV fluids
stages.explanation: A patient with secondary adrenal insufficiency under physiologic stress (fever, vomiting, hypotension) is at risk for adrenal crisis and needs immediate stress-dose IV hydrocortisone plus IV fluids; this is life-saving. Increasing levothyroxine does nothing for the crisis and could worsen it. Insulin is irrelevant and dangerous without hyperglycemia. Desmopressin treats DI, not present here. Withholding medications would be fatal in adrenal crisis. All patients with adrenal insufficiency need sick-day glucocorticoid rules and emergency injectable hydrocortisone.

pearls: Failure to lactate plus amenorrhea after postpartum hemorrhage is the classic presentation of Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary infarction).
Central hypothyroidism shows low free T4 with an inappropriately low or normal TSH; central deficiencies require measuring the target gland hormone, not just the pituitary hormone.
Always replace glucocorticoids before levothyroxine to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis.
MRI may reveal an empty sella; patients need stress-dose steroid education and emergency injectable hydrocortisone.

cases.title: Sudden Worst Headache with Double Vision
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt severe headache with double vision and a drooping eyelid.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man arrives in the emergency department after the sudden onset, about three hours ago, of an excruciating headache he describes as the worst of his life, peaking within seconds. The headache is retro-orbital and frontal, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. He noticed double vision and that his right eyelid is drooping, and his vision in the right eye seems dim. He has a history of hypertension treated with amlodipine but no known intracranial disease; in retrospect he recalls several months of mild fatigue, decreased libido, and occasional headaches that he ignored. He has no known drug allergies and takes no anticoagulants. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems is otherwise notable for lightheadedness. He denies fever, neck stiffness, recent trauma, and prior similar episodes. He appears acutely unwell and anxious. His symptoms have progressed over the last hour with worsening of the eyelid droop and a sense that he feels faint when sitting up.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, diaphoretic man in obvious distress. He is hypotensive and tachycardic. Right eye shows ptosis, a dilated pupil, and limited adduction, elevation, and depression consistent with a third nerve palsy; right eye visual acuity is reduced to counting fingers. Left eye is normal. There is no nuchal rigidity. Cardiopulmonary exam shows tachycardia without murmurs. Abdomen is soft. Neurologic exam is otherwise nonfocal with normal strength and no other cranial nerve deficits. Skin is cool. There is no rash or papilledema on fundoscopy of the left eye.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Considering the hypotension and tachycardia in this presentation, which immediate intervention is most critical alongside emergent imaging?
options: Give intravenous mannitol for cerebral edema
Start a nicardipine drip to lower blood pressure
stages.explanation: The hypotension and tachycardia signal acute secondary adrenal insufficiency from sudden ACTH deficiency—a hallmark danger of pituitary apoplexy—so empiric stress-dose IV hydrocortisone is immediately life-saving and should be given without waiting for confirmatory cortisol. Mannitol is not indicated and could worsen hypotension. Lowering blood pressure is dangerous in a hypotensive patient. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with a possible mass and adds little. Antibiotics/acyclovir treat meningitis/encephalitis, but there is no fever or nuchal rigidity and the picture fits apoplexy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Sodium: 130 mEq/L
Glucose: 64 mg/dL
8 AM cortisol (drawn before steroids): 1.8 µg/dL
Non-contrast CT head: hyperdense sellar/suprasellar mass with hemorrhage; no subarachnoid blood
MRI: hemorrhage within an enlarged pituitary adenoma with suprasellar extension compressing the optic chiasm and extending toward the right cavernous sinus
WBC: 9.0 ×10³/µL
Platelets: 240 ×10³/µL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The imaging and clinical picture are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Subarachnoid hemorrhage from a ruptured aneurysm
Pituitary apoplexy (hemorrhage into a pituitary adenoma)
stages.explanation: Sudden thunderclap headache, ophthalmoplegia (third nerve palsy), visual loss, and hypopituitarism with MRI showing hemorrhage into an enlarged pituitary adenoma define pituitary apoplexy. Subarachnoid hemorrhage is a key mimic but the CT shows no subarachnoid blood and the mass is sellar. Meningitis lacks fever and nuchal rigidity. Cavernous sinus thrombosis usually involves proptosis, chemosis, and infection, without an intrasellar hemorrhagic adenoma. Migraine does not cause a third nerve palsy with hemorrhage and hypopituitarism.

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: His sodium is 130, glucose 64, and pre-treatment cortisol 1.8 µg/dL. Which hormone deficiency poses the most immediate threat to life?
stages.explanation: Acute ACTH deficiency causing cortisol deficiency is the immediate life threat in apoplexy: it produces hypotension, hyponatremia, and hypoglycemia and can cause fatal adrenal crisis, which is why glucocorticoids are given emergently. Growth hormone and prolactin deficiencies are not acutely dangerous. Gonadotropin deficiency causes hypogonadism but is not an emergency. Central hypothyroidism develops and matters, but its effects are slower and thyroid hormone should only be started after glucocorticoids to avoid precipitating crisis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After hemodynamic stabilization and steroids, he has progressive visual loss and worsening ophthalmoplegia. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Observation with serial visual field testing only
Urgent neurosurgical consultation for transsphenoidal decompression
stages.explanation: Pituitary apoplexy with progressive or severe visual deficits and worsening ophthalmoplegia warrants urgent neurosurgical evaluation for transsphenoidal decompression to relieve compression and preserve vision. Observation alone is appropriate only for stable, mild deficits, not deteriorating vision. Cabergoline with discharge is unsafe in this acute setting even if the tumor is a prolactinoma. Anticoagulation is contraindicated with acute intracranial hemorrhage. Radiation is not an acute therapy and does not address the emergent compression.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which long-term follow-up plan is most appropriate after recovery?
options: No endocrine follow-up needed once acute illness resolves
Lifelong assessment and replacement of pituitary hormone deficiencies with periodic reevaluation
Only annual visual field testing without hormone testing
stages.explanation: Most apoplexy patients have persistent hypopituitarism, so they require lifelong endocrine follow-up with assessment and replacement of deficient axes (cortisol first, then thyroid and gonadal hormones) plus periodic reevaluation, since some deficits recover and others emerge. Discharging without endocrine care risks fatal adrenal crisis. Visual field testing alone ignores the hormonal deficiencies. Prophylactic antibiotics and repeat lumbar punctures have no role in this condition.

cases.diagnosis: Pituitary apoplexy (hemorrhage into a pituitary adenoma)
pearls: Pituitary apoplexy presents with sudden severe headache, ophthalmoplegia (often CN III), and visual loss; MRI shows hemorrhage into a pituitary adenoma.
Acute ACTH/cortisol deficiency is the immediate life threat—give empiric stress-dose hydrocortisone before confirmatory testing.
Distinguish from subarachnoid hemorrhage: apoplexy shows an intrasellar hemorrhagic mass without diffuse subarachnoid blood.
Progressive or severe visual deficits warrant urgent transsphenoidal decompression; survivors usually need lifelong hormone replacement.

cases.title: Gradual Vision Trouble and Fatigue in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Bumping into objects on the sides and feeling persistently tired.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents because over the past year he has been bumping into door frames and people on either side and missing peripheral details while driving. He also reports gradually worsening fatigue, decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, and a vague heaviness behind his eyes with occasional dull headaches. He denies galactorrhea, weight gain with purple striae, palpitations, tremor, or change in shoe or ring size. He has hypertension on lisinopril 20 mg daily and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired teacher, drinks alcohol occasionally, and quit smoking years ago. Family history is unremarkable for endocrine tumors. Review of systems is negative for polyuria, cold intolerance, and skin changes, though he notes he has felt low energy and less interested in activities. He has not had recent infections or major stressors. His wife reports he seems slower and more withdrawn but has had no acute neurological events.
cases.exam: Well-developed man in no distress. Confrontation visual field testing reveals a bitemporal visual field defect, worse superiorly. Extraocular movements are intact and there is no ptosis. Fundoscopy shows mild optic disc pallor on the right. Thyroid is normal. There is no galactorrhea, no acromegalic features, and no Cushingoid changes. Body hair is sparse. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are normal. Testes are normal to slightly small. Neurologic exam is otherwise nonfocal with normal strength, sensation, and reflexes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the gradual bitemporal field defect and constitutional symptoms, what is the most appropriate combined initial workup?
options: Pituitary MRI plus a full anterior pituitary hormone panel
Empiric testosterone replacement without further testing
Formal perimetry alone with no labs or imaging
stages.explanation: A slowly progressive bitemporal hemianopsia points to a sellar mass compressing the optic chiasm, and his fatigue and hypogonadal symptoms suggest hormone deficiency; the right approach is pituitary MRI plus a comprehensive anterior pituitary panel (prolactin, IGF-1, morning cortisol/ACTH, TSH/free T4, LH/FSH/testosterone) to define both the lesion and any deficiencies or excess. Carotid ultrasound does not explain a chiasmal defect. Empiric testosterone skips diagnosis and could be unsafe. Perimetry alone characterizes the defect but misses the cause. EEG is irrelevant here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Prolactin: 38 ng/mL (mildly elevated)
IGF-1: normal for age
Morning cortisol: 6 µg/dL (low-normal)
ACTH: 14 pg/mL
TSH: 1.0 µIU/mL
Free T4: 0.6 ng/dL (low)
LH: 1.2 IU/L
FSH: 2.0 IU/L
Total testosterone: 120 ng/dL (low)
MRI: 2.2 cm sellar mass with suprasellar extension compressing the optic chiasm; no hemorrhage

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: The mildly elevated prolactin (38 ng/mL) with low free T4, low testosterone, and low gonadotropins is best explained by which mechanism?
options: Stalk effect with associated hypopituitarism from a nonfunctioning macroadenoma
stages.explanation: A large sellar mass with only mildly elevated prolactin (typically <100-150 ng/mL) plus multiple hormone deficiencies indicates the stalk effect—compression of the pituitary stalk disinhibits prolactin slightly while impairing other axes—characteristic of a nonfunctioning macroadenoma causing hypopituitarism. A prolactinoma of this size would usually drive prolactin into the hundreds or thousands. Primary hypothyroidism would show a high TSH, but TSH is normal with low free T4 (central pattern). IGF-1 is normal, excluding acromegaly, and there is no hypercortisolism to suggest Cushing disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before any surgical planning, which hormone deficiency must be addressed first for safety?
stages.explanation: Secondary adrenal insufficiency (low-normal cortisol with low ACTH in this context) must be identified and treated with glucocorticoids first, especially before surgery and before starting levothyroxine, because unrecognized cortisol deficiency under stress can precipitate fatal adrenal crisis. Testosterone and growth hormone replacement are not urgent. Thyroid replacement is needed but must follow glucocorticoids to avoid precipitating crisis. Prolactin is not replaced. Glucocorticoid coverage is the safety priority.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given the chiasmal compression and visual field loss with a nonfunctioning macroadenoma, what is the definitive treatment of the mass?
stages.explanation: For a nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma causing optic chiasm compression and visual field loss, transsphenoidal surgical resection is the definitive treatment to decompress the chiasm and preserve vision. Unlike prolactinomas, nonfunctioning adenomas do not reliably shrink with dopamine agonists, so cabergoline is not primary therapy. Observation is inappropriate with active visual loss. Octreotide is used for some hormone-secreting tumors, not standard here. Radiation is adjuvant for residual or recurrent tumor, not first-line for acute compression.

cases.diagnosis: Nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma with mass effect and hypopituitarism
pearls: Nonfunctioning macroadenomas present with mass effect—bitemporal hemianopsia and hypopituitarism—rather than a hormone-excess syndrome.
A large sellar mass with only mildly elevated prolactin (<100-150 ng/mL) suggests stalk effect, not a prolactinoma.
Identify and treat cortisol deficiency before levothyroxine and before surgery to avoid adrenal crisis.
Transsphenoidal surgery is definitive for nonfunctioning adenomas with chiasmal compression; dopamine agonists do not reliably shrink them.

cases.title: A Tall Teen with Breast Development and Lagging Puberty
cases.chiefComplaint: Breast tissue and concerns about incomplete puberty in a tall adolescent.
cases.history: A 17-year-old boy is brought in by his mother who is concerned about bilateral breast enlargement and that he seems less developed than peers despite being one of the tallest in his class. He reports minimal facial and body hair, infrequent shaving, and is self-conscious about his chest. He has had some learning difficulties and needed extra support in reading and language at school but is otherwise healthy. He takes no medications, supplements, anabolic steroids, or recreational drugs, and has no known drug allergies. He does not drink alcohol. Family history is unremarkable; he has no siblings. Review of systems is negative for headache, visual changes, galactorrhea, and weight change. His mother notes he had no major medical problems in childhood. He denies testicular pain or trauma. He is doing reasonably well socially but has low confidence about his physical development. There is no history of cryptorchidism noted, and he reached typical childhood developmental milestones aside from speech and reading delays.
cases.exam: Tall, slender adolescent with long limbs and an arm span exceeding height. There is bilateral, symmetric gynecomastia without masses or discharge. Facial and body hair are sparse and pubic hair is Tanner stage 3. Genital exam shows a normal-sized penis but small, firm testes (approximately 4 mL each). There is no galactorrhea. Thyroid is normal. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are unremarkable. There is no abdominal mass. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. Visual fields are full to confrontation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial laboratory studies best evaluate this pattern of tall stature, small firm testes, and gynecomastia?
options: Morning testosterone, LH, FSH, and estradiol
