stages.explanation: Small firm testes with gynecomastia and incomplete virilization suggest primary hypogonadism; the appropriate first labs are a morning testosterone with LH and FSH (to localize the defect) plus estradiol (relevant to gynecomastia). In primary testicular failure, low testosterone drives compensatory elevation of LH and FSH. TSH/free T4 and prolactin are reasonable in a gynecomastia workup but do not address the core hypogonadism pattern. Urine cortisol evaluates Cushing, not suggested here. GH stimulation testing is for growth hormone disorders, not this presentation.

stages.label: LABORATORY RESULTS
stages.content:
Total testosterone: 180 ng/dL (low)
LH: 22 IU/L (elevated)
FSH: 38 IU/L (elevated)
Estradiol: 42 pg/mL (high-normal)
Prolactin: 12 ng/mL
TSH: 1.6 µIU/mL
Morning glucose: 88 mg/dL

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: Low testosterone with elevated LH and FSH indicates which type of hypogonadism?
options: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty
stages.explanation: Low testosterone with elevated gonadotropins (LH and FSH) defines primary hypogonadism—the testes are failing and the pituitary is appropriately driving them harder. Secondary hypogonadism would show low or inappropriately normal LH/FSH. Constitutional delay typically presents with short stature and low-normal gonadotropins, not tall stature with high FSH/LH. Androgen insensitivity features a female phenotype or undervirilization with high testosterone. The hormone pattern with small firm testes is clearly abnormal, excluding normal puberty.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given tall stature, small firm testes, gynecomastia, learning difficulties, and hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, what is the most appropriate confirmatory test?
stages.explanation: This constellation is classic for Klinefelter syndrome, and a karyotype is the confirmatory test, typically revealing 47,XXY. Testicular ultrasound may be used to evaluate masses but does not establish the genetic diagnosis. Pituitary MRI is for central causes, but his gonadotropins are high, indicating a primary testicular problem. Semen analysis will show azoospermia or severe oligospermia but is not the diagnostic confirmation. Bone age helps assess growth potential but does not diagnose Klinefelter.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The karyotype confirms 47,XXY. What is the most appropriate long-term management for his hypogonadism?
options: Gonadotropin therapy to stimulate the testes
stages.explanation: Klinefelter syndrome causes primary testicular failure, so testosterone replacement therapy is the mainstay to promote virilization, bone health, energy, and libido, with monitoring of levels, hematocrit, and bone density. Gonadotropin therapy works only when the testes can respond, which they cannot in primary failure. No treatment leaves him hypogonadal with adverse skeletal and metabolic consequences. An aromatase inhibitor alone does not replace deficient testosterone. Estrogen would worsen feminization and gynecomastia. Fertility counseling and consideration of sperm retrieval are also important.

cases.diagnosis: Primary hypogonadism due to Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY)
pearls: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) presents with tall stature, small firm testes, gynecomastia, and learning/language difficulties.
Primary hypogonadism shows low testosterone with HIGH LH and FSH; karyotype confirms 47,XXY.
Testosterone replacement is the mainstay; gonadotropins are ineffective because the defect is testicular.
Patients have azoospermia and high infertility risk—offer fertility counseling and consider testicular sperm extraction.

cases.title: Delayed Puberty and a Missing Sense of Smell
cases.chiefComplaint: Lack of pubertal development and inability to smell.
cases.history: A 19-year-old man presents because he has not gone through puberty like his peers: he has minimal facial and body hair, a high-pitched voice, and has never needed to shave regularly. He has no morning erections and reports low libido. On detailed questioning he reveals he has never been able to smell anything, including food aromas or smoke, for as long as he can remember. He is otherwise healthy, takes no medications or supplements, and has no known drug allergies. He does not use anabolic steroids or drugs. He is a college student, does not smoke, and drinks rarely. Family history reveals a maternal uncle who also had delayed puberty. Review of systems is negative for headaches, visual changes, galactorrhea, polyuria, and weight change. He recalls being told as a child that one kidney was small. He has felt socially behind his peers and is anxious about his development. He has had no testicular trauma and no history of cryptorchidism that he is aware of.
cases.exam: Slim young man with eunuchoid body proportions (arm span greater than height). Facial and body hair are sparse and there is no temporal hair recession. There is no gynecomastia. Genital exam shows a small penis and small, soft prepubertal testes (about 3 mL) bilaterally, both descended. Formal scratch-and-sniff testing confirms anosmia. Thyroid is normal. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are unremarkable. Visual fields are full to confrontation, and extraocular movements are intact. Neurologic exam is otherwise nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial set of hormone studies?
stages.explanation: Delayed puberty with sexual infantilism requires measuring a morning testosterone alongside LH and FSH to determine whether the defect is testicular (primary, high gonadotropins) or central (secondary, low/inappropriately normal gonadotropins). TSH/free T4 screens for thyroid contributors but is not the core test. Random cortisol and IGF-1 assess other axes that are not the focus here. Urine catecholamines evaluate pheochromocytoma, which is unrelated. The testosterone-with-gonadotropins panel localizes the lesion.

stages.label: LABORATORY RESULTS
stages.content:
Total testosterone: 90 ng/dL (low)
LH: 0.6 IU/L (low)
FSH: 1.0 IU/L (low)
Prolactin: 9 ng/mL
TSH: 1.4 µIU/mL
Free T4: 1.0 ng/dL
Morning cortisol: 14 µg/dL
Bone age radiograph: delayed relative to chronologic age

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: Low testosterone with low LH and FSH indicates which problem, and in this patient what is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Low testosterone with low (inappropriately normal) LH and FSH defines hypogonadotropic (secondary/central) hypogonadism, and the combination with congenital anosmia is classic for Kallmann syndrome—failed migration of GnRH neurons and olfactory neurons from the olfactory placode. Klinefelter and other primary causes would show HIGH gonadotropins. Constitutional delay is a diagnosis of exclusion and does not cause lifelong anosmia. Testicular torsion is unrelated and would not produce this hormone pattern. The anosmia is the key clue distinguishing Kallmann.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which additional finding would most strongly support Kallmann syndrome over isolated constitutional delay?
options: Absent or hypoplastic olfactory bulbs on brain MRI
stages.explanation: Brain MRI showing absent or hypoplastic olfactory bulbs and sulci supports Kallmann syndrome, correlating with the anosmia and the developmental failure of GnRH/olfactory neuron migration. Associated anomalies such as unilateral renal agenesis (recall his small kidney) and midline defects also occur. Elevated prolactin would suggest a different cause. A high FSH would indicate primary, not central, hypogonadism. An enlarged sella suggests a mass. Hypercalcemia is unrelated. The olfactory bulb finding is the anatomic correlate of Kallmann.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He wishes to develop secondary sexual characteristics now but also hopes for fertility in the future. What is the best long-term management strategy?
options: Testosterone replacement for virilization, with gonadotropin or pulsatile GnRH therapy when fertility is desired
Lifelong testosterone only, with no fertility options available
Observation, since puberty will eventually occur on its own
stages.explanation: In Kallmann syndrome, testosterone replacement induces and maintains virilization, while fertility can later be achieved with gonadotropin therapy (hCG with FSH) or pulsatile GnRH, since the testes and pituitary can respond to appropriate stimulation. Testosterone-only with no fertility option is inaccurate—fertility is often attainable. Observation is wrong because this is a permanent congenital deficiency, not constitutional delay. An aromatase inhibitor does not provide androgen. Estrogen would feminize him. The two-pronged approach addresses both virilization and future fertility.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which associated congenital anomaly mentioned in his history should be specifically evaluated?
stages.explanation: Kallmann syndrome is associated with midline and renal anomalies, and his childhood history of a small kidney should prompt evaluation for unilateral renal agenesis, a recognized association. Polycystic ovaries are irrelevant in a male and unrelated. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia causes a different hormonal picture with virilization or salt-wasting. Thyroid nodules and pituitary macroadenoma are not characteristic associations. Other Kallmann features can include synkinesia (mirror movements), cleft lip/palate, and dental agenesis.

cases.diagnosis: Male hypogonadotropic hypogonadism due to Kallmann syndrome
pearls: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism shows low testosterone with low or inappropriately normal LH and FSH.
Delayed puberty plus congenital anosmia equals Kallmann syndrome—failed migration of GnRH and olfactory neurons.
Brain MRI shows absent/hypoplastic olfactory bulbs; associations include unilateral renal agenesis, synkinesia, and midline defects.
Testosterone induces virilization; gonadotropins or pulsatile GnRH can restore fertility because the gonadal axis can respond to stimulation.

cases.title: Irregular Periods, Acne, and Unwanted Hair Growth
cases.chiefComplaint: Infrequent periods with worsening acne and excess facial hair.
cases.history: A 24-year-old woman presents with irregular, infrequent menstrual periods occurring roughly every two to three months since her late teens, along with worsening acne and increasing dark coarse hair on her upper lip, chin, and lower abdomen over the past few years. She has gained about 15 pounds in two years. She is not pregnant, is sexually active using condoms, and has never been able to conceive, though she has not been actively trying. She takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is notable for type 2 diabetes in her mother and irregular periods in a sister. Review of systems is negative for galactorrhea, rapid virilization, deepening voice, headaches, and visual changes. She denies heat or cold intolerance and has no purple striae. She reports the hair growth and acne are distressing and affecting her self-esteem. She has had no sudden or dramatic symptom onset, and her symptoms have developed gradually.
cases.exam: Overweight young woman in no distress (BMI 29). There is moderate hirsutism on the upper lip, chin, and lower abdomen, and facial acne. There is mild acanthosis nigricans at the nape of the neck and axillae. There is no galactorrhea, no clitoromegaly, no temporal balding, and no abdominal mass. Thyroid is normal. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are unremarkable. There are no purple striae, no proximal muscle weakness, and no moon facies. Neurologic exam is nonfocal and visual fields are full.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After confirming a negative pregnancy test, which initial laboratory workup is most appropriate?
options: Total and free testosterone, TSH, prolactin, and 17-hydroxyprogesterone
stages.explanation: Oligomenorrhea with hirsutism and acne suggests androgen excess, most commonly PCOS, but other causes must be excluded; the appropriate workup includes total and free testosterone (to assess androgen excess and screen for very high levels suggesting a tumor), TSH and prolactin (to exclude thyroid disease and hyperprolactinemia), and 17-hydroxyprogesterone (to screen for nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia). Brain MRI is not first-line without focal pituitary signs. Pelvic CT is not the initial step. Growth hormone and lithium level are irrelevant to this presentation.

stages.label: LABORATORY RESULTS
stages.content:
β-hCG: negative
Total testosterone: 78 ng/dL (mildly elevated)
Free testosterone: elevated
LH/FSH ratio: ~2.5:1
TSH: 1.9 µIU/mL
Prolactin: 14 ng/mL
17-hydroxyprogesterone: normal
DHEAS: upper-normal
Fasting glucose: 104 mg/dL
Lipid panel: low HDL, mildly elevated triglycerides
Pelvic ultrasound: multiple small follicles in both ovaries (polycystic morphology)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the Rotterdam criteria, which diagnosis best fits this clinical and laboratory picture?
stages.explanation: She meets Rotterdam criteria for PCOS (at least two of: oligo/anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovaries) with all three present and other causes excluded. Nonclassic CAH is excluded by a normal 17-hydroxyprogesterone. An androgen-secreting tumor typically causes rapid virilization with markedly high testosterone (often >150-200 ng/dL) or high DHEAS, not the mild elevation seen here. Cushing syndrome lacks supporting features (no striae, weakness, or moon facies). Idiopathic hirsutism has regular menses and normal androgens, unlike this patient.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is not currently seeking pregnancy and wants help with her irregular cycles, acne, and hirsutism. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
options: Metformin monotherapy as primary treatment for hirsutism
stages.explanation: For a woman with PCOS not seeking pregnancy, combined oral contraceptives are first-line: they regulate menses, protect the endometrium from unopposed estrogen, and reduce androgens to improve acne and hirsutism. Clomiphene and gonadotropins are ovulation-induction agents for those trying to conceive, not appropriate here. Metformin helps insulin resistance and menstrual regularity but is not primary therapy for hirsutism. Spironolactone is an effective antiandrogen but is teratogenic and must be paired with reliable contraception, so it is added to, not used alone instead of, an OCP.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given her fasting glucose of 104 mg/dL, weight gain, and acanthosis nigricans, what long-term complication should be screened for and addressed?
options: Type 2 diabetes mellitus and metabolic syndrome
stages.explanation: PCOS is strongly associated with insulin resistance, and her impaired fasting glucose (104 mg/dL), central weight gain, acanthosis nigricans, and dyslipidemia place her at high risk for type 2 diabetes and metabolic syndrome; she should be screened (e.g., oral glucose tolerance test or HbA1c) and counseled on lifestyle modification, with metformin considered. Adrenal insufficiency and diabetes insipidus are unrelated. Hyperthyroidism is not suggested. PCOS involves estrogen excess/unopposed estrogen rather than deficiency, so estrogen-deficiency osteoporosis is not the concern; endometrial hyperplasia risk is.

pearls: PCOS is diagnosed by Rotterdam criteria (2 of 3: oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries) after excluding thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, and nonclassic CAH.
Markedly elevated testosterone (>150-200 ng/dL) or DHEAS, or rapid virilization, should prompt evaluation for an androgen-secreting tumor.
Combined oral contraceptives are first-line for menstrual regulation and hyperandrogenism when pregnancy is not desired; add spironolactone (with contraception) for refractory hirsutism.
PCOS carries high risk of insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, metabolic syndrome, and endometrial hyperplasia from unopposed estrogen—screen and manage accordingly.

cases.title: Routine pregnancy glucose screen comes back high
cases.chiefComplaint: My doctor said my pregnancy sugar test was abnormal and wants me to come back for more testing.
cases.history: A 31-year-old woman, gravida 2 para 1, at 26 weeks gestation presents after a routine 50-gram glucose challenge test returned a 1-hour value of 168 mg/dL. She feels well and denies polyuria, polydipsia, blurry vision, or recurrent yeast infections. Her first pregnancy 3 years ago was complicated by delivery of a 4.3 kg infant at term with shoulder dystocia. She has gained 11 kg this pregnancy and her pre-pregnancy BMI was 29 kg/m². She takes a prenatal vitamin daily and has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use recreational drugs. Her diet includes frequent sweetened beverages and she exercises infrequently. Family history is notable for type 2 diabetes in her mother and maternal grandmother. She is of South Asian descent. Fetal movement has been normal and she has had no contractions, vaginal bleeding, or fluid leakage. Her blood pressure has been normal throughout this pregnancy and prior anatomy ultrasound was unremarkable.
cases.exam: Well-appearing gravid woman in no distress. Fundal height measures 27 cm, appropriate for gestational age. Fetal heart tones are 148 bpm by Doppler. There is no peripheral edema beyond mild ankle swelling and no facial puffiness. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. Abdomen is gravid, soft, and nontender with no contractions palpated. There is no acanthosis nigricans noted at the neck. Deep tendon reflexes are normal without clonus. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness and urinalysis dipstick shows no protein.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The 1-hour 50-g glucose challenge test was 168 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Diagnose gestational diabetes based on this single value
Perform a 3-hour 100-gram oral glucose tolerance test
Check a hemoglobin A1c and treat if above 6.5%
stages.explanation: Using the two-step approach, an abnormal 1-hour 50-g screen (commonly ≥130-140 mg/dL) is confirmed with a diagnostic 3-hour 100-g OGTT; two or more elevated values confirm gestational diabetes. Starting insulin empirically is premature before diagnosis is confirmed and most patients respond to diet first. A single screening value is not diagnostic—the screen is designed to be sensitive, not specific. A1c is not used to diagnose GDM because pregnancy alters red-cell turnover and A1c lacks sensitivity in this setting. Repeating the screen later delays diagnosis and risks fetal complications during the interval.

stages.label: CONFIRMATORY LABS
stages.content:
3-hour 100-g OGTT (Carpenter-Coustan thresholds):
Fasting glucose 98 mg/dL (threshold 95)
1-hour glucose 192 mg/dL (threshold 180)
2-hour glucose 168 mg/dL (threshold 155)
3-hour glucose 130 mg/dL (threshold 140)
Urinalysis: no protein, no ketones
Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the 3-hour OGTT results, which statement correctly characterizes her diagnosis?
options: Normal test because the 3-hour value was within range
Gestational diabetes, since three of four values meet or exceed thresholds
Pregestational type 2 diabetes that predated conception
Impaired fasting glucose only, not requiring treatment
Test is non-diagnostic and must be repeated
stages.explanation: Two or more values meeting or exceeding Carpenter-Coustan thresholds confirm gestational diabetes; here the fasting, 1-hour, and 2-hour values are all elevated. The normal 3-hour value does not negate the diagnosis. This is not pregestational diabetes—she had a normal early pregnancy and GDM by definition is glucose intolerance first recognized in pregnancy; pregestational diabetes would typically show an elevated early A1c or known prior diabetes. It is not merely impaired fasting glucose; multiple post-load values are abnormal. The test is fully diagnostic and need not be repeated. Documenting these abnormal post-load values establishes the need for treatment and close fetal surveillance.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for her newly diagnosed gestational diabetes?
options: Metformin as the preferred initial agent
Medical nutrition therapy, exercise, and self-monitoring of blood glucose
Caloric restriction to fewer than 1200 kcal per day
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for GDM is medical nutrition therapy with carbohydrate-controlled meals, moderate exercise, and self-monitoring of fasting and postprandial glucose; most patients achieve targets with lifestyle alone. Insulin is added when lifestyle fails to meet targets and is the preferred pharmacologic agent, but it is not first-line before a diet trial. Metformin and glyburide cross the placenta and are considered second-line; insulin is preferred when medication becomes necessary. Severe caloric restriction below 1200 kcal per day risks maternal ketonemia and fetal harm and is therefore inappropriate in pregnancy. Postprandial glucose targets and fasting goals guide whether pharmacologic therapy is ultimately needed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Given her prior macrosomic infant and current diagnosis, which fetal/neonatal complication is she at greatest risk for?
options: Fetal macrosomia with shoulder dystocia and neonatal hypoglycemia
stages.explanation: GDM drives fetal hyperinsulinemia, which acts as a growth factor producing macrosomia (especially shoulder and trunk adiposity) with increased shoulder dystocia risk, and neonatal hypoglycemia after cord clamping removes the maternal glucose supply while fetal insulin remains high. Neural tube defects are linked to pregestational diabetes affecting organogenesis in the first trimester, not GDM diagnosed at 26 weeks. GDM causes macrosomia, not growth restriction. Congenital heart block is associated with maternal anti-Ro/SSA antibodies in lupus, not diabetes. Polyhydramnios, not oligohydramnios, is the classic GDM-associated amniotic fluid abnormality, and neonates are also at risk for hyperbilirubinemia and hypocalcemia after delivery.

pearls: GDM is screened at 24-28 weeks; the two-step method uses a 50-g 1-hour screen followed by a diagnostic 3-hour 100-g OGTT.
Two or more elevated values on the 3-hour OGTT confirm the diagnosis using Carpenter-Coustan thresholds (95/180/155/140).
First-line treatment is medical nutrition therapy and exercise; insulin is the preferred drug when glucose targets are not met.
Screen for type 2 diabetes with a 75-g OGTT at 4-12 weeks postpartum, as GDM markedly raises lifetime diabetes risk.

cases.title: Frothy urine and rising creatinine in a longtime diabetic
cases.chiefComplaint: My urine has been foamy and my last blood test showed my kidney numbers are getting worse.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents for follow-up after labs showed a rising creatinine. He reports frothy urine for several months and mild ankle swelling. He denies hematuria, dysuria, flank pain, or recent NSAID use. His diabetes has been poorly controlled, with most recent A1c values between 9% and 10%, and he frequently misses appointments. He has hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily, glipizide 10 mg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, and aspirin 81 mg daily. He has no drug allergies. He smokes one pack per day for 35 years and drinks alcohol rarely. He works as a truck driver and eats fast food often. Family history includes type 2 diabetes and end-stage kidney disease in his father, who required dialysis. He was told a few years ago he had 'a little protein in the urine' but did not follow up. He notes some numbness in both feet and has not seen an ophthalmologist in 4 years.
cases.exam: Overweight man in no acute distress. Fundoscopic exam shows scattered dot-blot hemorrhages and hard exudates consistent with diabetic retinopathy. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rhythm with an S4 gallop and no murmurs. Lungs are clear. Abdomen is soft and nontender without bruits. There is 1+ pitting edema to the mid-shins bilaterally. Distal lower-extremity exam shows decreased sensation to monofilament and vibration in a stocking distribution with intact dorsalis pedis pulses. There is no rash, no joint swelling, and no costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which test best quantifies his proteinuria and is most appropriate for staging diabetic kidney disease?
options: 24-hour urine collection for creatinine clearance only
stages.explanation: The spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio is the standard, convenient method to quantify albuminuria and, combined with eGFR, stages diabetic kidney disease and guides therapy. A 24-hour collection measuring only creatinine clearance omits albumin quantification and is cumbersome with frequent collection errors. Dipstick protein is insensitive to moderately increased albuminuria (formerly microalbuminuria) and only turns positive at higher levels. Serum protein electrophoresis evaluates for a monoclonal gammopathy, not diabetic nephropathy. Renal ultrasound assesses structure and obstruction but does not quantify proteinuria or establish the diagnosis of diabetic kidney disease. The ACR also serves as a sensitive early marker that guides timing of renoprotective therapy.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Serum creatinine 1.9 mg/dL (baseline 1.2 two years ago)
eGFR 42 mL/min/1.73m²
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio 680 mg/g
Urinalysis: 2+ protein, no blood, no casts, no WBCs
Hemoglobin A1c 9.6%
Potassium 4.6 mEq/L
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
Serum albumin 3.8 g/dL
Lipid panel: LDL 96 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports diabetic nephropathy rather than another glomerular disease in this patient?
options: Presence of red-cell casts on urinalysis
Coexisting diabetic retinopathy with bland urine sediment and gradual albuminuria progression
stages.explanation: Diabetic nephropathy classically features gradually progressive albuminuria, a bland sediment without active casts, and concurrent diabetic retinopathy—microvascular disease tends to track together, so retinopathy strongly supports diabetic glomerulosclerosis. Red-cell casts indicate a proliferative glomerulonephritis, not diabetic nephropathy. A rapid creatinine rise over weeks suggests acute kidney injury or rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis and warrants alternative workup. Hypocomplementemia points to lupus nephritis or post-infectious or membranoproliferative disease. Sudden gross hematuria suggests IgA nephropathy or malignancy. His indolent course, bland sediment, and retinopathy together fit diabetic nephropathy best. When the picture is atypical, a kidney biopsy is considered to exclude a superimposed nondiabetic glomerular disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His ACR is 680 mg/g, eGFR 42, BP 148/90, and A1c 9.6%. Beyond optimizing his ACE inhibitor and glycemic control, which agent has the strongest evidence to slow progression of diabetic kidney disease?
options: Add a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker
Add a sulfonylurea at higher dose
stages.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors reduce albuminuria and slow eGFR decline in diabetic kidney disease and are indicated when eGFR is above roughly 20-25 mL/min, providing cardiorenal protection independent of glucose lowering; this is the highest-yield addition. A dihydropyridine CCB lowers blood pressure but lacks the antiproteinuric, renoprotective benefit of RAAS and SGLT2 blockade. A thiazide helps blood pressure but does not specifically slow DKD progression and loses efficacy at lower GFR. Increasing a sulfonylurea raises hypoglycemia risk, especially with declining renal clearance. NSAIDs are nephrotoxic and worsen proteinuria and GFR. Blood pressure should also be brought to goal, since hypertension accelerates kidney function decline.

stages.label: MEDICATION SAFETY
stages.question: With his eGFR now 42 mL/min/1.73m², which medication adjustment is most appropriate?
options: Stop the lisinopril because of the reduced eGFR
Continue metformin but monitor eGFR; stop it if eGFR falls below 30
Discontinue atorvastatin due to kidney disease
Stop the SGLT2 inhibitor because of proteinuria
stages.explanation: Metformin can be continued with eGFR between 30 and 45 with monitoring and dose review, but should be stopped if eGFR falls below 30 due to lactic acidosis risk. Lisinopril should be continued—RAAS blockade is renoprotective and a modest creatinine rise is expected and acceptable; stopping it removes a key therapy. Statins are continued in CKD for cardiovascular risk reduction and do not require discontinuation. Switching prophylactic aspirin to full anticoagulation has no indication here. SGLT2 inhibitors are continued precisely because they reduce proteinuria and protect the kidney. Potassium should be monitored after RAAS and SGLT2 changes, as hyperkalemia can complicate therapy.

pearls: Diabetic nephropathy is suggested by progressive albuminuria, bland sediment, and coexisting retinopathy after long-standing diabetes.
Quantify albuminuria with a spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio and stage disease with eGFR.
RAAS blockade plus an SGLT2 inhibitor are the cornerstones of slowing progression; nonsteroidal MRAs like finerenone add further benefit.
Stop metformin when eGFR falls below 30 mL/min/1.73m² because of lactic acidosis risk.

cases.title: Burning feet that worsen at night
cases.chiefComplaint: My feet feel numb and burn, especially at night, and it's slowly creeping up my legs.
cases.history: A 63-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 11 years presents with 8 months of bilateral foot numbness and a burning, tingling discomfort that is worse at night and sometimes wakes her from sleep. The symptoms began in the toes and have gradually ascended to the mid-foot, and she has recently noticed similar tingling in her fingertips. She describes a sensation of 'walking on cotton.' She denies acute weakness, back pain, bowel or bladder changes, and there is no asymmetry. Her A1c has run between 8% and 9%. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily and empagliflozin 10 mg daily. She has no allergies. She drinks one glass of wine weekly, does not smoke, and follows a vegetarian diet. Family history includes type 2 diabetes in both parents. She has not had her feet examined in over a year and admits she cannot always feel pebbles in her shoes. She has no history of alcohol abuse, thyroid disease, or chemotherapy, and is up to date on B12-containing supplements.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Lower-extremity inspection shows dry skin and intact, well-perfused feet without ulcers or deformity. Sensory exam reveals symmetric loss of light touch, vibration (decreased to a 128-Hz tuning fork at the great toes), and pinprick in a stocking distribution to the mid-shin, with early diminished sensation at the fingertips. Ankle reflexes are absent bilaterally; knee reflexes are reduced. Motor strength is preserved and there is no focal weakness. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ bilaterally. Romberg is mildly positive. There is no spinal tenderness and gait is cautious but symmetric.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which finding on bedside testing is most characteristic of diabetic peripheral neuropathy and is the best simple screen for loss of protective sensation?
options: Loss of 10-gram monofilament sensation in a stocking distribution
Asymmetric foot drop on dorsiflexion testing
Unilateral loss of pinprick over a single dermatome
stages.explanation: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is a length-dependent, symmetric polyneuropathy; loss of 10-g monofilament sensation in a stocking pattern identifies loss of protective sensation and predicts ulceration risk—it is the recommended screening test. Asymmetric foot drop suggests a mononeuropathy or radiculopathy, not the diffuse distal pattern of DPN. Diabetic neuropathy causes hyporeflexia (absent ankle jerks), not hyperreflexia, which would suggest an upper-motor-neuron process. A positive straight-leg raise indicates lumbar radiculopathy. Loss of pinprick over a single dermatome reflects a focal nerve-root lesion rather than the diffuse, distal, symmetric pattern seen here. A 128-Hz tuning fork and ankle-reflex testing complement the monofilament for a complete bedside screen.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is largely a clinical diagnosis. Which laboratory evaluation is most appropriate to exclude common mimics before attributing her symptoms to diabetes?
options: Lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis
Nerve conduction studies in all patients
Vitamin B12, TSH, and serum protein electrophoresis
stages.explanation: Even with classic diabetes, you should exclude common, treatable mimics: B12 deficiency (especially in a vegetarian), hypothyroidism (TSH), and paraproteinemia (SPEP). These are cheap, high-yield screens. Lumbar puncture is reserved for suspected inflammatory demyelinating neuropathy (e.g., CIDP), not typical DPN. Nerve conduction studies are not needed routinely when the presentation is classic and symmetric; they are used for atypical, asymmetric, or rapidly progressive cases. Lumbar spine MRI evaluates radiculopathy, which does not fit her symmetric distal pattern. Genetic testing is reserved for suspected hereditary neuropathies, typically with foot deformities and earlier onset.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin A1c 8.7%
Vitamin B12 540 pg/mL (normal)
TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal)
Serum protein electrophoresis: no monoclonal spike
Comprehensive metabolic panel: within normal limits
Monofilament: insensate at 6 of 10 plantar sites bilaterally

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her mimics are excluded. Beyond optimizing glycemic control, which is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic therapy for her painful diabetic neuropathy?
options: Oral oxycodone scheduled around the clock
stages.explanation: First-line agents for painful diabetic neuropathy include the SNRI duloxetine, gabapentinoids (pregabalin or gabapentin), and certain tricyclics; duloxetine and pregabalin have specific labeling for diabetic neuropathic pain. Chronic around-the-clock opioids are not first-line due to limited long-term efficacy, tolerance, and dependence risk. Topical hydrocortisone treats inflammation or dermatitis, not neuropathic pain. B12 injections help only if deficiency exists, and her level is normal. Systemic corticosteroids have no role in length-dependent diabetic polyneuropathy and worsen glycemic control. Glycemic optimization slows progression but does not rapidly relieve established pain. Topical agents such as capsaicin and tight glucose control are useful adjuncts for symptom relief.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Because she has lost protective sensation, which counseling point is most important to prevent the most feared complication?
options: Soak feet in hot water nightly to improve circulation
Daily foot inspection, proper footwear, and prompt care of any wound
Begin high-intensity barefoot running for nerve regeneration
Trim calluses aggressively at home with a blade
Apply heating pads directly to numb areas overnight
stages.explanation: Loss of protective sensation predisposes to unnoticed trauma, ulceration, and amputation, so daily foot inspection, well-fitting protective footwear, and prompt attention to any wound or blister are the cornerstone of prevention. Hot-water soaks and heating pads risk burns precisely because she cannot feel temperature, a classic cause of injury in insensate feet. Barefoot running invites puncture wounds and ulcers. Aggressive self-debridement of calluses with a blade can create wounds that become infected; callus care should be done by a professional. Protective behaviors, not heat or trauma, prevent the diabetic foot ulcer cascade.

pearls: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is a length-dependent, symmetric stocking-glove sensory loss with absent ankle reflexes.
The 10-gram monofilament test identifies loss of protective sensation and predicts ulcer risk.
Exclude common mimics—B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, and paraproteinemia—before attributing symptoms solely to diabetes.
First-line agents for painful DPN are duloxetine, pregabalin, gabapentin, or a TCA; opioids are not first-line.

cases.title: A foot sore that won't heal
cases.chiefComplaint: I have a sore on the bottom of my foot that's been open for weeks and now my foot is red and warm.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man with type 2 diabetes for 18 years is admitted for a nonhealing plantar foot ulcer present for 7 weeks. He first noticed a callus over the ball of his right foot, which broke down into an open sore he could not feel because of long-standing numbness. Over the past week the surrounding skin became red, warm, and malodorous, with thick drainage. He denies fever at home but felt fatigued. His diabetes is poorly controlled with an A1c near 10%. He has hypertension, hyperlipidemia, peripheral neuropathy, and known peripheral arterial disease. Medications are insulin glargine 40 units nightly, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril, atorvastatin, and aspirin. He has no drug allergies. He smokes one pack daily for 40 years and drinks occasionally. He lives alone and walks on the foot daily. Family history includes diabetes and amputation in a sibling. He has not seen a podiatrist. He reports no chest pain or shortness of breath. He has had no prior foot surgery and no recent trauma other than the callus.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing but hemodynamically stable. The right plantar forefoot has a 2.5 cm ulcer over the first metatarsal head with surrounding erythema, warmth, induration, and purulent malodorous drainage. A sterile metal probe inserted into the ulcer base contacts a hard, gritty structure consistent with bone. There is surrounding cellulitis extending toward the midfoot but no crepitus or fluctuance. Sensation to monofilament is absent across the forefoot. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are faint and the foot is cool distally with delayed capillary refill. There is no proximal lymphangitic streaking above the ankle and no abscess pointing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A sterile probe in the ulcer contacts bone. What does a positive 'probe-to-bone' test most strongly suggest, and what is the best next imaging step?
options: It suggests cellulitis only; obtain a venous duplex
It confirms gas gangrene; obtain a chest x-ray
It is nonspecific; obtain an echocardiogram
It suggests osteomyelitis; obtain a plain radiograph of the foot
It rules out bone involvement; no imaging is needed
stages.explanation: A positive probe-to-bone test in a diabetic foot ulcer substantially raises the probability of underlying osteomyelitis. The appropriate first imaging is a plain radiograph, which may show cortical erosion, periosteal reaction, or bony destruction and is cheap and fast; MRI follows if x-rays are negative but suspicion remains. Venous duplex evaluates DVT, not bone infection. Echocardiography is irrelevant to a foot ulcer. Gas gangrene would show crepitus and gas on imaging, which he does not have, and a chest x-ray is unhelpful. The test does not rule out bone involvement—it does the opposite, so imaging is essential.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 14.6 ×10³/µL with left shift
ESR 78 mm/hr
CRP 96 mg/L
Hemoglobin A1c 10.2%
Glucose 268 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
Foot radiograph: cortical destruction and periosteal reaction at the first metatarsal head with adjacent soft-tissue swelling
Ankle-brachial index 0.6 on the right

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the probe-to-bone finding, radiographic cortical destruction, and ESR of 78, which is the gold standard to confirm osteomyelitis and identify the causative organism?
options: Bone biopsy with culture and histopathology
stages.explanation: Bone biopsy for culture and histopathology is the diagnostic gold standard for osteomyelitis; it confirms infection and provides reliable organism identification and susceptibilities to target therapy. Superficial swabs reflect surface colonizers and correlate poorly with the true bone pathogen, leading to inappropriate antibiotics. Blood cultures are often negative in chronic diabetic-foot osteomyelitis and rarely identify the bone organism. Repeating a radiograph in 6 weeks delays definitive diagnosis and treatment when destruction is already evident. Procalcitonin is a nonspecific inflammatory marker and cannot confirm osteomyelitis or guide pathogen-directed therapy. MRI is the most sensitive imaging when bone biopsy is deferred and radiographs are equivocal.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond glycemic control, which combination is the cornerstone of treating this diabetic foot osteomyelitis with surrounding infection and an ABI of 0.6?
options: Oral antibiotics alone for 7 days with continued weight-bearing
Surgical debridement, culture-directed antibiotics, offloading, and vascular assessment
Topical antibiotics and daily soaks only
stages.explanation: Diabetic foot osteomyelitis requires source control with surgical debridement or resection of infected bone, culture-directed systemic antibiotics, pressure offloading to allow healing, and vascular evaluation or revascularization given the low ABI indicating arterial insufficiency. A short oral course with continued weight-bearing is inadequate for bone infection and impairs healing. Topical antibiotics and soaks do not penetrate bone or address ischemia. Above-knee amputation is far too aggressive when limb salvage with debridement and revascularization is feasible. Hyperbaric oxygen is at most an adjunct for selected nonhealing wounds, never sole therapy for active osteomyelitis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His ABI is 0.6 and pulses are diminished. Why is vascular assessment and possible revascularization critical to his outcome?
options: Because antibiotics work better with normal blood pressure
Because adequate arterial perfusion is required for antibiotic delivery and wound healing
Because revascularization eliminates the need for any antibiotics
Because it confirms the diagnosis of osteomyelitis
stages.explanation: An ABI of 0.6 indicates significant peripheral arterial disease; without adequate perfusion, systemic antibiotics cannot reach the infected tissue in effective concentrations and wounds cannot heal, dramatically raising amputation risk. Revascularization restores blood flow to support healing and drug delivery. Systemic blood pressure is not the issue—local arterial inflow is. Revascularization does not eliminate the need for antibiotics; both are required. Vascular studies assess perfusion, not the presence of bone infection, which is confirmed by biopsy and imaging. Perfusion has no bearing on neuropathy of the opposite limb. Smoking cessation is also essential, since continued smoking further impairs perfusion and wound healing.

pearls: A positive probe-to-bone test plus elevated ESR/CRP and an abnormal radiograph strongly indicate diabetic foot osteomyelitis.
Bone biopsy with culture is the gold standard for diagnosis and organism-directed therapy; avoid relying on superficial swabs.
Treatment combines surgical debridement, culture-directed antibiotics, mechanical offloading, and vascular assessment.
A low ABI signals peripheral arterial disease that impairs healing and antibiotic delivery—revascularization improves limb salvage.

cases.title: Full after a few bites and wildly swinging sugars
cases.chiefComplaint: I get full after just a few bites, feel bloated and nauseated, and my blood sugars are all over the place.
cases.history: A 41-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 22 years presents with 6 months of progressive early satiety, postprandial bloating, nausea, and intermittent vomiting of undigested food eaten hours earlier. Her glucose readings have become erratic, with unexpected post-meal hypoglycemia followed by later hyperglycemia, making insulin dosing difficult. She has lost 4 kg unintentionally. She denies hematemesis, melena, dysphagia to solids progressing to liquids, or abdominal pain that is relieved by defecation. Her A1c is 8.9% and she has known background retinopathy and peripheral neuropathy. Medications are insulin pump therapy (aspart) and a multivitamin; she recently tried a GLP-1 agonist briefly that worsened symptoms. She has no drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks rarely, and works as a teacher. Family history includes autoimmune thyroid disease in her mother. She has no history of abdominal surgery, scleroderma, or opioid use. Review of systems is negative for fever, jaundice, and change in bowel caliber. She reports feeling bloated even after small meals and sometimes tastes food from earlier in the day.
cases.exam: Thin woman in no acute distress. Abdomen is soft and mildly distended with a succussion splash audible on rocking the patient several hours after a meal. Bowel sounds are present but hypoactive. There is no guarding, rebound, organomegaly, or palpable mass, and no epigastric tenderness to deep palpation. Fundoscopic exam shows background diabetic retinopathy. Lower extremities show stocking-distribution sensory loss with absent ankle reflexes. There is no scleroderma skin tightening, no lymphadenopathy, and no jaundice. Mucous membranes are moist and there are no signs of dehydration.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before attributing her symptoms to a motility disorder, what is the most important initial step?
options: Order an upper endoscopy to exclude mechanical gastric outlet obstruction
Start a prokinetic empirically without further workup
Prescribe long-term opioids for symptom control
stages.explanation: Before diagnosing gastroparesis you must exclude mechanical obstruction; upper endoscopy evaluates for gastric outlet obstruction, ulcer, malignancy, or retained food and is the appropriate first step. Starting a prokinetic empirically risks missing an obstructing lesion. TPN is not first-line and is reserved for refractory malnutrition after diagnosis. Colonoscopy evaluates the lower GI tract and does not address upper-GI symptoms of early satiety and vomiting undigested food. Opioids worsen gastric emptying and are contraindicated, as they can cause or aggravate gastroparesis. Endoscopy first ensures a later delayed-emptying study is interpreted correctly.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY
stages.content:
Upper endoscopy: retained food residue in the stomach despite an overnight fast; no obstructing mass, stricture, or ulcer; pylorus patent
TSH 2.0 mIU/L (normal)
Basic metabolic panel: within normal limits
Glucose at time of testing: 142 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Endoscopy shows retained food without obstruction. Which test is the gold standard to confirm the diagnosis, and what condition must be optimized for the test to be valid?
options: Barium swallow; ensure the patient is fasting only
4-hour solid-phase gastric emptying scintigraphy; ensure glucose is not markedly elevated
Abdominal CT; ensure the bladder is full
Esophageal manometry; ensure the patient is sedated
Hydrogen breath test; ensure recent antibiotic use
stages.explanation: Solid-phase gastric emptying scintigraphy over 4 hours is the gold standard; greater than 10% retention at 4 hours (or more than 60% at 2 hours) confirms delayed emptying. Acute hyperglycemia itself slows gastric emptying, so glucose should be reasonably controlled (commonly below 275 mg/dL) for the study to be valid. A barium swallow assesses the esophagus, not quantitative gastric emptying. Abdominal CT and a full bladder are irrelevant to motility. Esophageal manometry evaluates esophageal motility, not gastric. A hydrogen breath test assesses small-intestinal bacterial overgrowth or carbohydrate malabsorption, not gastric emptying.

stages.label: GASTRIC EMPTYING STUDY
stages.content:
4-hour solid-phase gastric emptying scintigraphy:
Retention at 2 hours 78% (normal <60%)
Retention at 4 hours 32% (normal <10%)
Interpretation: markedly delayed gastric emptying
No mechanical obstruction on prior endoscopy

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her gastroparesis is confirmed. Beyond dietary modification and glycemic optimization, which prokinetic is the only FDA-approved agent for diabetic gastroparesis, and what is its key limitation?
options: Erythromycin; safe for indefinite long-term use
Metoclopramide; limited by risk of tardive dyskinesia and extrapyramidal effects
stages.explanation: Metoclopramide, a dopamine antagonist with prokinetic and antiemetic action, is the only FDA-approved drug for diabetic gastroparesis, but it carries a boxed warning for tardive dyskinesia and extrapyramidal symptoms, so it is used at the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Erythromycin is a motilin agonist that aids emptying but develops tachyphylaxis and is not approved for chronic gastroparesis. Loperamide slows gut motility and would worsen symptoms. Ondansetron treats nausea but is not prokinetic and prolongs (not shortens) the QT interval. Octreotide inhibits motility and is inappropriate. Refractory cases may benefit from gastric electrical stimulation or pyloric-directed therapies after specialist referral.

stages.label: GLYCEMIC IMPACT
stages.question: Why does her gastroparesis cause the pattern of early postprandial hypoglycemia followed by later hyperglycemia in a patient on mealtime insulin?
options: Insulin is destroyed by stomach acid before absorption
Delayed gastric emptying causes a mismatch between rapid-acting insulin action and slow nutrient delivery
Gastroparesis directly stimulates pancreatic glucagon secretion
The stomach absorbs glucose faster than normal
Mealtime insulin is no longer needed in gastroparesis
stages.explanation: In gastroparesis, mealtime rapid-acting insulin peaks before the slowly emptying stomach delivers nutrients to the intestine, producing early hypoglycemia; later, when delayed food finally reaches the gut, glucose rises after the insulin has waned, causing delayed hyperglycemia. This mismatch is managed by timing insulin to actual nutrient delivery, such as dosing after meals or using extended boluses. Insulin is injected, not subjected to stomach acid. Gastroparesis does not directly drive glucagon secretion. The stomach does not absorb glucose—absorption occurs in the small intestine, and emptying is delayed, not accelerated. Mealtime insulin is still required but must be retimed.

stages.label: DIETARY ADVICE
stages.question: Which dietary recommendation is most appropriate to help control her gastroparesis symptoms?
options: Large, high-fat, high-fiber meals three times daily
Small, frequent, low-fat and low-fiber meals, with more liquid-based nutrition as needed
A strict ketogenic diet with no carbohydrates
Bulk-forming fiber supplements before each meal
Carbonated beverages with every meal to aid emptying
stages.explanation: Small, frequent, low-fat, low-fiber meals reduce the gastric workload and ease symptoms; fat and fiber both slow gastric emptying, so limiting them helps, and liquids empty more readily than solids when symptoms are severe. Large, high-fat, high-fiber meals do the opposite and worsen retention. A strict ketogenic diet is unnecessary and very high in fat, which delays emptying further. Bulk-forming fiber can form bezoars and aggravate retention in a poorly emptying stomach. Carbonated beverages add gas and bloating without improving emptying, so they are not recommended for symptom control. Adequate hydration and avoiding alcohol and tobacco, which slow emptying, further support symptom control.

pearls: Suspect diabetic gastroparesis with early satiety, bloating, vomiting of undigested food, and erratic glucose in long-standing diabetes.
Always exclude mechanical obstruction with endoscopy before diagnosing a motility disorder.
4-hour solid-phase gastric emptying scintigraphy is the gold standard; correct hyperglycemia first since it transiently delays emptying.
Metoclopramide is the only FDA-approved prokinetic for diabetic gastroparesis but carries a tardive dyskinesia boxed warning.

cases.title: A lean adult whose pills stopped working
cases.chiefComplaint: I was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes a year ago, but my pills just aren't keeping my sugars down anymore.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes 14 months ago after routine labs showed an A1c of 8.4%. He was started on metformin, later up-titrated, and a sulfonylurea was added, yet his glucose control has steadily worsened with an A1c now 9.8% and recent fasting readings above 250 mg/dL. He has lost 5 kg without trying and reports increasing thirst and nocturia. He is lean, with a BMI of 23 kg/m², and was never overweight. He denies abdominal pain, vomiting, or polyphagia, and there is no acanthosis nigricans. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily and glimepiride 4 mg daily. He has no drug allergies. He does not smoke, drinks socially, and runs three times weekly. Family history is notable for Hashimoto thyroiditis in his sister and vitiligo in his mother; there is no strong family history of type 2 diabetes. He has never had ketoacidosis. He wonders why a 'thin, active person' would have typical type 2 diabetes that is failing so quickly.
cases.exam: Lean, well-appearing man in no distress. There is no acanthosis nigricans at the neck or axillae and no central adiposity. A small patch of depigmented skin (vitiligo) is present on the dorsum of the left hand. Thyroid is normal in size and nontender without nodules. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and without organomegaly. There is no Kussmaul breathing and no acetone odor. Distal sensation is intact and pulses are 2+. Mucous membranes are slightly dry but he is otherwise well-hydrated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A lean 40-year-old with rapidly failing oral agents, weight loss, and a personal and family pattern of autoimmunity makes you doubt classic type 2 diabetes. Which test best clarifies the underlying etiology?
stages.explanation: His phenotype—lean, autoimmune background, rapid secondary failure of oral agents, weight loss—suggests latent autoimmune diabetes in adults (LADA), an adult-onset autoimmune diabetes. GAD-65 autoantibodies are the most sensitive islet autoantibody for LADA and best clarify the autoimmune etiology. Fasting C-peptide reflects residual beta-cell function and is supportive but, alone, cannot distinguish autoimmune from non-autoimmune causes. An OGTT confirms diabetes severity but not etiology. Repeating A1c documents control but does not explain why a lean patient is failing therapy. A lipid panel assesses cardiovascular risk, not the cause of his diabetes.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
GAD-65 antibody: positive (strongly elevated)
IA-2 antibody: positive
Fasting C-peptide: 0.5 ng/mL (low-normal, range 0.8-3.1)
Hemoglobin A1c: 9.8%
TSH: 3.2 mIU/L
BMI: 23 kg/m²
Urine ketones: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: GAD-65 and IA-2 antibodies are positive with a low-normal C-peptide in this lean adult. What is the most accurate diagnosis?
stages.explanation: LADA is an adult-onset, slowly progressive autoimmune diabetes defined by adult age at diagnosis, presence of islet autoantibodies (especially GAD-65), and initial lack of insulin requirement that progresses to insulin dependence faster than in type 2. His positive antibodies, lean body habitus, and declining C-peptide fit LADA. Type 2 diabetes is antibody-negative with insulin resistance; he is lean and antibody-positive. MODY is antibody-negative and monogenic with a strong multigenerational pattern. Fulminant T1DM presents abruptly with DKA, which he lacks. Pancreatitis-related diabetes would have a pancreatic injury history, absent here. Confirming LADA changes both the treatment approach and the need for autoimmune comorbidity screening.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given autoimmune beta-cell failure with a low and declining C-peptide, what is the most appropriate management change?
options: Start a high-dose SGLT2 inhibitor as monotherapy
Stop all medications and trial diet alone
stages.explanation: Because LADA is autoimmune beta-cell destruction, patients progress to insulin dependence; with a low, declining C-peptide and failing oral agents, initiating insulin is appropriate and provides physiologic replacement. Pushing the sulfonylurea harder further stresses already-failing beta cells and is discouraged in LADA. Pioglitazone targets insulin resistance, which is not the primary problem in this lean autoimmune patient. SGLT2 inhibitors carry a heightened risk of euglycemic DKA in insulin-deficient patients and should not be relied on as monotherapy here. Stopping medications for diet alone is unsafe given marked hyperglycemia and beta-cell failure.

stages.label: ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS
stages.question: Because LADA is autoimmune, which associated condition should you screen for given his family history of Hashimoto thyroiditis and vitiligo?
options: Autoimmune thyroid disease (check TSH and thyroid antibodies)
stages.explanation: LADA clusters with other organ-specific autoimmune diseases, most commonly autoimmune thyroid disease such as Hashimoto thyroiditis or Graves disease, and others like celiac disease, pernicious anemia, and Addison disease; given his family history and vitiligo, screening TSH and thyroid peroxidase antibodies is appropriate. Pheochromocytoma and primary hyperaldosteronism are not autoimmune and are unrelated to LADA. MEN2 is a genetic tumor syndrome featuring medullary thyroid cancer and pheochromocytoma, not part of the autoimmune polyglandular spectrum. Acromegaly results from a GH-secreting pituitary adenoma and is unrelated to islet autoimmunity. Celiac serologies and a B12 level are also reasonable given the polyglandular autoimmune association.

stages.label: PITFALL
stages.question: His prior clinician treated him as type 2 diabetes for over a year. Why is misclassifying LADA as type 2 diabetes clinically important?
options: It leads to unnecessary insulin from the start
It delays needed insulin and risks reliance on agents that fail as beta cells are destroyed
It has no clinical consequence since treatment is identical
It prevents the patient from ever needing insulin
stages.explanation: Misclassifying LADA as type 2 matters because clinicians keep escalating oral agents that progressively fail as autoimmune destruction continues, delaying appropriate insulin and risking hyperglycemic complications or eventual ketoacidosis. Recognizing LADA prompts earlier, physiologic insulin replacement and avoidance of beta-cell-stressing sulfonylureas. It does not cause unnecessary insulin 'from the start'—the problem is delayed, not premature, insulin. Treatment is not identical, since LADA trends toward insulin dependence sooner. It does not cause overdiagnosis of hypoglycemia, and it certainly does not prevent the eventual need for insulin—it makes that need inevitable. Early recognition also prompts education about ketoacidosis risk if insulin is ever omitted.

pearls: Suspect LADA in a lean adult with 'type 2' diabetes that fails oral agents quickly, especially with weight loss or autoimmune history.
GAD-65 is the most sensitive islet autoantibody for diagnosing LADA; C-peptide tends to be low and declining.
Patients with LADA progress to insulin dependence faster than typical type 2 diabetes—initiate insulin when beta-cell reserve is low.
Screen for associated autoimmune disorders, especially autoimmune thyroid disease and celiac disease.

cases.title: Mild high sugars running through the family
cases.chiefComplaint: My blood sugar has been mildly high for years and so was my mom's and my grandmother's, but none of us feel sick.
cases.history: A 22-year-old woman is referred for persistently mild fasting hyperglycemia first noted on a college physical at age 18 and stable since. Fasting glucose runs 110-130 mg/dL with an A1c around 6.3-6.6%. She is asymptomatic—no polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, or blurry vision. She is of normal weight with a BMI of 21 kg/m² and is physically active. She has never had ketoacidosis. Medications are none; she takes a daily multivitamin and has no allergies. She does not smoke or use drugs and drinks rarely. Strikingly, her mother, maternal grandmother, and a maternal aunt all carry a label of 'mild diabetes' or 'borderline sugar' diagnosed young, none obese, several managed with diet or low-dose sulfonylureas, and none requiring insulin even decades later. There is no history of obesity or acanthosis in the family. She was told she might be type 1 or type 2 but the picture never quite fit, and a prior clinician started then stopped metformin because it 'didn't seem necessary.' She has no symptoms of thyroid disease.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing young woman of normal weight in no distress. There is no acanthosis nigricans and no central or generalized obesity. Thyroid is normal without nodules. Cardiac, pulmonary, and abdominal exams are unremarkable. There is no hepatomegaly. Distal sensation is intact, pulses are 2+, and there are no skin findings such as eruptive xanthomas. Fundoscopic exam is normal without retinopathy. The remainder of the exam, including neurologic testing, is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A lean young woman with stable, mild hyperglycemia across three generations without obesity makes classic type 1 and type 2 unlikely. What is the best initial step to clarify the diagnosis?
options: Obtain islet autoantibodies (GAD-65, IA-2) and C-peptide
Diagnose type 2 diabetes and begin metformin
stages.explanation: Her phenotype suggests maturity-onset diabetes of the young (MODY), so the first step is to distinguish autoimmune and insulin-deficient causes: checking islet autoantibodies (negative in MODY) and C-peptide (preserved in MODY) frames the diagnosis before genetic testing. Starting insulin empirically is inappropriate for what may be a sulfonylurea-responsive or benign monogenic form. Diagnosing type 2 and starting metformin ignores her lean, multigenerational, non-insulin-resistant pattern. A 72-hour fast evaluates hypoglycemia, not hyperglycemia. A glucagon stimulation test is used for growth hormone or other endocrine evaluation, not to classify diabetes type. Confirming negative antibodies and preserved C-peptide is what makes genetic testing worthwhile next.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
GAD-65 antibody: negative
IA-2 antibody: negative
Fasting C-peptide: 1.8 ng/mL (normal)
Fasting glucose: 122 mg/dL
Hemoglobin A1c: 6.5%
BMI: 21 kg/m²
Lipid panel: within normal limits
TSH: 1.9 mIU/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Antibodies are negative with preserved C-peptide in a lean young woman with an autosomal-dominant, three-generation pattern of mild diabetes. What is the most likely diagnosis and the confirmatory test?
options: Type 1 diabetes; confirm with continued antibody testing
Maturity-onset diabetes of the young; confirm with genetic testing for MODY mutations
Type 2 diabetes; confirm with an insulin resistance index
Latent autoimmune diabetes in adults; confirm with repeat GAD-65
Gestational diabetes; confirm with an OGTT
stages.explanation: MODY is a monogenic, autosomal-dominant diabetes presenting in lean young people with a strong multigenerational history, negative islet autoantibodies, and preserved C-peptide—exactly this picture. Genetic testing for MODY genes such as GCK, HNF1A, and HNF4A confirms the diagnosis and guides management. Type 1 is autoimmune with low C-peptide; her antibodies are negative and C-peptide is preserved. Type 2 features insulin resistance and obesity, which are absent here. LADA is autoimmune and antibody-positive. Gestational diabetes applies only during pregnancy, which is not the context for this referral. Identifying the precise subtype also informs prognosis and screening of at-risk relatives.

stages.label: GENETIC TESTING
stages.content:
MODY genetic panel:
Heterozygous GCK (glucokinase) pathogenic variant identified
HNF1A: no variant
HNF4A: no variant
Interpretation: GCK-MODY (MODY2)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her genetic testing confirms a GCK (glucokinase) variant—GCK-MODY. What is the most appropriate management for this specific subtype outside of pregnancy?
options: Typically no pharmacologic treatment; reassurance and monitoring
stages.explanation: GCK-MODY (MODY2) causes a mild, stable, non-progressive elevation in the glucose set point; it rarely leads to microvascular complications and usually requires no pharmacologic therapy outside pregnancy—reassurance and monitoring suffice. Insulin and high-dose oral agents do not meaningfully lower the genetically reset fasting glucose and add risk without benefit. This contrasts with HNF1A-MODY (MODY3), which is exquisitely sulfonylurea-sensitive; sulfonylureas are the treatment of choice there but are not needed in GCK-MODY. SGLT2 inhibitors are unnecessary and inappropriate for this benign, set-point disorder with preserved long-term prognosis. Correctly diagnosing GCK-MODY also spares the patient lifelong unnecessary medication and its side effects.

stages.label: GENETIC COUNSELING
stages.question: She asks about her future children. Which counseling point is most accurate for this autosomal-dominant condition?
options: There is no risk of passing it to her children
Each child has a 50% chance of inheriting the variant
Only sons can inherit the condition
The condition skips every other generation
Inheritance requires both parents to carry the gene
stages.explanation: MODY follows autosomal-dominant inheritance, so each child of an affected heterozygous parent has a 50% chance of inheriting the pathogenic variant—consistent with the three-generation pattern in her family. It is not true that there is no transmission risk; the dominant pattern means substantial risk. Inheritance is autosomal, not sex-linked, so it is not limited to sons. Autosomal-dominant conditions do not characteristically skip generations, which would suggest reduced penetrance atypical for highly penetrant GCK-MODY. Autosomal-dominant disorders require only one copy of the variant, not two, so both parents need not carry it.

stages.label: PREGNANCY PLANNING
stages.question: If this patient becomes pregnant, what is the key management consideration specific to GCK-MODY in pregnancy?
options: She should always be started on lifelong insulin before conception
Her mild hyperglycemia is irrelevant and needs no monitoring
All of her future pregnancies will require cesarean delivery
Management depends on whether the fetus inherited the GCK variant, which affects fetal growth and whether maternal treatment is beneficial
GCK-MODY guarantees the infant will have severe neonatal diabetes
stages.explanation: In GCK-MODY pregnancy, management hinges on the fetal genotype: an unaffected fetus senses the mother's higher glucose as excessive and over-grows, so maternal glucose lowering with insulin may be warranted to limit macrosomia; an affected fetus shares the same set point and grows normally, so aggressive maternal treatment offers little benefit and risks fetal growth restriction. Lifelong preconception insulin is not automatically indicated. Her hyperglycemia is not irrelevant in pregnancy and does require monitoring. GCK-MODY does not mandate cesarean delivery in every case. It causes mild stable hyperglycemia, not severe neonatal diabetes, which is a different genetic entity.

cases.diagnosis: Maturity-onset diabetes of the young (GCK-MODY / MODY2)
pearls: Suspect MODY in lean young patients with mild, stable hyperglycemia and a strong autosomal-dominant, multigenerational history.
MODY is antibody-negative with preserved C-peptide; genetic testing confirms the subtype and guides therapy.
GCK-MODY (MODY2) is a benign set-point disorder needing no treatment outside pregnancy; HNF1A-MODY (MODY3) is sulfonylurea-sensitive.
MODY is autosomal dominant—each child of an affected parent has a 50% chance of inheriting the variant.

cases.title: Confusion and sweating that vanish after eating
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep getting shaky, sweaty, and confused, and it only gets better after I eat something sugary.
cases.history: A 36-year-old woman is admitted for recurrent episodes of diaphoresis, tremor, palpitations, confusion, and on two occasions near-loss of consciousness, occurring over the past 4 months. Episodes tend to occur in the morning before breakfast or after exercise and resolve promptly with eating. A family member documented a fingerstick glucose of 38 mg/dL during one spell. She has gained 6 kg recently because she snacks frequently to ward off symptoms. She has no diabetes and takes no glucose-lowering medications. She denies access to insulin or sulfonylureas and works as an accountant, not in healthcare. Medications are a daily multivitamin only; she has no allergies. She does not drink heavily, does not use supplements, and has no history of bariatric or gastric surgery. Family history is unremarkable, with no MEN syndromes known. She denies fevers, weight loss, or prior critical illness. Review of systems is otherwise negative. She is frightened that the episodes are becoming more frequent and severe and now avoids driving.
cases.exam: Well-nourished woman, currently asymptomatic and in no distress between episodes. She is alert and fully oriented at present. There is no thyromegaly, no tremor at rest, and no exophthalmos. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. The abdomen is soft and nontender without palpable masses or organomegaly; no abdominal bruit is heard. There are no needle marks or injection-site changes on the abdomen, thighs, or arms. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. There is no hyperpigmentation and no postural hypotension.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her episodes suggest hypoglycemia. Which classic triad must be satisfied to attribute her symptoms to true hypoglycemia and justify a workup?
options: Whipple triad: symptoms of hypoglycemia, low plasma glucose, and relief with glucose
Tremor, weight gain, and heat intolerance
stages.explanation: Whipple triad—symptoms consistent with hypoglycemia, a concurrently low plasma glucose measured during those symptoms, and resolution of symptoms when glucose is raised—must be present to confirm true hypoglycemia and warrant further evaluation. The fever, leukocytosis, and tachycardia triad suggests an infectious process. Polyuria, polydipsia, and weight loss describe hyperglycemia and diabetes. Hypertension, headache, and palpitations evoke pheochromocytoma. Tremor, weight gain, and heat intolerance are an inconsistent mix and do not define hypoglycemia. Documenting Whipple triad anchors the entire subsequent workup, including the supervised fast and paired hormone measurements, and prevents pursuing costly evaluation for spurious low readings.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: To evaluate fasting hypoglycemia, which is the most appropriate diagnostic test?
options: Random glucose in the fed state
Supervised 72-hour fast with paired glucose, insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, and sulfonylurea screen at the time of hypoglycemia
stages.explanation: The supervised 72-hour fast is the gold standard for fasting hypoglycemia; the critical step is drawing paired glucose, insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, beta-hydroxybutyrate, and a sulfonylurea or meglitinide screen at the moment the patient becomes hypoglycemic. This panel differentiates endogenous hyperinsulinism from exogenous insulin and from secretagogue use. A random fed glucose misses the fasting physiology and the diagnostic window. An OGTT provokes reactive postprandial dips and is misleading for fasting hypoglycemia. A1c reflects average glycemia, not acute hypoglycemia. A cortisol test alone evaluates adrenal insufficiency, only one of many causes.

stages.label: 72-HOUR FAST RESULTS
stages.content:
At the time of symptomatic hypoglycemia:
Plasma glucose 39 mg/dL
Insulin 24 µU/mL (inappropriately elevated)
C-peptide 3.6 ng/mL (elevated)
Proinsulin 28 pmol/L (elevated)
Beta-hydroxybutyrate 0.8 mmol/L (low)
Sulfonylurea/meglitinide screen: negative
Symptoms resolved with IV dextrose

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With hypoglycemia accompanied by high insulin, high C-peptide, high proinsulin, low ketones, and a negative sulfonylurea screen, what is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Endogenous hyperinsulinism with elevated insulin, C-peptide, and proinsulin plus suppressed ketones (from insulin's antiketogenic effect) and a negative sulfonylurea screen points to an insulin-secreting tumor—insulinoma. Exogenous insulin would show high insulin but suppressed C-peptide, since injected insulin lacks C-peptide. Sulfonylurea misuse mimics insulinoma biochemically but the drug screen would be positive; here it is negative. Adrenal insufficiency causes hypoglycemia with low insulin and typically other features such as hyponatremia, hypotension, and hyperpigmentation. Non-islet-cell tumor hypoglycemia is mediated by IGF-2 with low insulin, C-peptide, and proinsulin. The suppressed beta-hydroxybutyrate reflects insulin's antiketogenic action and supports a hyperinsulinemic state here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biochemistry confirms insulinoma. What is the definitive management, and which imaging is used for localization?
options: Whipple procedure for all patients regardless of tumor location
Surgical resection (enucleation) after localization with CT/MRI and endoscopic ultrasound
stages.explanation: Most insulinomas are small, benign, solitary pancreatic tumors, and surgical removal—often enucleation—is curative; localization uses contrast CT or MRI and, when needed, endoscopic ultrasound, which is highly sensitive for small lesions. Diazoxide suppresses insulin secretion and is a useful medical bridge or option for nonoperative patients but is not definitive for resectable disease. A Whipple is reserved for tumors in the pancreatic head not amenable to enucleation, not for all patients. Chemotherapy is for metastatic malignant insulinoma, not the typical benign tumor. Octreotide may control symptoms but is not a definitive cure.

stages.label: FURTHER WORKUP
stages.question: Before surgery, why might you screen for hypercalcemia and a pituitary disorder in a patient with a confirmed insulinoma?
options: To rule out coexisting type 2 diabetes
Because insulinoma can be a manifestation of MEN1, which also causes hyperparathyroidism and pituitary tumors
Because insulinoma always metastasizes to the parathyroid glands
stages.explanation: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, including insulinoma, can be part of multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1), which features the '3 Ps': parathyroid hyperplasia causing hypercalcemia, pancreatic or duodenal neuroendocrine tumors, and pituitary adenomas. Screening calcium, PTH, and for pituitary disease identifies a syndromic, often multifocal disease that changes surgical planning and family screening. It is not done to rule out type 2 diabetes. Diazoxide does not cause hypercalcemia. Insulinomas do not metastasize 'to the parathyroid glands' as a rule. Hypoglycemia itself does not directly raise serum calcium; the link is the underlying MEN1 syndrome.

pearls: Confirm true hypoglycemia with Whipple triad before pursuing an expensive workup.
The supervised 72-hour fast with paired glucose, insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, ketones, and a sulfonylurea screen is the key diagnostic test.
Insulinoma shows high insulin AND high C-peptide and proinsulin with suppressed ketones; a negative sulfonylurea screen excludes secretagogue misuse.
Most insulinomas are benign and solitary—surgical enucleation after CT/MRI and endoscopic ultrasound localization is curative; consider MEN1.

cases.title: A nurse with repeated unexplained low sugars
cases.chiefComplaint: I was found confused and sweating again, and my sugar was dangerously low even though I'm not diabetic.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after being found diaphoretic and confused at home; EMS recorded a glucose of 32 mg/dL that improved with dextrose. This is her fourth such presentation in 3 months, and prior workups were 'inconclusive.' She has no diagnosis of diabetes. She works as a hospital nurse with access to medications on her unit. She denies taking any glucose-lowering drugs, but staff note her episodes cluster around her work shifts. She has a history of an anxiety disorder and a prior eating disorder, and there is concern from family about mood and recent stressors. Medications are an SSRI and a multivitamin; she has no allergies. She does not drink heavily, has had no bariatric surgery, and has no family history of MEN syndromes. She is of normal weight with no recent unintentional weight change. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable. She becomes guarded and evasive when asked detailed questions about medication access at work, and prior records note missing insulin vials reported on her unit.
cases.exam: After dextrose, she is alert and oriented, anxious-appearing, in no acute distress. There is no thyromegaly or exophthalmos. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. The abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly or masses. A careful skin survey reveals a few small ecchymoses and possible injection-site marks on the lateral thighs and lower abdomen. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. There is no hyperpigmentation, no postural hypotension, and no goiter. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has documented hypoglycemia without diabetes. Which set of labs drawn during hypoglycemia best discriminates among the causes of hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia?
options: Glucose, insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, beta-hydroxybutyrate, and sulfonylurea/meglitinide screen
Random cortisol in the fed state
stages.explanation: During hypoglycemia, the critical panel is paired glucose, insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, beta-hydroxybutyrate, and a sulfonylurea or meglitinide screen; the relationship between insulin and C-peptide is what distinguishes endogenous hyperinsulinism such as insulinoma, secretagogue misuse, and exogenous insulin. A CBC and cultures evaluate infection, not the hypoglycemia mechanism. Thyroid tests do not differentiate insulin sources. LFTs and ammonia assess hepatic failure, an uncommon and clinically distinct cause. A fed-state random cortisol misses the diagnostic window and only screens for adrenal insufficiency. Drawing the full panel at the time of low glucose is essential.

stages.label: LABS DURING HYPOGLYCEMIA
stages.content:
Plasma glucose 34 mg/dL
Insulin 62 µU/mL (markedly elevated)
C-peptide 0.3 ng/mL (suppressed)
Proinsulin 2 pmol/L (low)
Beta-hydroxybutyrate 0.5 mmol/L (low)
Sulfonylurea/meglitinide screen: negative
Insulin antibodies: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: She has hypoglycemia with HIGH insulin but SUPPRESSED C-peptide and a negative sulfonylurea screen. What does this pattern indicate?
