stages.explanation: Endogenous insulin is secreted with C-peptide in equimolar amounts, so high insulin with high C-peptide indicates endogenous overproduction. Here insulin is high but C-peptide is suppressed—the signature of exogenous injected insulin, which contains no C-peptide and feeds back through hyperinsulinemia to shut off endogenous beta-cell secretion. Insulinoma would instead show elevated C-peptide and proinsulin along with high insulin. A sulfonylurea stimulates endogenous secretion, raising C-peptide and turning the drug screen positive; both findings argue against it here. Reactive hypoglycemia is postprandial and self-limited rather than fasting. Adrenal insufficiency causes hypoglycemia with low, not high, insulin.

stages.label: CLINICAL REASONING
stages.question: Given her occupation, reported missing insulin, evasiveness, and the biochemical pattern, what is the most likely overall diagnosis?
options: Factitious hypoglycemia from surreptitious insulin use
stages.explanation: The combination of high insulin with suppressed C-peptide, a negative sulfonylurea screen, healthcare access to insulin, possible injection marks, and evasive behavior is classic for factitious hypoglycemia from surreptitious insulin administration, a form of factitious disorder. Insulinoma is excluded because endogenous tumor secretion raises C-peptide, which is suppressed here. Type 1 diabetes presents with hyperglycemia or DKA, not recurrent hypoglycemia in a non-diabetic. Non-islet-cell tumor hypoglycemia is IGF-2-mediated with low insulin and C-peptide. Glycogen storage disease typically presents in childhood with hepatomegaly and a different biochemical profile. Reviewing pharmacy and unit medication records can provide objective corroboration of insulin access.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: How should the team best manage this patient once factitious insulin use is identified?
options: Confront her aggressively and discharge immediately
Ensure safety, treat hypoglycemia, secure medication access, and arrange psychiatric evaluation in a nonpunitive, supportive manner
Begin octreotide and refer to oncology
stages.explanation: Factitious hypoglycemia is a psychiatric condition; management centers on patient safety, treating acute hypoglycemia, removing or securing access to insulin, and arranging compassionate psychiatric evaluation, ideally in a nonconfrontational, supportive way to engage the patient in care. Pancreatic surgery is for insulinoma, which is biochemically excluded. Aggressive confrontation and abrupt discharge are unsafe, can precipitate harm, and damage the therapeutic relationship. Diazoxide and octreotide target endogenous insulin oversecretion and oncologic causes—neither applies to exogenous insulin misuse. Recognizing the psychiatric basis and ensuring safety is the priority. Involving risk management and occupational health may also be appropriate given her workplace access.

stages.label: PITFALL
stages.question: Which feature of her case most reliably separates factitious insulin use from a true insulinoma?
options: The absolute glucose value at presentation
The C-peptide level relative to the insulin level
The presence of anxiety and an eating disorder
Whether episodes occur in the morning
stages.explanation: The single most reliable discriminator is the C-peptide level relative to insulin: insulinoma produces endogenous insulin with proportionately high C-peptide, whereas exogenous insulin yields high insulin with suppressed C-peptide and low proinsulin. The absolute glucose value is equally low in both and does not distinguish them. Anxiety and an eating-disorder history raise suspicion but are not diagnostic and could occur with either condition. Timing of episodes overlaps between insulinoma (fasting/morning) and factitious use timed to shifts. BMI does not separate the two. C-peptide is the biochemical key to the diagnosis.

pearls: High insulin with SUPPRESSED C-peptide indicates exogenous insulin, since injected insulin contains no C-peptide.
A negative sulfonylurea/meglitinide screen distinguishes factitious insulin use from secretagogue-induced hypoglycemia.
Healthcare or diabetic-household access plus evasive behavior raises suspicion for factitious disorder.
Management is supportive and psychiatric—ensure safety and secure medication access rather than pursue surgery.

cases.title: Stubborn ulcers and chronic diarrhea
cases.chiefComplaint: My stomach ulcers keep coming back no matter what I take, and I have constant burning pain and diarrhea.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents with 8 months of recurrent epigastric burning pain and chronic watery diarrhea. He has been diagnosed with peptic ulcer disease twice, including ulcers in unusual locations such as the distal duodenum, and ulcers recur despite standard-dose proton pump inhibitor therapy and a documented negative Helicobacter pylori test. He has lost 4 kg and notes the diarrhea improves somewhat when he takes higher PPI doses, suggesting an acid-related process. He denies NSAID use. He has no other significant past medical history. Medications are omeprazole 20 mg daily (recently increased) and an antacid; he has no allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is notable for a parent with kidney stones and a 'pituitary problem,' and a sibling with recurrent ulcers, raising concern for a familial endocrine syndrome. Review of systems includes occasional heartburn and steatorrhea. He has not had prior gastric surgery. He is frustrated that conventional ulcer treatment keeps failing and that the diarrhea persists between meals.
cases.exam: Thin man in no acute distress. Abdominal exam reveals mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation without guarding or rebound; bowel sounds are normoactive and there is no organomegaly or palpable mass. There is no jaundice or scleral icterus. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. There is no thyromegaly. There are no obvious skin lesions. Stool is heme-negative on exam today. No abdominal bruit is heard. There is no peripheral edema and no lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Multiple recurrent ulcers in atypical locations, H. pylori-negative, NSAID-negative, refractory to PPIs, with diarrhea, suggest a hypersecretory state. What is the best initial screening test?
options: Fasting serum gastrin level (off PPI long enough to be valid, if safe)
stages.explanation: The clinical picture suggests Zollinger-Ellison syndrome from a gastrin-secreting tumor; the best initial screen is a fasting serum gastrin level. Because PPIs raise gastrin, interpretation requires accounting for PPI use, ideally measured off PPI when it is safe to do so, and a markedly elevated gastrin supports the diagnosis. Repeating H. pylori testing is low-yield when already negative and does not explain the refractory, multifocal disease. Amylase and lipase evaluate pancreatitis, not acid hypersecretion. Colonoscopy assesses lower-GI pathology, not the upper-GI ulcer and gastrin axis. A plain radiograph adds nothing to diagnosing a gastrinoma.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Fasting serum gastrin: 1,450 pg/mL (normal <100)
Gastric pH: 1.5 (markedly acidic)
Helicobacter pylori: negative
Serum calcium: 11.2 mg/dL (elevated)
Intact PTH: 88 pg/mL (elevated)
Chromogranin A: elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Gastrin is markedly elevated with a low gastric pH. Some patients have only modest gastrin elevations. Which confirmatory test best distinguishes gastrinoma from other causes of hypergastrinemia?
options: Secretin stimulation test showing a paradoxical rise in gastrin
stages.explanation: The secretin stimulation test confirms gastrinoma: in Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, IV secretin paradoxically increases gastrin, whereas secretin normally inhibits gastrin, distinguishing a gastrinoma from other causes of hypergastrinemia such as atrophic gastritis or chronic PPI use. A glucose tolerance test evaluates diabetes, not gastrin. The dexamethasone suppression test assesses cortisol excess in Cushing syndrome. The water-deprivation test evaluates diabetes insipidus. The ACTH stimulation test evaluates adrenal insufficiency. Only the secretin test directly probes the gastrin axis to confirm a gastrin-secreting tumor when fasting gastrin is equivocal. A modestly elevated gastrin with high acid output is exactly when the secretin test is most useful.

stages.label: ASSOCIATED SYNDROME
stages.question: His elevated calcium and PTH, plus family history of kidney stones and a 'pituitary problem,' point to an inherited syndrome. Which syndrome is associated with gastrinoma in this setting?
stages.explanation: Gastrinomas occur in MEN1, characterized by the '3 Ps': parathyroid hyperplasia causing hypercalcemia and elevated PTH, pancreatic or duodenal neuroendocrine tumors including gastrinoma and insulinoma, and anterior pituitary adenomas such as prolactinomas. His hypercalcemia with high PTH plus the family history of kidney stones and a pituitary problem fit MEN1 well. MEN2A features medullary thyroid cancer, pheochromocytoma, and hyperparathyroidism, but not gastrinoma. Von Hippel-Lindau causes hemangioblastomas, renal cell carcinoma, and pheochromocytoma. Familial adenomatous polyposis causes colonic polyps and cancer. McCune-Albright involves fibrous dysplasia, café-au-lait spots, and precocious puberty, none of which explain his gastrinoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While localizing the tumor, what is the cornerstone of medical management to control his acid hypersecretion?
stages.explanation: High-dose proton pump inhibitor therapy is the cornerstone of medical management in Zollinger-Ellison syndrome; it controls the massive acid hypersecretion, heals ulcers, and relieves diarrhea while the tumor is localized and surgical resection is planned. Low-dose antacids are wholly inadequate for the acid output of a gastrinoma. An H1 antihistamine targets allergic histamine receptors, not gastric acid; only H2 blockers reduce acid, and even those are far less effective than high-dose PPIs here. Stopping acid suppression would cause severe ulceration and bleeding. Antibiotics are irrelevant since H. pylori is negative and the cause is a tumor.

stages.label: SEQUENCING
stages.question: He has both Zollinger-Ellison syndrome and primary hyperparathyroidism from MEN1. When both are present, which is generally addressed first and why?
options: The gastrinoma, because acid must always be controlled surgically first
The hyperparathyroidism (parathyroidectomy), because correcting hypercalcemia lowers gastrin and acid output
Neither; both are managed with PPIs alone
The pituitary adenoma, regardless of symptoms
Both simultaneously in a single operation in all cases
stages.explanation: In MEN1 with both gastrinoma and primary hyperparathyroidism, parathyroidectomy is generally pursued first because hypercalcemia stimulates gastrin secretion and acid output; correcting the calcium often reduces gastrin levels and improves control of the acid hypersecretion, sometimes lessening symptoms before gastrinoma-directed therapy. Acid does not have to be controlled surgically first, and high-dose PPIs manage acid in the interim. PPIs alone do not address the hyperparathyroidism or definitively treat the tumor. The pituitary adenoma is treated based on its own symptoms and hormone activity, not reflexively first. A single combined operation is not the standard approach for all patients.

pearls: Suspect gastrinoma with multiple or refractory ulcers in atypical locations, H. pylori-negative, plus diarrhea and high acid output.
Screen with a fasting serum gastrin; the secretin stimulation test confirms a paradoxical gastrin rise in Zollinger-Ellison syndrome.
Gastrinoma can be part of MEN1—look for hypercalcemia and high PTH (parathyroid) and pituitary tumors (the 3 Ps).
High-dose PPIs control acid while localizing the tumor; in MEN1, treating hyperparathyroidism first can lower gastrin.

cases.title: Relentless Watery Diarrhea and Profound Weakness
cases.chiefComplaint: Large-volume watery diarrhea for 4 months with progressive fatigue and muscle weakness.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman is admitted with 4 months of profuse, watery, odorless diarrhea, often exceeding 3 liters per day and persisting even when she fasts overnight. She describes the stool as tea-colored and secretory in character. She has lost 7 kg, feels constantly weak, and has noted intermittent facial flushing. She denies recent travel, sick contacts, antibiotic use, or laxative misuse. Over-the-counter loperamide has provided no relief. Past medical history includes hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg daily. She has no prior abdominal surgery. She drinks alcohol socially and does not smoke. Family history is unremarkable for endocrine tumors. Review of systems is positive for polydipsia, leg cramps, and palpitations. She reports no fever, no blood or mucus in the stool, and no abdominal pain. Allergies: none. On the ward she has required repeated potassium repletion, yet her serum potassium keeps falling. Her primary team is puzzled by the persistence of diarrhea despite bowel rest and the absence of any infectious source on stool studies.
cases.exam: The patient appears fatigued and mildly dehydrated with dry mucous membranes and reduced skin turgor. There is transient flushing of the face during the encounter. Abdomen is soft, nondistended, and nontender with normal, slightly hyperactive bowel sounds; no organomegaly or masses are palpable. Neuromuscular exam shows diminished deep tendon reflexes and proximal muscle weakness. No goiter, no lymphadenopathy, and no skin rash. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmurs. There is no peripheral edema and no clubbing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly suggests a secretory rather than osmotic cause of this patient's diarrhea?
options: Diarrhea that resolves completely with fasting
Large-volume watery diarrhea that persists during fasting
Presence of fecal leukocytes and blood
stages.explanation: Persistence of large-volume watery diarrhea despite fasting is the hallmark of secretory diarrhea, which points toward a hormone-mediated process such as a neuroendocrine tumor. Osmotic diarrhea characteristically resolves with fasting because the unabsorbed osmotic load is removed. Fecal leukocytes and blood indicate an inflammatory or invasive infectious process, not the bland secretory picture described here. Response to loperamide is absent in this case and would not distinguish mechanism; secretory diarrheas from neuroendocrine tumors are notoriously refractory to antimotility agents. The combination of persistence with fasting, hypokalemia, and flushing should prompt evaluation for a secretory hormone-producing tumor.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
Na 141 mEq/L
K 2.7 mEq/L
Cl 112 mEq/L
HCO3 18 mEq/L
BUN 28 mg/dL
Cr 1.3 mg/dL
Glucose 138 mg/dL
Ca 10.8 mg/dL
Mg 1.6 mg/dL
Stool osmotic gap: low (secretory pattern)
Stool studies: no leukocytes, no ova/parasites, C. difficile negative
Venous blood gas: pH 7.30 (non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given secretory diarrhea with hypokalemia, hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, and flushing, which single test best confirms the suspected diagnosis?
options: Fasting serum vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP) level
stages.explanation: The clinical triad of high-volume secretory diarrhea, hypokalemia, and achlorhydria (the WDHA / Verner-Morrison syndrome) is caused by a VIP-secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor, so an elevated fasting serum VIP level (typically >75 pg/mL with massive diarrhea) is confirmatory. Urinary 5-HIAA screens for carcinoid syndrome, which causes flushing and diarrhea but not this degree of hypokalemia or the non-anion-gap acidosis. Serum gastrin evaluates for Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, which classically causes acid hypersecretion and peptic ulcers rather than achlorhydria. Fecal elastase assesses pancreatic exocrine insufficiency, producing steatorrhea, not secretory watery diarrhea. The achlorhydria and hypercalcemia further support a VIPoma.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Serum VIP is markedly elevated. What is the most appropriate next step to localize the tumor?
options: Multiphase contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pancreas
Repeat stool cultures and giardia antigen
stages.explanation: Once a VIPoma is biochemically confirmed, the next step is anatomic localization with multiphase (arterial and venous) contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen focused on the pancreas, where most VIPomas arise (usually in the tail) and where metastases are often already present. Somatostatin receptor imaging (DOTATATE PET) and EUS complement CT but CT is the standard first localization study. Colonoscopy would not reveal a pancreatic tumor and the secretory pattern is not colonic. Exploratory laparotomy is premature without imaging localization and risk stratification. Repeating infectious stool studies is futile given confirmed biochemical diagnosis and already-negative cultures.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.content:
Multiphase CT abdomen: 3.5 cm hypervascular mass in the pancreatic tail
Liver: two arterially enhancing lesions consistent with metastases
Ga-68 DOTATATE PET: intense somatostatin-receptor uptake in pancreatic mass and hepatic lesions
Fasting VIP: 540 pg/mL (markedly elevated)
Chromogranin A: elevated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While planning oncologic therapy, which intervention most directly controls this patient's life-threatening diarrhea and electrolyte losses?
options: A long-acting somatostatin analog (octreotide/lanreotide) plus aggressive fluid and potassium repletion
stages.explanation: A somatostatin analog such as octreotide or lanreotide suppresses VIP secretion and dramatically reduces stool volume, allowing correction of dehydration, hypokalemia, and acidosis; this is the cornerstone of symptom control while definitive surgery or systemic therapy is arranged, alongside vigorous IV fluid and potassium replacement. A proton pump inhibitor addresses acid hypersecretion in gastrinoma, the opposite of the achlorhydria seen here, and does not reduce VIP-driven secretion. Oral vancomycin treats C. difficile, which was negative. Cholestyramine binds bile acids for bile-acid diarrhea and is ineffective against hormone-mediated secretory diarrhea. Definitive management includes surgical resection or debulking and possibly peptide receptor radionuclide therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Because most VIPomas are sporadic but a minority occur within a familial syndrome, which condition should be considered when counseling and screening this patient?
stages.explanation: A pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor such as a VIPoma can arise within MEN1, so it is reasonable to assess for associated primary hyperparathyroidism and pituitary disease and to take a careful family history; identifying MEN1 changes screening and counseling for the patient and relatives. MEN2B features medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, mucosal neuromas, and a marfanoid habitus, not gastroenteropancreatic VIPomas. Von Hippel-Lindau can include pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors but the classic functional pancreatic tumor (VIPoma) clusters with MEN1, making MEN1 the more directly relevant syndrome to evaluate; VHL is not the single best answer. Familial medullary thyroid carcinoma involves only the thyroid C cells and is unrelated to pancreatic VIPomas.

cases.diagnosis: VIPoma (pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor causing Verner-Morrison / WDHA syndrome) with hepatic metastases
pearls: VIPoma causes the WDHA syndrome: Watery Diarrhea, Hypokalemia, and Achlorhydria; secretory diarrhea persists despite fasting.
Expect non-anion-gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis from bicarbonate-rich stool losses, often with hypercalcemia and hyperglycemia.
Confirm with an elevated fasting serum VIP level, then localize with multiphase pancreatic CT and Ga-68 DOTATATE PET; most are in the pancreatic tail and frequently metastatic.
Somatostatin analogs (octreotide/lanreotide) rapidly control the diarrhea and electrolyte derangements before definitive surgical or systemic therapy.

cases.title: Spreading Rash, New Diabetes, and Unintended Weight Loss
cases.chiefComplaint: A migrating, crusting rash for several months along with new high blood sugars and weight loss.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man presents to clinic with a 5-month history of a recurrent, painful rash that begins as red patches in the groin and perineum, blisters, crusts, and heals centrally while spreading outward, leaving hyperpigmentation. Multiple courses of topical antifungals and steroids prescribed elsewhere have failed. Over the same period he has lost about 11 kg without trying, feels persistently fatigued, and was recently told his blood sugar was high. He also reports a sore, beefy red tongue and occasional loose stools. Past medical history is notable only for mild hyperlipidemia. Medications: atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks two beers on weekends and quit smoking a decade ago. Family history includes type 2 diabetes in his mother but no endocrine tumors. Review of systems is positive for angular cheilitis, brittle nails, and a depressed mood; he denies fever, abdominal pain, melena, or jaundice. He has not started any diabetes medication yet. He is frustrated that several clinicians have treated the rash as eczema or candidiasis without improvement, and he wonders whether the rash, weight loss, and new diabetes could be connected.
cases.exam: The patient is thin and appears chronically ill. Skin exam reveals annular and polycyclic erythematous plaques with superficial erosions, central clearing, crusting, and surrounding hyperpigmentation involving the perineum, groin folds, lower abdomen, and perioral region. The tongue is smooth, beefy, and erythematous (glossitis), and there is angular cheilitis. Nails are dystrophic. There is mild temporal and proximal muscle wasting. Abdomen is soft and nontender without hepatomegaly or palpable mass. No lymphadenopathy. No peripheral edema. Mucous membranes are otherwise moist. Neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The characteristic migratory rash with central clearing, glossitis, weight loss, and new diabetes most strongly suggests which underlying process?
options: A paraneoplastic syndrome from a pancreatic islet-cell tumor
Pellagra from isolated dietary niacin deficiency
stages.explanation: The migratory, crusting, centrally clearing rash (necrolytic migratory erythema) combined with glossitis, weight loss, and new-onset diabetes is the classic constellation of glucagonoma, a glucagon-secreting pancreatic islet-cell (neuroendocrine) tumor. Cutaneous T-cell lymphoma causes patches and plaques but not this metabolic triad of diabetes and amino-acid-depletion dermatitis. Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis was effectively excluded by failure of antifungals and does not explain diabetes or marked weight loss. Pellagra shares features with the rash because of similar nutrient depletion, but isolated dietary niacin deficiency would not produce hyperglucagonemia, new diabetes, and a pancreatic mass; the broader syndrome points to glucagonoma.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
Glucose (fasting) 196 mg/dL
HbA1c 8.4%
Hemoglobin 10.1 g/dL (normocytic anemia)
Albumin 3.0 g/dL
Zinc: low
Amino acid panel: generalized hypoaminoacidemia
LFTs: within normal limits
Lipase: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory test would most directly confirm the suspected diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A markedly elevated fasting serum glucagon level (often >500-1000 pg/mL) confirms glucagonoma in this clinical setting; levels are typically far above the threshold that could be explained by stress or fasting alone. VIP is the marker for VIPoma, which causes secretory diarrhea and hypokalemia, not necrolytic migratory erythema with diabetes. Urinary metanephrines screen for pheochromocytoma, which presents with episodic hypertension and adrenergic spells, not this dermatologic-metabolic syndrome. Anti-tissue transglutaminase evaluates celiac disease, which can cause weight loss and dermatitis herpetiformis but not new diabetes with hyperglucagonemia and a pancreatic mass. Hypoaminoacidemia and low zinc support the glucagonoma-related catabolic and dermatitic picture.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Serum glucagon returns markedly elevated. What is the best next step?
options: Start metformin and refer for skin biopsy only
Obtain multiphase contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen/pancreas to localize and stage the tumor
Initiate a niacin and zinc supplementation trial and reassess in 3 months
stages.explanation: With biochemical confirmation, the priority is anatomic localization and staging using multiphase contrast-enhanced CT of the pancreas; glucagonomas are usually large pancreatic tail tumors and are frequently metastatic to the liver at diagnosis. Starting metformin and obtaining only a skin biopsy ignores the need to localize a malignant tumor, although a biopsy would confirm necrolytic migratory erythema. Antifungal therapy has already failed and addresses the wrong diagnosis. Nutrient supplementation alone may transiently improve the rash by correcting amino-acid and zinc depletion but does not treat the tumor and delays definitive staging. Imaging guides resection, debulking, somatostatin analog therapy, and venous thromboembolism prophylaxis planning.

stages.label: IMAGING & STAGING
stages.content:
Multiphase CT abdomen: 4.8 cm hypervascular mass in the pancreatic tail
Liver: multiple hypervascular lesions consistent with metastases
Ga-68 DOTATATE PET: avid uptake in pancreatic mass and hepatic lesions
Fasting glucagon: 1,150 pg/mL (markedly elevated)
Skin biopsy: necrolytic migratory erythema (psoriasiform with superficial necrosis)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond oncologic treatment, which intervention is most important to address a major cause of morbidity and mortality specific to glucagonoma?
options: Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis and a low threshold for evaluating clots
stages.explanation: Glucagonoma carries a strikingly high risk of venous thromboembolism, including deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, which is a leading cause of death; thromboprophylaxis and prompt evaluation of any thrombotic symptoms are essential, particularly perioperatively. A somatostatin analog also controls glucagon secretion and helps the rash and catabolic state. Systemic corticosteroids do not treat the tumor and can worsen the already-present hyperglycemia. High-dose PPI is the treatment for gastrinoma-related acid hypersecretion, not glucagonoma. A ketogenic diet is inappropriate in a catabolic, malnourished patient with hypoaminoacidemia and would not address the tumor or thrombosis risk. Definitive care includes resection/debulking and amino-acid and zinc repletion.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: How should his new hyperglycemia be managed alongside treatment of the tumor?
options: Discontinue all glucose monitoring since the tumor will be resected
Treat the diabetes with standard glucose-lowering therapy while expecting improvement as glucagon excess is controlled by tumor-directed treatment and somatostatin analogs
Start a sulfonylurea exclusively and avoid insulin in all cases
Withhold any diabetes treatment until after surgery regardless of glucose levels
stages.explanation: The diabetes of glucagonoma is driven by glucagon excess, so glycemia should be managed with standard glucose-lowering therapy (often insulin if hyperglycemia is significant) while the underlying cause is treated; controlling glucagon with tumor resection/debulking and somatostatin analogs typically improves or resolves the hyperglycemia. Stopping monitoring is unsafe because symptomatic hyperglycemia persists until the tumor burden and hormone excess are reduced. Relying on a sulfonylurea alone is often inadequate and inappropriately rigid; insulin may be needed, and somatostatin analogs themselves can affect glycemia. Withholding all treatment until after surgery risks hyperglycemic complications in the interim. Glucose should be followed during and after definitive therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Glucagonoma (glucagon-secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor) with necrolytic migratory erythema and hepatic metastases
pearls: Think glucagonoma with the 4 D's: Dermatitis (necrolytic migratory erythema), Diabetes, DVT (thromboembolism), and Depression, plus weight loss and glossitis.
Necrolytic migratory erythema reflects hypoaminoacidemia and zinc/nutrient depletion; confirm the syndrome with a markedly elevated fasting serum glucagon.
Tumors are usually large, in the pancreatic tail, and frequently metastatic to the liver at diagnosis; localize with multiphase CT and DOTATATE PET.
Venous thromboembolism is a major cause of death in glucagonoma, so prioritize prophylaxis; somatostatin analogs control secretion and improve the rash.

cases.title: Kidney Stones, Headaches, and a Worrisome Family History
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent kidney stones and new headaches in a man with multiple relatives who had endocrine problems.
cases.history: A 39-year-old man is referred to endocrinology after his third calcium-containing kidney stone in two years. He also reports several months of dull bifrontal headaches and decreased peripheral vision while driving, plus reduced libido and fatigue. He has long-standing dyspepsia and was found to have recurrent duodenal ulcers despite Helicobacter pylori eradication. Past medical history includes peptic ulcer disease and nephrolithiasis. Medications: omeprazole 40 mg twice daily and as-needed acetaminophen. He has no allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is striking: his mother had a calcium problem and a pituitary tumor, a maternal uncle died of complications from a pancreatic tumor, and a maternal cousin had parathyroid surgery in his thirties. Review of systems is positive for polyuria, constipation, and low mood; he denies palpitations, episodic sweating, flushing, or diarrhea. He has noticed no skin changes. He is anxious about the family pattern and asks whether his various problems could share one cause and whether his children should be screened.
cases.exam: The patient is well appearing. Visual field testing by confrontation suggests a bitemporal field deficit. There is no goiter or thyroid nodule, and no neck masses are palpable. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly or masses; mild epigastric tenderness is present. There is no skin flushing, no lipomas noted today, and no tremor. Reflexes are normal and symmetric. There is no orthostatic change in blood pressure. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam, aside from the visual field finding, is intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Recurrent calcium stones with refractory peptic ulcers and a pituitary lesion in a young man, plus a similar family pattern, should raise suspicion for which unifying diagnosis?
stages.explanation: The clustering of primary hyperparathyroidism (stones, hypercalcemia), a pituitary adenoma (headaches, bitemporal hemianopsia, low libido), and a gastrin-secreting pancreatic/duodenal tumor (refractory ulcers) in a young patient with an autosomal-dominant family history is classic MEN1 (the '3 P's': Parathyroid, Pituitary, Pancreas). Sporadic primary hyperparathyroidism would not explain the pituitary and pancreatic findings or the family pattern. MEN2A features medullary thyroid carcinoma and pheochromocytoma, not pituitary or gastroenteropancreatic tumors. Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia causes benign asymptomatic hypercalcemia with low urinary calcium and rarely stones, and does not produce pituitary or pancreatic tumors.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
Calcium 11.6 mg/dL (corrected)
Albumin 4.2 g/dL
Intact PTH 142 pg/mL (elevated)
Phosphate 2.3 mg/dL (low)
24-hour urine calcium: elevated
Prolactin 38 ng/mL (mildly elevated)
Fasting gastrin 820 pg/mL (elevated)
IGF-1: normal
Morning cortisol/ACTH: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The elevated calcium with inappropriately high PTH and low phosphate confirms primary hyperparathyroidism. Which test best confirms the suspected genetic syndrome?
stages.explanation: Germline testing for a mutation in the MEN1 (menin) tumor-suppressor gene confirms the syndrome and enables cascade screening of at-risk relatives, including this patient's children. RET testing is used for MEN2A/2B, which involve medullary thyroid carcinoma and pheochromocytoma rather than the pituitary-pancreas-parathyroid triad. The calcium-to-creatinine clearance ratio helps distinguish familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia from primary hyperparathyroidism but does not identify MEN1; here urinary calcium is high, arguing against FHH. Plasma free metanephrines screen for pheochromocytoma, a feature of MEN2, and are not part of confirming MEN1. Identifying the menin mutation also guides lifelong biochemical and imaging surveillance.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the elevated gastrin and refractory ulcers, what is the most appropriate next step regarding the suspected gastrinoma?
options: Stop the proton pump inhibitor permanently to reduce gastrin
Confirm with a secretin stimulation test (continuing acid suppression) and localize with cross-sectional and somatostatin-receptor imaging
Begin triple-antibiotic therapy for presumed persistent H. pylori
stages.explanation: An elevated gastrin can be confounded by PPI use, so confirmation typically uses a secretin stimulation test showing a paradoxical rise in gastrin, while acid suppression is maintained to protect against ulcer complications; localization then proceeds with CT/MRI, endoscopic ultrasound, and Ga-68 DOTATATE PET. Stopping the PPI is dangerous because it removes protection against acid hypersecretion and ulcer bleeding/perforation. Total pancreatectomy is premature and excessive before localization, and MEN1 gastrinomas are often multifocal and duodenal. Re-treating H. pylori ignores the biochemical evidence of a gastrinoma and prior successful eradication. MEN1-associated gastrinomas are frequently small, multiple, and located in the duodenum.

stages.label: GENETICS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MEN1 gene: pathogenic germline mutation identified
Secretin stimulation test: paradoxical rise in gastrin (positive)
Pituitary MRI: 9 mm sellar adenoma with mild optic chiasm contact
Multiphase CT/EUS pancreas: 1.2 cm pancreatic head lesion and small duodenal wall nodules
Ga-68 DOTATATE PET: uptake in pancreatic and duodenal lesions

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Among his confirmed MEN1 tumors, which lesion should generally be addressed first, and how?
options: The pituitary microadenoma with immediate transsphenoidal surgery
Primary hyperparathyroidism with subtotal/total parathyroidectomy (with continued acid suppression for the gastrinoma)
The gastrinoma with urgent Whipple resection before any other intervention
Observation of all lesions with annual labs only
stages.explanation: In MEN1, symptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism is usually addressed first with subtotal (3.5-gland) or total parathyroidectomy (often with autotransplantation), because correcting hypercalcemia also reduces gastrin levels and ulcer burden and relieves stone-forming hypercalciuria; the gastrinoma is managed in parallel with high-dose PPI and localization-guided surgery. Immediate transsphenoidal surgery is not first-line for a mildly prolactin-elevated microadenoma, which often responds to a dopamine agonist; surgery is reserved for failure or threatening mass effect. Urgent Whipple is overly aggressive and premature given multifocal MEN1 disease. Observation alone is inappropriate for symptomatic hypercalcemia, nephrolithiasis, and an active gastrinoma.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His mildly prolactin-secreting pituitary microadenoma with only minimal chiasm contact and no visual loss is best managed how?
options: A dopamine agonist (cabergoline or bromocriptine) with periodic MRI and prolactin monitoring
No treatment and no follow-up imaging
stages.explanation: A prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma), even within MEN1, is first treated medically with a dopamine agonist such as cabergoline, which lowers prolactin, restores gonadal function, and frequently shrinks the tumor; serial prolactin levels and MRI monitor response. Immediate transsphenoidal resection is reserved for dopamine-agonist failure or intolerance, or for threatening mass effect/visual loss, neither of which is present with a microadenoma and only minimal chiasm contact. Radiotherapy is a later option for refractory or aggressive tumors, not first-line. No treatment or follow-up is inappropriate because he is symptomatic (low libido, fatigue) and MEN1 lesions require ongoing surveillance for growth. Definitive lifelong MEN1 monitoring continues for all target organs.

cases.diagnosis: Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1) with primary hyperparathyroidism, prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma, and gastrinoma
pearls: MEN1 = the '3 P's': Parathyroid (primary hyperparathyroidism, usually the first and most common manifestation), Pituitary adenoma, and Pancreatic/duodenal neuroendocrine tumors; it is autosomal dominant from a menin mutation.
Refractory or recurrent peptic ulcers in a young person, especially with hypercalcemia, should prompt evaluation for gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison) within MEN1.
PPIs raise gastrin, so confirm gastrinoma with a secretin stimulation test while maintaining acid suppression; MEN1 gastrinomas are often small, multiple, and duodenal.
Treat symptomatic hyperparathyroidism first (subtotal/total parathyroidectomy); confirm the syndrome with MEN1 gene testing and screen at-risk relatives.

cases.title: Neck Mass, Spells of Pounding Heart, and a Strong Family History
cases.chiefComplaint: A firm neck lump plus episodes of palpitations, sweating, and headache.
cases.history: A 33-year-old woman presents with a firm, painless anterior neck lump she noticed two months ago. She also describes intermittent spells of pounding heartbeat, drenching sweats, severe throbbing headache, and a sense of impending doom, each lasting 10-20 minutes, sometimes triggered by exertion. She has had occasional loose stools and facial flushing. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: a combined oral contraceptive only. She has no allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is notable: her father had thyroid cancer in his 40s and had his adrenal glands removed, and a paternal aunt died suddenly during an operation. She has not been screened for any familial condition. Review of systems is positive for episodic anxiety, weight loss despite normal appetite, and intermittent palpitations; she denies hoarseness, dysphagia, hemoptysis, or neck pain. She reports no diarrhea between spells and no kidney stones. She is worried about the family history and asks whether her neck lump and her spells are related, and whether she could pass this on to a future child.
cases.exam: The patient appears anxious and diaphoretic. A firm, nontender, 2 cm nodule is palpable in the right thyroid lobe; it moves with swallowing. No cervical lymphadenopathy is appreciated. There is no proptosis, lid lag, or thyroid bruit. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmurs. There is fine resting tremor of the outstretched hands. Abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly. No mucosal neuromas are seen on the lips or tongue, and the body habitus is not marfanoid. Blood pressure is elevated with mild orthostatic variability.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A thyroid nodule plus paroxysms of headache, palpitations, and diaphoresis with hypertension in a young woman with a striking family history suggests which syndrome?
options: Papillary thyroid carcinoma with anxiety disorder
stages.explanation: The combination of a thyroid nodule (likely medullary thyroid carcinoma) and the classic pheochromocytoma triad of episodic headache, palpitations, and sweating with hypertension, in a young patient whose father had thyroid cancer and adrenalectomy, is highly suggestive of MEN2A. MEN2A links medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and primary hyperparathyroidism. Graves disease causes diffuse hyperthyroidism with a bruit, proptosis, and lid lag, not a solitary firm nodule with adrenergic spells and a familial cancer pattern. MEN1 involves parathyroid, pituitary, and pancreatic tumors, not medullary thyroid carcinoma or pheochromocytoma. Isolated papillary carcinoma with anxiety would not explain the catecholamine-excess spells, hypertension, and family history of adrenalectomy.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
Plasma free metanephrines: markedly elevated
Plasma free normetanephrines: elevated
Serum calcitonin 480 pg/mL (markedly elevated)
Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA): elevated
Calcium 10.2 mg/dL
Intact PTH: upper normal
TSH 1.8 mIU/L (normal)
Thyroid ultrasound: 2.1 cm hypoechoic solid right lobe nodule with microcalcifications

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test best confirms the underlying genetic syndrome and should guide management sequencing?
options: BRAF V600E mutation testing of the thyroid nodule
stages.explanation: Germline RET proto-oncogene mutation testing confirms MEN2A, stratifies medullary thyroid carcinoma aggressiveness by codon, and enables cascade testing and risk-based prophylactic thyroidectomy timing in relatives and future children. MEN1 testing is for the parathyroid-pituitary-pancreas syndrome, which does not include medullary thyroid carcinoma or pheochromocytoma. BRAF V600E is associated with papillary thyroid carcinoma, not the medullary (parafollicular C-cell, calcitonin-producing) carcinoma indicated by the elevated calcitonin and CEA. TSH receptor antibodies diagnose Graves disease, which is not the issue here. RET status also informs whether hyperparathyroidism surveillance is needed.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: RET testing confirms MEN2A. Imaging shows a unilateral adrenal pheochromocytoma. What is the correct sequence of definitive surgical management?
options: Total thyroidectomy first, then resect the pheochromocytoma
Resect the pheochromocytoma first (after alpha- then beta-blockade), then perform total thyroidectomy
Simultaneous thyroidectomy and adrenalectomy without preoperative blockade
Begin chemotherapy for medullary thyroid carcinoma before any surgery
stages.explanation: The pheochromocytoma must be addressed first, with adequate preoperative alpha-blockade (e.g., phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) started before beta-blockade and volume repletion, to prevent a catecholamine crisis during any subsequent surgery or anesthesia; the medullary thyroid carcinoma is resected by total thyroidectomy afterward. Performing thyroidectomy first risks a lethal intraoperative hypertensive crisis from the untreated pheochromocytoma. Simultaneous surgery without blockade is extremely dangerous for the same reason. Chemotherapy first is inappropriate; medullary thyroid carcinoma is primarily surgical, and systemic therapy (e.g., RET inhibitors) is reserved for advanced unresectable disease. Beta-blockade before alpha-blockade can precipitate hypertensive crisis from unopposed alpha stimulation.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During the days of preoperative alpha-blockade before adrenalectomy, which additional measure is essential to reduce the risk of post-resection hypotension?
options: Fluid and salt restriction to control blood pressure
Adding an ACE inhibitor as the primary agent
Starting a beta-blocker before any alpha-blockade
Liberal salt intake and volume expansion (high-sodium diet and/or IV fluids) before surgery
stages.explanation: Catecholamine excess in pheochromocytoma causes chronic vasoconstriction and a contracted intravascular volume; once the tumor is removed and catecholamine levels fall, profound hypotension can occur. Volume expansion with a high-sodium diet and adequate fluids during alpha-blockade repletes the intravascular space and blunts this drop, which is why it is essential preoperatively. Fluid and salt restriction would worsen the volume contraction and the risk of post-resection hypotension. An ACE inhibitor is not the primary agent; nonselective alpha-blockade (e.g., phenoxybenzamine) is standard first. Starting a beta-blocker before alpha-blockade is dangerous because unopposed alpha stimulation can precipitate a hypertensive crisis. Adequate alpha-blockade plus volume loading is the cornerstone of safe preparation.

stages.label: OPERATIVE PLAN & STAGING
stages.content:
Genetics: RET germline mutation consistent with MEN2A
Metaiodobenzylguanidine/DOTATATE imaging: unilateral right adrenal pheochromocytoma, no metastasis
Neck imaging: medullary thyroid carcinoma confined to right lobe, suspicious central node
Preop: alpha-blockade initiated, volume repletion ongoing
Calcium/PTH: normal (no current hyperparathyroidism)

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful treatment, which lifelong surveillance and counseling plan is most appropriate?
options: No further screening once both tumors are resected
Lifelong monitoring of calcitonin/CEA, periodic plasma metanephrines and calcium/PTH, and genetic counseling with cascade testing of relatives
stages.explanation: MEN2A requires lifelong surveillance: serial calcitonin and CEA to detect medullary thyroid carcinoma recurrence, periodic plasma or urinary metanephrines for new or contralateral pheochromocytoma, and intermittent calcium/PTH for primary hyperparathyroidism, plus genetic counseling and cascade RET testing of first-degree relatives (with risk-stratified prophylactic thyroidectomy for affected children). Stopping screening ignores the lifelong, multi-organ, recurrent nature of the syndrome. TSH alone does not track medullary carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, or hyperparathyroidism. Repeating germline RET testing annually is unnecessary because germline status does not change; one definitive test suffices for the patient, while relatives need one-time cascade testing.

cases.diagnosis: Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2A (MEN2A): medullary thyroid carcinoma with pheochromocytoma (RET mutation)
pearls: MEN2A combines Medullary thyroid carcinoma, Pheochromocytoma, and primary hyperParathyroidism; it is autosomal dominant from a RET proto-oncogene mutation.
Medullary thyroid carcinoma arises from parafollicular C cells and secretes calcitonin (and CEA), which serve as tumor markers for diagnosis and surveillance.
Always resect (and adequately block) a pheochromocytoma BEFORE any other surgery; start alpha-blockade before beta-blockade to avoid a hypertensive crisis.
Confirm with germline RET testing to guide prophylactic thyroidectomy timing and cascade screening of relatives.

cases.title: Episodic Facial Flushing, Diarrhea, and Wheezing
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent flushing of the face and neck with diarrhea and intermittent wheezing.
cases.history: A 54-year-old woman is referred for evaluation of 8 months of episodic flushing of the face, neck, and upper chest, often provoked by stress, alcohol, or aged cheeses, and accompanied by warmth and palpitations. She also reports frequent watery diarrhea, crampy abdominal pain, and occasional wheezing without prior asthma. Past medical history includes hypertension. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily. She has no allergies. She drinks wine a few times weekly, which seems to trigger episodes, and does not smoke. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for unintentional weight loss, intermittent dyspnea, and a new sensation of fatigue with exertion; she denies hematochezia, fevers, and night sweats. She has been told she may have rosacea, menopausal hot flashes, or irritable bowel syndrome, but treatments for these have not helped. On further questioning, the flushing is dry rather than sweaty, and she has noticed a fluttering sensation in her chest and mild ankle swelling. She is concerned the episodes are becoming more frequent and longer and wonders whether a single condition could explain the flushing, the diarrhea, and the wheezing together.
cases.exam: The patient is well appearing but flushes visibly during the interview, with erythema of the face and neck that fades over minutes. There is no diaphoresis with the flush. Scattered expiratory wheezes are heard on lung auscultation. Cardiac exam reveals a soft holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that augments with inspiration, and mild jugular venous distention. The abdomen is soft with mild diffuse tenderness and hyperactive bowel sounds; the liver edge is firm and palpable 3 cm below the costal margin. There is trace pedal edema. No goiter and no lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Episodic dry flushing, secretory diarrhea, wheezing, and a right-sided heart murmur in this patient most strongly suggest which diagnosis?
options: Carcinoid syndrome from a metastatic neuroendocrine tumor
stages.explanation: The tetrad of episodic flushing (classically dry, triggered by alcohol/stress), secretory diarrhea, bronchospasm/wheezing, and right-sided valvular heart disease points to carcinoid syndrome, which occurs when a serotonin-secreting neuroendocrine tumor (usually midgut) metastasizes to the liver and bypasses hepatic clearance. A firm enlarged liver supports hepatic metastases. Systemic mastocytosis causes flushing and can mimic carcinoid but typically involves urticaria pigmentosa, pruritus, and tryptase elevation, not 5-HIAA elevation and tricuspid disease. Pheochromocytoma causes flushing with sweating, severe hypertension, and headache, not chronic secretory diarrhea and right-heart valvulopathy. Menopausal flushing is sweaty and does not cause diarrhea, wheezing, or carcinoid heart disease.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
24-hour urinary 5-HIAA: markedly elevated
Chromogranin A: elevated
NT-proBNP: elevated
Niacin/B-vitamin status: low-normal
CBC: mild anemia
Electrolytes: within normal limits
Echocardiogram: thickened, retracted tricuspid leaflets with regurgitation and mild pulmonic stenosis (right-sided carcinoid heart disease)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA is markedly elevated. What is the most appropriate next step to localize the primary tumor and metastases?
options: Empiric trial of antihistamines and reassess
Cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI) plus Ga-68 DOTATATE PET
stages.explanation: After biochemical confirmation with elevated urinary 5-HIAA (and chromogranin A), the next step is anatomic and functional localization using cross-sectional imaging (CT or MRI of the abdomen/pelvis) combined with Ga-68 DOTATATE PET, which is highly sensitive for somatostatin-receptor-expressing neuroendocrine tumors and identifies the primary (often ileal/midgut) and hepatic metastases. An antihistamine trial is appropriate for mastocytosis, not carcinoid, and delays diagnosis. Colonoscopy alone would miss small-bowel and hepatic disease and is insufficient for staging. Bone marrow biopsy is used for hematologic disease such as mastocytosis or lymphoma, not for localizing a midgut carcinoid. DOTATATE imaging also guides eligibility for peptide receptor radionuclide therapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which therapy best controls her flushing and diarrhea while slowing tumor progression?
options: Oral antihistamines and a low-tyramine diet
A long-acting somatostatin analog (octreotide LAR or lanreotide)
stages.explanation: Long-acting somatostatin analogs (octreotide LAR or lanreotide) are first-line: they suppress hormone secretion to control flushing and diarrhea AND provide antiproliferative benefit that slows progression of well-differentiated metastatic neuroendocrine tumors. Antihistamines and dietary changes do not control serotonin-mediated symptoms or tumor growth. Glucocorticoids are not a primary therapy for carcinoid symptom control and carry metabolic harms. Loperamide alone may modestly help diarrhea but does not control flushing, treat the tumor, or address the underlying hormone excess. Additional measures include niacin supplementation for pellagra risk, and short-acting octreotide perioperatively to prevent carcinoid crisis.

stages.label: STAGING & WORKUP
stages.content:
Multiphase CT abdomen: 2.6 cm ileal mass with mesenteric desmoplastic reaction
Liver: multiple bilobar hypervascular metastases
Ga-68 DOTATATE PET: avid uptake in ileal primary and hepatic lesions
Echocardiogram (confirmed): moderate-severe tricuspid regurgitation
Fasting 5-HIAA and chromogranin A: persistently elevated

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before she undergoes surgical debulking, which precaution is essential to prevent a life-threatening intraoperative carcinoid crisis?
options: Administer intravenous octreotide before and during the procedure
Withhold all antihypertensives the morning of surgery
Pretreat with epinephrine to maintain blood pressure
stages.explanation: Carcinoid crisis (profound flushing, bronchospasm, and labile, often refractory hypotension or hypertension) can be precipitated by anesthesia, tumor manipulation, or stress; intravenous octreotide given before and continued during the procedure is the key prophylaxis and treatment. Beta-blockade alone does not prevent the mediator surge and can worsen bronchospasm. Withholding all antihypertensives does not address the catecholamine-independent, serotonin/kallikrein-mediated crisis. Epinephrine can paradoxically worsen a carcinoid crisis by stimulating further mediator release and is contraindicated as first-line; octreotide and supportive measures are preferred. Avoid catecholamine pressors and treat hypotension with fluids and octreotide.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Chronic serotonin overproduction diverts dietary tryptophan, putting this patient at risk for a deficiency state. Which supplementation is appropriate to prevent it?
stages.explanation: In carcinoid syndrome, tumor cells shunt large amounts of tryptophan into serotonin synthesis, leaving less tryptophan available for niacin (vitamin B3) production; this can cause pellagra (dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia). Supplementing niacin prevents this deficiency state and is a recognized part of management. Vitamin B12 deficiency relates to ileal resection or pernicious anemia, not tryptophan diversion, although extensive ileal disease/resection can separately warrant B12 monitoring. Vitamin C deficiency (scurvy) is unrelated to serotonin metabolism. Vitamin D is not depleted by tryptophan shunting. The tryptophan-to-niacin link makes niacin supplementation the correct preventive measure here, alongside somatostatin-analog therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Carcinoid syndrome from a metastatic midgut (ileal) neuroendocrine tumor with carcinoid heart disease
pearls: Carcinoid syndrome appears only after a serotonin-secreting (usually midgut) tumor metastasizes to the liver, escaping first-pass hepatic clearance; symptoms are flushing, secretory diarrhea, and bronchospasm.
Right-sided valvular disease (tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonic stenosis) is the classic carcinoid heart disease because the lungs metabolize serotonin before it reaches the left heart.
Confirm with elevated 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA (avoid serotonin-rich foods beforehand) and chromogranin A; localize with CT/MRI and Ga-68 DOTATATE PET.
Somatostatin analogs control symptoms and slow tumor growth; give IV octreotide perioperatively to prevent carcinoid crisis, and avoid epinephrine as a pressor.

cases.title: A Hip Fracture After a Minor Fall
cases.chiefComplaint: Hip and groin pain after a low-energy fall, now referred to clinic for bone health.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman is seen in clinic after sustaining a left hip fracture from a fall while standing at her kitchen sink three weeks ago; she underwent surgical fixation and is now ambulating with a walker. She reports she simply lost her balance and fell from standing height. She went through menopause at age 49 and never used hormone therapy. She is sedentary, has a slight build, and has smoked half a pack of cigarettes daily for 30 years. Her mother fractured her hip in her seventies. Past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily and a daily multivitamin; she takes no calcium or vitamin D supplement. She has no known drug allergies. She drinks one to two glasses of wine most evenings. She has lost about 3 cm of height over the past decade. Review of systems is negative for bone pain at rest, prior fractures, kidney stones, thyroid symptoms, or steroid use. She is worried about breaking another bone and asks what testing and treatment she needs to protect herself.
cases.exam: The patient is a thin, well-appearing older woman. There is a healing surgical incision over the left hip with no erythema or drainage, and she ambulates cautiously with a walker. There is mild thoracic kyphosis. There is no spinal point tenderness and no obvious deformity of the long bones. Proximal muscle strength is grossly intact aside from guarding around the operative hip. No proximal muscle weakness on formal testing, no waddling gait. There is no thyromegaly and no peripheral edema. The remainder of the musculoskeletal and neurologic exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A hip fracture from a fall from standing height in a postmenopausal woman is best classified as which type of fracture, with what diagnostic implication?
options: A high-energy traumatic fracture requiring no metabolic workup
A fragility (low-trauma) fracture that itself suggests osteoporosis
A pathologic fracture mandating immediate bone biopsy
stages.explanation: A fracture occurring from a fall from standing height or less is a fragility (low-trauma) fracture. In a postmenopausal woman, a hip or vertebral fragility fracture is itself diagnostic of osteoporosis clinically, independent of the DEXA T-score, and warrants treatment and a workup for secondary causes. A high-energy mechanism is absent here; a fall from standing is low-energy. A pathologic fracture implies an underlying focal lesion such as malignancy; biopsy is not indicated by this presentation alone without suspicious imaging. A stress fracture results from repetitive loading in active individuals, not a single low-energy fall. This event places her at high risk for future fractures.

stages.label: LABS & DEXA
stages.content:
DEXA femoral neck T-score: -2.9
DEXA lumbar spine T-score: -2.6
Calcium 9.3 mg/dL
Phosphate 3.4 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 78 U/L (normal)
25-OH vitamin D 22 ng/mL
Intact PTH: normal
TSH 1.9 mIU/L
Serum protein electrophoresis: normal
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How do the DEXA results refine the diagnosis?
options: Normal bone density; the fracture was purely accidental
Osteopenia only, not meeting criteria for osteoporosis
Osteoporosis, confirmed by a T-score of -2.5 or lower (and by the fragility fracture)
stages.explanation: A T-score of -2.5 or lower at the femoral neck or spine defines osteoporosis by densitometry; her femoral neck T-score of -2.9 meets this threshold, and a fragility hip fracture independently confirms the diagnosis. A normal density is incorrect given the T-scores. Osteopenia is defined as a T-score between -1.0 and -2.5; her values are below that range, and the fragility fracture alone already establishes osteoporosis. Osteomalacia is a qualitative defect in bone mineralization diagnosed by low vitamin D with high alkaline phosphatase and characteristic findings, not by T-score; her alkaline phosphatase and calcium are normal. Her vitamin D is insufficient and should be repleted alongside therapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which pharmacologic therapy is first-line to reduce her future fracture risk?
options: An oral bisphosphonate (e.g., alendronate) with calcium and vitamin D repletion
Calcium and vitamin D supplements alone
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for postmenopausal osteoporosis, especially after a hip fracture, is an antiresorptive bisphosphonate such as alendronate (or IV zoledronic acid if oral is not tolerated), combined with adequate calcium intake and vitamin D repletion to optimize response. Calcium and vitamin D alone are necessary adjuncts but insufficient to reduce fracture risk in established osteoporosis. Estrogen reduces fracture risk but is not first-line in a 68-year-old because of cardiovascular and thromboembolic risks and is reserved mainly for younger postmenopausal women with vasomotor symptoms. Calcitonin is weak and not first-line. Anabolic agents (e.g., teriparatide, romososumab) are options for very high-risk patients.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which additional measure is most appropriate to reduce her risk of another fracture?
options: Strict bed rest to avoid further falls
Smoking cessation, limiting alcohol, weight-bearing exercise, and fall-prevention/home-safety measures
stages.explanation: Lifestyle and fall-prevention measures are essential: smoking cessation and limiting alcohol improve bone health, weight-bearing and resistance exercise maintain bone and muscle, and home-safety modifications (removing trip hazards, good lighting, assistive devices, vision and medication review) reduce falls, the proximate cause of most fractures. Bed rest accelerates bone loss and deconditioning and increases fall and thrombosis risk. A routine annual whole-body bone scan is not standard monitoring; DEXA is repeated at intervals to assess therapy. Long-term glucocorticoids cause bone loss and would worsen osteoporosis, the opposite of the goal. She should also have DEXA monitoring on therapy and ensure adherence.

cases.diagnosis: Postmenopausal osteoporosis (confirmed by fragility hip fracture and femoral neck T-score of -2.9)
pearls: A fragility fracture (from a fall from standing height) of the hip or spine is clinically diagnostic of osteoporosis regardless of the DEXA T-score.
By densitometry, osteoporosis is a T-score of -2.5 or lower; osteopenia is -1.0 to -2.5; in osteoporosis calcium, phosphate, and alkaline phosphatase are typically normal.
First-line pharmacotherapy is a bisphosphonate plus calcium and vitamin D repletion; reserve anabolic agents (teriparatide, romosozumab) for very high-risk patients.
Risk reduction also requires smoking cessation, alcohol moderation, weight-bearing exercise, and fall-prevention/home-safety measures.

cases.title: Aching Bones and Difficulty Climbing Stairs
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of diffuse bone aches and worsening weakness getting up from chairs and climbing stairs.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman presents with about 6 months of diffuse, dull bone pain in her hips, lower back, ribs, and thighs, along with progressive difficulty rising from chairs, climbing stairs, and lifting objects overhead. The pain is worse with weight bearing and tender to pressure over the bones. She emigrated from a northern climate, wears concealing clothing outdoors, and rarely consumes dairy. She has had gastric bypass surgery for obesity 8 years ago and does not take her recommended supplements consistently. Past medical history includes obesity (post-bypass) and depression. Medications: sertraline 50 mg daily; she takes a multivitamin sporadically and no dedicated vitamin D or calcium. She has no known allergies. She does not smoke and rarely drinks alcohol. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for fatigue, muscle cramps, and occasional perioral tingling; she denies fevers, weight loss, prior fractures, or kidney stones. She reports the weakness is symmetric and most noticeable in her hips and shoulders. She has tried acetaminophen with little relief and is frustrated that her labored, waddling walk has made her job on her feet difficult.
cases.exam: The patient walks with a waddling, antalgic gait. There is diffuse tenderness to palpation over the pelvis, lower ribs, and proximal long bones. Proximal muscle weakness is evident, with difficulty rising from a squat and a positive Gowers-like maneuver; she struggles to abduct the shoulders against resistance. Chvostek sign is mildly positive. There is no joint swelling, erythema, or deformity. Deep tendon reflexes are present and symmetric. There is no spinal step-off. Sensation is intact. No thyromegaly, no rashes, and no peripheral edema are noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Diffuse bone pain with bony tenderness and symmetric proximal muscle weakness in a malabsorptive patient with limited sun exposure most suggests which process?
options: Defective bone mineralization from vitamin D deficiency (osteomalacia)
stages.explanation: Diffuse bone pain with diffuse bony tenderness plus symmetric proximal myopathy in someone with malabsorption (gastric bypass), limited sun exposure, and poor intake strongly suggests osteomalacia from vitamin D deficiency, in which inadequate mineralization of osteoid produces painful, weak bones and a proximal myopathy. Polymyalgia rheumatica causes proximal shoulder/hip girdle pain and stiffness with elevated inflammatory markers, typically in patients over 50, but not bony tenderness or the metabolic picture here. Metastatic bone disease usually causes focal pain and lytic/blastic lesions, not symmetric proximal weakness with diffuse tenderness in this risk context. Muscular dystrophy is a primary muscle disorder and would not cause bone pain, bony tenderness, or the relevant metabolic abnormalities.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
25-OH vitamin D 8 ng/mL (markedly low)
Calcium 8.2 mg/dL (low-normal/low)
Phosphate 2.2 mg/dL (low)
Alkaline phosphatase 280 U/L (elevated)
Intact PTH 142 pg/mL (elevated, secondary hyperparathyroidism)
Magnesium: low-normal
X-ray: pseudofractures (Looser zones) at the medial femoral necks and pubic rami
Renal function: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory pattern, together with Looser zones, best confirms vitamin D-deficiency osteomalacia?
options: High calcium, low PTH, low alkaline phosphatase
Low/low-normal calcium and phosphate, low 25-OH vitamin D, high alkaline phosphatase, and elevated PTH
Normal calcium, normal vitamin D, isolated high alkaline phosphatase
High phosphate with low alkaline phosphatase
stages.explanation: Classic vitamin D-deficiency osteomalacia shows low or low-normal calcium and low phosphate, a low 25-OH vitamin D, a high alkaline phosphatase reflecting increased osteoblastic activity, and secondary hyperparathyroidism (elevated PTH) driven by hypocalcemia; Looser zones (pseudofractures) are the radiographic hallmark. That option (high calcium, low PTH, low ALP) describes hypercalcemia of non-PTH origin, the opposite pattern. Another option (normal calcium and vitamin D with isolated high ALP) is more typical of Paget disease of bone, which has normal calcium and vitamin D. A third option (high phosphate, low ALP) does not fit osteomalacia, where ALP is elevated and phosphate is typically low. The constellation here confirms osteomalacia with secondary hyperparathyroidism.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for her condition?
options: Repletion with vitamin D (often high-dose) plus calcium, then correcting the underlying malabsorption/intake
stages.explanation: The cornerstone is vitamin D repletion, often with high-dose ergocalciferol or cholecalciferol, together with calcium supplementation, followed by maintenance dosing and correction of the underlying cause (here, malabsorption from gastric bypass and low intake/sun exposure); bone pain, weakness, and biochemistry improve as mineralization is restored. A bisphosphonate alone is inappropriate and even harmful before correcting vitamin D and calcium, because antiresorptives in untreated deficiency can precipitate hypocalcemia. Glucocorticoids impair calcium absorption and bone formation and would worsen the disease. Calcitonin does not treat the mineralization defect. Monitor calcium, phosphate, PTH, alkaline phosphatase, and 25-OH vitamin D to confirm response and avoid hypercalcemia.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Why is it especially important to check and correct magnesium and to monitor calcium when starting therapy in this patient?
options: Hypomagnesemia impairs PTH secretion/action and can perpetuate hypocalcemia, and 'hungry bone' remineralization can drop calcium further
Magnesium has no role in calcium metabolism
stages.explanation: Magnesium is required for normal PTH secretion and action, so untreated hypomagnesemia can blunt the parathyroid response and perpetuate hypocalcemia and refractory symptoms; correcting magnesium is therefore important. As mineralization resumes during repletion, rapid uptake of calcium and phosphate into remineralizing bone ('hungry bone'-type effect) can transiently lower serum calcium and phosphate, so calcium must be monitored and supplemented. That option is false: magnesium is integral to calcium homeostasis. Another option is incorrect; magnesium repletion supports, not causes, mineralization. A third option is wrong because calcium frequently shifts during treatment and requires monitoring. Careful biochemical follow-up prevents symptomatic hypocalcemia and ensures adequate repletion.

cases.diagnosis: Osteomalacia due to vitamin D deficiency (with secondary hyperparathyroidism), in the setting of malabsorption
pearls: Osteomalacia from vitamin D deficiency presents with diffuse bone pain, bony tenderness, and symmetric proximal myopathy (waddling gait); Looser zones/pseudofractures are the radiographic clue.
Typical labs: low/low-normal calcium and phosphate, low 25-OH vitamin D, HIGH alkaline phosphatase, and elevated PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism).
Contrast with Paget disease (normal calcium, normal vitamin D, isolated high ALP) and osteoporosis (normal calcium/phosphate/ALP).
Treat with vitamin D and calcium repletion and correct the underlying cause; check magnesium and monitor calcium to avoid refractory or worsening hypocalcemia.

cases.title: An Unexpected Lab Result and a Hat That No Longer Fits
cases.chiefComplaint: Incidentally elevated alkaline phosphatase, plus localized bone pain and a head that seems to be getting larger.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man is referred after routine bloodwork showed an isolated elevation of alkaline phosphatase. On questioning, he reports a deep, aching pain in his right thigh and lower back that is present even at rest and at night, and he has noticed his hat and eyeglasses feel tight, suggesting his head has enlarged. He also reports new hearing difficulty in his right ear. Past medical history includes osteoarthritis and benign prostatic hyperplasia. Medications: tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily and as-needed acetaminophen for joint pain. He has no allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is notable for a brother with a bone disorder requiring treatment. Review of systems is positive for the bone pain, head enlargement, and unilateral hearing loss; he denies weight loss, fevers, prior fractures, or kidney stones, and reports normal appetite. He has noticed slight bowing of his right shin and that the skin over that area feels warm. He is otherwise active. He is concerned about whether the abnormal blood test and his symptoms reflect a serious condition such as cancer.
cases.exam: The patient is well appearing. The skull appears enlarged with frontal bossing. There is anterolateral bowing of the right tibia, and the overlying skin is warm to palpation, with tenderness over the right tibia and lumbar spine. Gait is mildly antalgic. Whispered-voice testing suggests right-sided hearing loss; otoscopy is normal. There is no spinal deformity beyond mild lumbar tenderness, no focal neurologic deficit, and no proximal muscle weakness. No lymphadenopathy and no abdominal masses are palpable. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams are normal. No skin lesions or rashes are noted aside from the warmth over the tibia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An isolated high alkaline phosphatase with localized bone pain, skull enlargement, tibial bowing, and unilateral hearing loss in an older adult most suggests which diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Paget disease of bone classically presents in older adults with an isolated elevated alkaline phosphatase, localized bone pain, bony deformities (skull enlargement/frontal bossing, increasing hat size, bowing of long bones such as the tibia), warmth over affected bone from hypervascularity, and hearing loss when the skull base is involved. Osteoporosis is typically asymptomatic until fracture and does not raise alkaline phosphatase or enlarge bones. Osteomalacia causes diffuse, not focal, pain with low vitamin D and abnormal calcium/phosphate, not isolated ALP elevation with normal mineral studies. Multiple myeloma causes lytic lesions, anemia, hypercalcemia, and renal failure, with a monoclonal protein, and usually a normal or low alkaline phosphatase.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Alkaline phosphatase 540 U/L (markedly elevated)
Calcium 9.6 mg/dL (normal)
Phosphate 3.5 mg/dL (normal)
25-OH vitamin D 34 ng/mL (sufficient)
Intact PTH: normal
GGT: normal (confirms bony, not hepatic, ALP source)
Urinary/serum bone turnover markers: elevated
X-ray right tibia: cortical thickening, coarse trabeculae, and bowing
Skull X-ray: 'cotton-wool' patchy sclerosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding best confirms the diagnosis and helps assess disease extent?
options: A bone marrow biopsy showing plasma cells
Characteristic radiographs plus a radionuclide bone scan to map active lesions, with isolated high ALP and normal calcium/vitamin D
Low 25-OH vitamin D with high PTH
Serum protein electrophoresis showing an M-spike
stages.explanation: Diagnosis rests on characteristic radiographs (cortical thickening, coarse trabeculae, bowing, 'cotton-wool' skull) in the setting of an isolated elevated alkaline phosphatase with normal calcium, phosphate, and vitamin D; a radionuclide bone scan maps the number and activity of pagetic lesions to guide treatment and follow-up. A bone marrow biopsy with plasma cells and an M-spike point to multiple myeloma, which is not consistent with isolated ALP elevation and these radiographs. Low vitamin D with high PTH indicates osteomalacia/secondary hyperparathyroidism, not Paget disease, in which vitamin D and PTH are normal. The normal calcium and vitamin D distinguish Paget from osteomalacia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has bone pain and skull involvement with hearing loss. What is the first-line treatment?
options: A potent nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate (e.g., IV zoledronic acid)
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for symptomatic Paget disease (bone pain, deformity, neurologic involvement such as hearing loss, or planned surgery on pagetic bone) is a potent nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate, with a single IV infusion of zoledronic acid being highly effective at suppressing the accelerated bone turnover and normalizing alkaline phosphatase, often for years. High-dose vitamin D alone treats osteomalacia, not Paget disease, where vitamin D is already sufficient. Glucocorticoids have no role and harm bone. Parathyroidectomy treats primary hyperparathyroidism, which is not the diagnosis here (PTH and calcium are normal). Follow alkaline phosphatase to assess response, and ensure adequate calcium and vitamin D before bisphosphonate dosing.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is the most feared, though rare, in long-standing Paget disease?
options: Osteosarcoma (malignant degeneration), heralded by sudden worsening pain, swelling, or a soft-tissue mass
stages.explanation: The most feared complication of Paget disease is malignant degeneration to osteosarcoma; though rare, it should be suspected when there is a sudden increase in pain, new swelling, a palpable soft-tissue mass, or a pathologic fracture in a previously stable pagetic bone, and it carries a poor prognosis. Conversion to osteoporosis is not how Paget disease behaves; the problem is disorganized overactive remodeling, not low bone mass. Hyperthyroidism is unrelated to Paget disease. Spontaneous resolution does not occur; the disease is chronic. Other complications include high-output cardiac failure from hypervascular bone, nerve compression and deafness, deformity, fractures, and secondary osteoarthritis.

cases.diagnosis: Paget disease of bone (polyostotic, with skull and tibial involvement)
pearls: Suspect Paget disease in an older adult with an ISOLATED elevated alkaline phosphatase, localized bone pain, bony deformity (enlarging skull/hat size, long-bone bowing), warmth over bone, and hearing loss.
Calcium, phosphate, and vitamin D are normal in Paget disease, distinguishing it from osteomalacia; confirm GGT is normal to verify the ALP is of bony origin.
Diagnose with characteristic radiographs ('cotton-wool' skull, cortical thickening) and stage with a radionuclide bone scan; alkaline phosphatase tracks disease activity.
Treat symptomatic disease with a potent IV bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid); the rare but devastating complication is osteosarcoma, signaled by sudden new pain, swelling, or a mass.

cases.title: A Toddler with Bowed Legs and Poor Growth
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive bowing of the legs and falling off the growth curve.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his parents for worsening bowing of both legs and short stature; he began walking at 14 months and his legs have curved outward since. His height has dropped from the 25th to below the 3rd percentile, while his head and weight are more preserved. He has dental abscesses and delayed dentition. He eats a varied diet, drinks fortified milk, and spends ample time outdoors, and he takes a standard infant/toddler multivitamin with vitamin D. There is no history of fractures from trivial trauma typical of brittle bone disease. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; he was born at term. Medications: a daily children's multivitamin. He has no allergies. Family history is notable: his mother is short with bowed legs and had dental problems and many relatives on her side have short stature and bowing in a pattern affecting both sexes but seemingly more in males. Review of systems is positive for waddling gait and leg pain after activity; he denies polyuria, diarrhea, and weight loss. The parents are confused because they were told his vitamin D intake is adequate, yet his legs keep bowing, and they want to know the cause.
cases.exam: The child is small for age with short lower limbs and symmetric genu varum (bowing) of both legs; the deformity is more pronounced at the knees. There is mild widening of the wrists and ankles (metaphyseal flaring) and a slightly prominent forehead. Gait is waddling. Dental exam shows poor enamel and a healing dental abscess.
