It is antibody (TRAb)-driven and part of the Graves triad with orbitopathy and thyroid acropachy.
First-line treatment is topical high-potency steroids under occlusion plus smoking cessation and euthyroidism.
Avoid surgical excision—lesions recur and may worsen with trauma.

cases.title: A Sleepy, Jaundiced Newborn With a Failed Screen
cases.chiefComplaint: Abnormal newborn screen with persistent jaundice and poor feeding.
cases.history: A 2-week-old full-term infant is brought in after the newborn screening laboratory flagged an abnormal thyroid result. The parents report the baby has been increasingly sleepy, feeds slowly and tires during feeds, and has had persistent yellowing of the skin that has not resolved. There has been infrequent stooling with hard stools and the baby seems 'too quiet.' The pregnancy was uncomplicated and delivery was at term with a normal birth weight. There is no maternal history of thyroid disease or antithyroid medication use, and the mother did not take amiodarone or excessive iodine. Family history is unremarkable. The infant is exclusively breastfed. There were no perinatal complications. The parents note the baby's cry sounds hoarse and the tongue looks large. Immunizations are up to date for age. The infant has not had any fevers. The parents are anxious about the screening result and the prolonged jaundice, which they were told to have evaluated promptly.
cases.exam: Lethargic but arousable infant with scleral and cutaneous jaundice. There is an enlarged tongue (macroglossia), an umbilical hernia, and dry, mottled skin with cool extremities. The anterior fontanelle is large and a posterior fontanelle is palpable/open. Tone is mildly decreased (hypotonia). The cry is hoarse. No goiter is palpable in most cases, though the thyroid may occasionally be enlarged. Cardiac exam shows a regular rate without murmur. The abdomen is soft with the umbilical hernia and no hepatosplenomegaly beyond mild. Red reflex is present bilaterally.

stages.label: CONFIRMATORY LABS
stages.content:
Newborn screen: low T4 with elevated TSH
Serum TSH: 90 mIU/L (markedly elevated)
Serum free T4: low
Total bilirubin: elevated, predominantly unconjugated
Thyroid ultrasound/scan (planned): to assess for thyroid aplasia/dysgenesis vs ectopic gland

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A 2-week-old with an abnormal newborn screen showing low T4 and high TSH, prolonged jaundice, lethargy, macroglossia, and an umbilical hernia most likely has which diagnosis?
stages.explanation: The combination of a low T4 with an elevated TSH on newborn screening plus classic clinical features (prolonged unconjugated jaundice, lethargy, poor feeding, macroglossia, umbilical hernia, hoarse cry, large fontanelles, hypotonia) is diagnostic of congenital hypothyroidism, most often from thyroid dysgenesis. Physiologic jaundice resolves within roughly two weeks and does not explain the thyroid labs or other findings. Neonatal Graves disease causes hyperthyroidism (tachycardia, irritability, weight loss), the opposite picture. Biliary atresia causes CONJUGATED hyperbilirubinemia with acholic stools, whereas this infant has unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia and a clearly abnormal thyroid screen.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the most common cause of permanent congenital hypothyroidism worldwide in iodine-sufficient regions?
options: Thyroid dysgenesis (aplasia, hypoplasia, or ectopic gland)
stages.explanation: In iodine-sufficient areas, thyroid dysgenesis—aplasia, hypoplasia, or an ectopic (e.g., lingual/sublingual) gland—is the most common cause of permanent congenital hypothyroidism, followed by dyshormonogenesis (inborn errors of hormone synthesis). Maternal Graves disease can cause transient neonatal hyperthyroidism (or transient hypothyroidism from maternal antithyroid drugs), not the most common permanent cause. Iodine deficiency is the leading cause worldwide overall but specifically in iodine-DEFICIENT regions; the question specifies iodine-sufficient settings. Transplacental antithyroid drug exposure causes transient, not permanent, hypothyroidism and is far less common than dysgenesis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management once congenital hypothyroidism is confirmed?
options: Delay treatment until 6 months of age to confirm permanence
Start levothyroxine promptly (ideally within the first 2 weeks of life) at appropriate weight-based dosing
Observe and recheck labs in 3 months
stages.explanation: Congenital hypothyroidism is a medical urgency because thyroid hormone is essential for neurodevelopment; levothyroxine should be started promptly, ideally within the first two weeks of life, at appropriate weight-based dosing (relatively high for infants, e.g., ~10-15 mcg/kg/day), with close monitoring to normalize T4/TSH quickly. Delaying treatment to confirm permanence risks irreversible cognitive impairment; the goal is to treat now and reassess etiology/permanence later (often around age 3) if needed. Liothyronine is not the standard agent—levothyroxine provides reliable T4 and peripheral T3. Observing for 3 months would dangerously delay therapy during a critical window for brain development.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Why is newborn screening for congenital hypothyroidism considered one of the most important public health screening programs?
options: Because untreated disease causes only cosmetic changes
Because early detection and prompt levothyroxine prevent intellectual disability (cretinism), which is otherwise irreversible
Because the condition always resolves on its own
Because it identifies infants who need surgery
stages.explanation: Congenital hypothyroidism is frequently asymptomatic or only subtly symptomatic at birth, yet untreated disease causes severe, IRREVERSIBLE intellectual disability and growth failure (historically 'cretinism'); because prompt levothyroxine within the first weeks of life prevents these devastating, permanent neurodevelopmental consequences, universal newborn screening is one of the highest-impact public health programs. The disease is not merely cosmetic, and it does not reliably resolve on its own (most permanent cases are due to dysgenesis). Screening identifies infants needing hormone replacement, not surgery; the therapeutic intervention is medical, making early biochemical detection the critical step.

pearls: Suspect congenital hypothyroidism with prolonged jaundice, lethargy, poor feeding, macroglossia, and umbilical hernia.
Newborn screen shows low T4 with high TSH; thyroid dysgenesis is the most common cause in iodine-sufficient areas.
Start levothyroxine promptly (within ~2 weeks of life) at weight-based dosing—do not delay.
Early treatment prevents otherwise irreversible intellectual disability; this is why universal screening exists.

cases.title: A Teen Boy with Sore, Swollen Breast Tissue
cases.chiefComplaint: Tender lumps under both nipples that have grown over the past few months.
cases.history: A 16-year-old boy presents with his mother because of firm, tender tissue beneath both nipples that he first noticed about 4 months ago. The swelling is mildly painful when his shirt rubs against it and causes him embarrassment in the locker room. He denies nipple discharge, rapid breast enlargement, or a single hard mass. He reached Tanner stage 3 genital development around age 13 and shaves intermittently. He has had normal linear growth and a recent growth spurt. He takes no medications, denies anabolic steroid, marijuana, or supplement use, and drinks no alcohol. He has no testicular pain, swelling, headaches, or visual changes. Past medical history is unremarkable. He is a sophomore athlete with good grades. Family history: father and an older brother both had transient breast swelling during their teens that resolved. There is no family history of testicular cancer, thyroid disease, or liver disease. Review of systems is negative for weight loss, heat intolerance, palpitations, and fatigue.
cases.exam: Well-appearing adolescent in no distress. BMI 22. Bilateral, symmetric, mobile, rubbery subareolar discs of glandular tissue approximately 3 cm, tender to palpation, without skin dimpling, nipple retraction, or discharge. No discrete hard or fixed mass and no axillary or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Testes are bilaterally descended, symmetric, approximately 20 mL each, without masses; no testicular asymmetry. Tanner stage 4 pubic hair. Thyroid is normal in size without nodules. No hepatomegaly, spider angiomata, palmar erythema, or signs of feminization. No visual field deficit on confrontation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely explanation for this adolescent's bilateral subareolar breast tissue?
stages.explanation: Bilateral, tender, mobile, rubbery subareolar glandular tissue in a mid-puberty boy with normal testes and a family history of transient gynecomastia is classic physiologic pubertal gynecomastia, driven by a transient relative excess of estradiol over testosterone; it resolves in most boys within 1-2 years. Pseudogynecomastia is soft fat without a palpable glandular disc and is non-tender. Carcinoma presents as a unilateral, hard, fixed, eccentric mass, often with skin or nipple changes - not bilateral tender discs. Klinefelter syndrome causes gynecomastia but with small firm testes (often under 5 mL), tall eunuchoid build, and hypogonadism, none of which are present here given normal 20 mL testes.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which feature in the history or exam would most prompt you to pursue laboratory and imaging workup rather than reassurance?
options: Rapid progression with small firm testes
stages.explanation: Rapid breast growth combined with small, firm, or asymmetric testes is a red flag suggesting a pathologic cause such as a testicular germ-cell or Leydig-cell tumor, Klinefelter syndrome, or an estrogen-producing source, and warrants hormonal testing and testicular ultrasound. Bilateral symmetry, tenderness, and onset during mid-puberty are all hallmarks of benign physiologic gynecomastia and on their own do not require an extensive workup. This patient's normal-sized symmetric testes and gradual course are reassuring, so red-flag features are what shift management toward investigation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Given the classic benign presentation, only limited testing was obtained:
Morning total testosterone: 480 ng/dL (normal pubertal male)
Estradiol: normal for age
LH 4.2 IU/L, FSH 3.8 IU/L (normal)
beta-hCG: undetectable
TSH 1.8 mIU/L, free T4 1.2 ng/dL
Liver function tests: normal
No testicular ultrasound indicated given symmetric normal-sized testes

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?
options: Start tamoxifen now to shrink the tissue
Reassurance and clinical re-evaluation in 3-6 months
Order an estradiol, hCG, LH, and testosterone panel today
stages.explanation: Physiologic pubertal gynecomastia is self-limited and resolves in roughly 90% of boys within 1-2 years, so reassurance with watchful waiting and re-examination in 3-6 months is the standard approach. Tamoxifen may be considered only for persistent, painful, or distressing gynecomastia after a period of observation, not as first-line therapy. Surgery is reserved for tissue that persists beyond 1-2 years or causes significant cosmetic or psychological distress despite observation. Routine hormonal panels are low yield in a classic benign presentation with normal testes and would be reserved for cases with red-flag features.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Several medications and substances can cause gynecomastia. Which of the following is a well-recognized pharmacologic cause to screen for in adolescents?
stages.explanation: Anabolic-androgenic steroids are aromatized peripherally to estrogens and are a classic, often-missed cause of gynecomastia in adolescent and young-adult males, making explicit screening important. Other well-known offenders include spironolactone, ketoconazole, cimetidine, marijuana, and certain antipsychotics. Ibuprofen, amoxicillin, and loratadine are not associated with gynecomastia. Asking directly and non-judgmentally about supplements, performance enhancers, and recreational drugs is part of every gynecomastia evaluation in this age group, even when the rest of the picture looks physiologic.

pearls: Pubertal gynecomastia is bilateral, tender, mobile glandular tissue that resolves spontaneously in most boys within 1-2 years.
A unilateral, hard, fixed, eccentric mass with skin or nipple change raises concern for breast carcinoma and warrants imaging and biopsy.
Small firm testes plus gynecomastia suggest Klinefelter syndrome; rapid progression with a testicular mass suggests a hormone-secreting tumor.
Always screen for offending drugs: anabolic steroids, spironolactone, cimetidine, ketoconazole, antipsychotics, and marijuana.

cases.title: A Young Girl with Early Breast Growth and a Sudden Height Spurt
cases.chiefComplaint: Breast development and a rapid increase in height in a 6-year-old.
cases.history: A 6-year-old girl is brought in by her parents because she developed breast buds about 8 months ago and has grown out of her clothes unusually fast. They also report fine pubic hair, mild body odor, and occasional emotional outbursts, but no vaginal bleeding. She was born full-term and has met all developmental milestones. There is no history of head trauma, central nervous system infection, seizures, or cranial irradiation. She takes no medications and has had no access to estrogen creams, oral contraceptives, or supplements. She has no headaches, vomiting, or visual complaints. Past medical history is unremarkable. Review of systems is negative for skin pigmentation changes, bone pain, and fractures. Family history: mother reached menarche at age 12, father had normal puberty, and there is no family history of precocious puberty, neurofibromatosis, or McCune-Albright syndrome. Growth records show her height tracking at the 50th percentile until 18 months ago, when it crossed upward to above the 97th percentile. Weight has tracked appropriately.
cases.exam: Height above the 97th percentile, well ahead of mid-parental target; weight at the 75th percentile; growth velocity accelerated. Tanner stage 3 breast development with palpable glandular tissue and areolar enlargement. Tanner stage 2 pubic hair. No axillary hair. No clitoromegaly; external genitalia show estrogenized, pink, moist vaginal mucosa without bleeding. Skin examination shows no large café-au-lait macules with irregular borders and no axillary freckling. Thyroid is normal. Abdomen is soft without palpable mass. Neurologic exam, including visual fields and fundoscopy, is normal. No cushingoid features.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Breast and pubic hair development before what age in girls is generally considered precocious and warrants evaluation?
stages.explanation: Pubertal development before age 8 in girls (and before age 9 in boys) is the conventional threshold for precocious puberty and prompts evaluation, particularly when accompanied by accelerated growth and advancing bone age. This girl at age 6 with Tanner 3 breasts and a growth spurt clearly meets criteria. Using age 10 or 12 would inappropriately delay workup of a 6-year-old with clear secondary sexual characteristics. While some clinicians lower the threshold to 6-7 in certain ethnic groups for isolated breast development, the combination of breast development, pubic hair, and growth acceleration before 8 always warrants evaluation regardless.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first test to distinguish central (gonadotropin-dependent) from peripheral (gonadotropin-independent) precocious puberty?
stages.explanation: A GnRH or GnRH-agonist stimulation test is the gold standard to separate central from peripheral precocious puberty: a pubertal LH response (LH rising into the pubertal range, with an LH-predominant pattern) indicates activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and confirms central precocious puberty, whereas a suppressed, prepubertal LH response points to a peripheral source. A random estradiol is too variable to be diagnostic. Pelvic ultrasound assesses uterine and ovarian maturation and can support the diagnosis but does not establish central versus peripheral mechanism. Karyotype is not part of the routine workup of an isosexual precocious girl with no dysmorphic features.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Bone age (left hand/wrist): 9 years (chronologic age 6)
Basal LH 0.8 IU/L
GnRH-agonist stimulated peak LH 9.4 IU/L (pubertal response)
Stimulated peak FSH 7.1 IU/L (LH-predominant pattern)
Estradiol 38 pg/mL
TSH 2.1 mIU/L, free T4 1.1 ng/dL
Pelvic ultrasound: uterine length 4.2 cm with endometrial stripe, bilateral enlarged ovaries with multiple follicles
17-OHP 45 ng/dL (normal), DHEA-S normal

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given a pubertal LH response confirming central precocious puberty, what is the most important next diagnostic step in this 6-year-old girl?
options: Start a GnRH agonist immediately without further imaging
MRI of the brain with attention to the hypothalamic-pituitary region
Abdominal CT to look for an adrenal tumor
Repeat the GnRH stimulation test in 6 months
stages.explanation: Once central precocious puberty is confirmed, brain MRI is essential to exclude an intracranial lesion such as a hypothalamic hamartoma, glioma, or other CNS pathology - especially important in a young child, since the probability of an identifiable CNS lesion rises the younger the child is at onset. Starting a GnRH agonist before imaging risks masking a treatable or dangerous lesion. Adrenal CT is appropriate for peripheral causes with elevated adrenal androgens, but her workup points to a central, LH-driven process. Repeating the stimulation test adds nothing once a clearly pubertal response has been documented.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Assuming brain MRI is unremarkable (idiopathic central precocious puberty), what is the primary goal and standard treatment?
options: No treatment, since idiopathic cases always resolve spontaneously
An aromatase inhibitor to block estrogen synthesis
A long-acting GnRH agonist to halt pubertal progression and preserve adult height
Combined oral contraceptives to regulate the cycle
stages.explanation: A continuously administered (depot) GnRH agonist downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing LH and FSH and thereby halting pubertal progression; the principal goals are to arrest premature sexual development and to slow bone-age advancement so that growth-plate fusion is delayed and adult height potential is preserved, along with addressing psychosocial concerns. Idiopathic central precocious puberty does not reliably resolve on its own and can compromise final height through rapid skeletal maturation. Aromatase inhibitors are used in select peripheral conditions (such as McCune-Albright syndrome), not central precocious puberty. Oral contraceptives do not suppress the central axis appropriately and are not the treatment.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If central precocious puberty were left untreated, which adverse outcome is the most important consequence to counsel the family about?
options: Compromised (reduced) adult height from premature growth-plate fusion
stages.explanation: The major long-term consequence of untreated central precocious puberty is a compromised final adult height: persistently elevated sex steroids accelerate bone maturation and cause the growth plates to fuse prematurely, so although the child is tall as a young child, the early epiphyseal closure curtails total growth and results in a shorter-than-expected adult stature - the very outcome GnRH-agonist therapy aims to prevent. Central precocious puberty does not cause permanent infertility; reproductive function is normal after treatment. It is unrelated to the autoimmune pathogenesis of type 1 diabetes. There is no established link to ovarian cancer. Height preservation and psychosocial well-being are the principal reasons to treat.

pearls: Puberty before age 8 in girls or 9 in boys with accelerated growth and advanced bone age defines precocious puberty.
A pubertal LH response on GnRH stimulation confirms central (gonadotropin-dependent) precocious puberty.
Always obtain brain MRI in confirmed central cases; the younger the child, the higher the risk of an intracranial lesion.
Depot GnRH agonists halt progression and protect adult height by slowing bone-age advancement.

cases.title: A Short 14-Year-Old Boy Worried He Hasn't Caught Up
cases.chiefComplaint: Being the shortest in his class and not yet showing signs of puberty.
cases.history: A 14-year-old boy presents with his father, concerned that he is the smallest boy in his grade and has not started puberty like his peers. He reports no growth of facial or underarm hair and no voice change. He eats well, has normal energy, and plays soccer without limitation. He denies headaches, visual changes, anosmia, chronic diarrhea, abdominal pain, or recurrent illness. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He was born full-term at a normal birth weight. His growth chart shows that he has tracked steadily along the 5th-10th percentile for height since early childhood, with a normal but slightly slow growth velocity, and no recent fall-off. Past medical history is unremarkable. Social history: he is doing well in school though somewhat self-conscious. Family history is notable: his father reports being a late bloomer who did not have his growth spurt until 16 and kept growing into college, reaching a normal adult height; his mother had menarche at 14. There is no family history of thyroid disease, celiac disease, or syndromic short stature.
cases.exam: Healthy, proportionate boy who appears younger than his stated age. Height at the 5th percentile, weight tracking proportionately, body proportions normal (normal arm span and upper-to-lower segment ratio). Tanner stage 1-2 genital development with testicular volume approximately 4 mL bilaterally and Tanner stage 1-2 pubic hair. No goiter. Sense of smell is intact. Visual fields full and fundoscopy normal. No midline facial defects, no webbed neck, and no skeletal dysmorphism. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are normal. Neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial test is most useful in evaluating short stature with apparent pubertal delay in this boy?
options: A left-hand and wrist radiograph for bone age
stages.explanation: A bone-age radiograph (left hand and wrist) is the single most informative initial test: a bone age that is delayed relative to chronologic age but consistent with height age suggests preserved growth potential and points toward constitutional delay. Random growth hormone levels are useless because GH is secreted in pulses; stimulation testing is reserved for cases suggesting true deficiency. Brain MRI is indicated if there are red flags such as visual changes, headaches, or a falling growth velocity, none present here. Karyotype is appropriate for girls with short stature (Turner syndrome) but is not a first-line test for a phenotypically normal boy with a strong family history of late puberty.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Bone age: 12 years (chronologic age 14)
Morning testosterone: 45 ng/dL (early pubertal range)
LH 1.8 IU/L, FSH 2.4 IU/L
TSH 1.9 mIU/L, free T4 1.2 ng/dL
IGF-1: low-normal for chronologic age but appropriate for bone age
CBC normal, CMP normal
Tissue transglutaminase IgA: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the history, exam, and these results, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty
stages.explanation: A healthy boy with lifelong tracking along a low-but-stable height percentile, a bone age delayed by about 2 years that matches his height age, early pubertal testosterone, and a strong paternal history of late blooming fits constitutional delay of growth and puberty - essentially a normal variant in which the tempo of maturation is shifted later. GH deficiency typically causes a falling growth velocity with a more severely low IGF-1 not explained by bone age, and warrants stimulation testing. Hypothyroidism is excluded by normal TSH and free T4. Congenital hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (e.g., Kallmann syndrome) would usually feature anosmia or absent puberty without the reassuring family history and early-pubertal labs.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this boy with constitutional delay?
options: Reassurance with watchful waiting, offering a short course of low-dose testosterone if distress is significant
stages.explanation: Constitutional delay is a benign normal variant, so reassurance and observation are first-line; most boys progress through puberty and reach a normal adult height consistent with their genetic potential. For an adolescent with significant psychosocial distress, a short (3-6 month) low-dose testosterone course can jump-start secondary sexual characteristics and a growth spurt without compromising final height or shutting down the axis. Growth hormone is not indicated when the axis is intact and IGF-1 is appropriate for bone age. Lifelong testosterone is for permanent hypogonadism, not a maturational delay. There is no pituitary lesion to operate on.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Regarding final adult height, what is the most accurate counseling for this family?
options: He will likely be permanently short and below his genetic target
His height cannot be predicted at all
He is expected to reach an adult height appropriate for his mid-parental (genetic) target
He requires growth hormone to reach a normal height
stages.explanation: Because constitutional delay reflects delayed skeletal maturation rather than impaired growth potential, the delayed bone age means the growth plates remain open longer, allowing continued growth after peers have stopped; these boys typically catch up and reach an adult height consistent with their mid-parental target. Telling the family he will remain short is inaccurate and needlessly distressing. While exact final height carries some uncertainty, the bone age and mid-parental calculation allow a reasonable estimate, so saying it cannot be predicted is wrong. Growth hormone is not required because his GH-IGF-1 axis is intact and the issue is timing, not deficiency.

cases.diagnosis: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty
pearls: Constitutional delay is the most common cause of short stature with pubertal delay and is a benign normal variant.
The hallmark is a bone age that is delayed but matches height age, with a strong family history of late bloomers.
First-line management is reassurance; a short low-dose testosterone course can be offered for significant distress.
A falling growth velocity or very low IGF-1 unexplained by bone age should redirect the workup toward true GH deficiency.

cases.title: A Short 15-Year-Old Girl Who Has Never Had a Period
cases.chiefComplaint: Short stature and never having started menstrual periods.
cases.history: A 15-year-old girl presents with her mother for evaluation of short stature and primary amenorrhea. She has been consistently the shortest in her class and has not developed breasts or had a menstrual period. She denies cyclic abdominal pain, galactorrhea, headaches, visual changes, or anosmia. She has a history of recurrent otitis media in childhood and was told she had a heart murmur as an infant that was being monitored. She takes no medications and has no allergies. She was born full-term; her mother recalls puffy hands and feet as a newborn. School performance is average with some difficulty in math. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Family history: her mother is of average height with menarche at 12, her father is of average height, and there is no family history of delayed puberty, autoimmune disease, or congenital heart disease. Review of systems is negative for cold intolerance, weight change, and recurrent infections, but she reports she has always felt self-conscious about her appearance and hearing.
cases.exam: Short, well-proportioned girl with height below the 3rd percentile. Notable features: a low posterior hairline with mild webbing of the neck, widely spaced nipples on a broad (shield) chest, and an increased carrying angle (cubitus valgus) of the elbows. Multiple pigmented nevi. Tanner stage 1 breast development; Tanner stage 2 sparse pubic hair. External genitalia are prepubertal in appearance. Cardiac exam reveals a soft systolic ejection murmur best heard at the upper sternal border and a brachial-femoral pulse delay. No goiter. Otoscopy shows scarring from prior infections. Neurologic exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given short stature, primary amenorrhea, and these physical features, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
stages.explanation: The constellation of short stature, primary amenorrhea, neck webbing, broad shield chest with widely spaced nipples, cubitus valgus, and a history of newborn lymphedema and a cardiac murmur strongly suggests Turner syndrome, for which a karyotype is the definitive initial test, classically revealing 45,X (or a mosaic). Prolactin would be checked in a workup of amenorrhea but does not explain the syndromic features. Pelvic ultrasound may show streak gonads and a small uterus but is supportive, not diagnostic. A GnRH stimulation test characterizes the axis but is unnecessary once the syndromic phenotype directs you straight to chromosomal analysis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Karyotype: 45,X (30 of 30 cells)
FSH: 82 IU/L (markedly elevated)
LH: 38 IU/L
Estradiol: <10 pg/mL
TSH 3.1 mIU/L, free T4 1.0 ng/dL; TPO antibodies positive
Pelvic ultrasound: small infantile uterus, bilateral streak gonads
Echocardiogram: bicuspid aortic valve with mild coarctation of the aorta
Renal ultrasound: horseshoe kidney

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The elevated FSH and LH with low estradiol in this patient indicate which type of hormonal failure?
stages.explanation: Markedly elevated FSH and LH with low estradiol indicate hypergonadotropic hypogonadism: the gonads (here, streak ovaries in Turner syndrome) cannot produce estrogen, so the pituitary loses negative feedback and gonadotropins rise. Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism shows low or inappropriately normal gonadotropins with low estradiol, reflecting a central (hypothalamic-pituitary) problem. A prolactinoma would typically suppress gonadotropins and raise prolactin, not elevate FSH/LH. Constitutional delay shows low-normal gonadotropins appropriate to a delayed but functional axis and is a normal variant, inconsistent with streak gonads and a 45,X karyotype.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond the cardiac and renal imaging already obtained, which additional baseline screening is most important to initiate in this newly diagnosed Turner patient?
options: Annual screening for autoimmune thyroiditis, celiac disease, hearing, and metabolic risk
Serial serum tumor markers for ovarian malignancy
stages.explanation: Turner syndrome is associated with multiple comorbidities that require structured surveillance, so initiating periodic screening for autoimmune (Hashimoto) thyroiditis, celiac disease, hearing loss (recurrent otitis and sensorineural decline), and metabolic risk (glucose intolerance, dyslipidemia, hypertension) is the highest-yield next step; this patient already has positive TPO antibodies. Serial ovarian tumor markers are not indicated unless a Y-chromosome cell line is present (which raises gonadoblastoma risk and prompts gonadectomy), and her karyotype is 45,X. Bone marrow biopsy has no role. Cardiac imaging is essential but is performed periodically, not monthly; monthly echocardiography is excessive and not standard.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which therapy is used during childhood to improve final adult height in Turner syndrome?
stages.explanation: Recombinant human growth hormone is given to girls with Turner syndrome (who are not GH-deficient but have intrinsic skeletal growth failure) to increase adult height, and it is started when growth begins to fall off, before estrogen is introduced. Estrogen is needed to induce puberty and protect bone, but it is generally deferred and introduced in low doses around age 11-12 so as not to prematurely fuse the growth plates and limit height gain. Combined oral contraceptives are used later for cycle maintenance, not for height. Thyroid hormone is given only if hypothyroidism develops (Turner patients need monitoring for autoimmune thyroiditis), not for stature.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which life-threatening cardiovascular complication must be screened for and monitored long-term in Turner syndrome?
stages.explanation: Turner syndrome carries a substantially increased risk of aortic root dilation progressing to aortic dissection or rupture, a leading cause of premature death, so lifelong cardiac imaging surveillance (echocardiography and often cardiac MRI) and blood-pressure control are essential, especially given this patient's bicuspid aortic valve and coarctation. While other defects occur, mitral valve prolapse, pulmonary hypertension, and isolated atrial septal defect are not the characteristic catastrophic risks driving surveillance. The combination of bicuspid valve, coarctation, and hypertension markedly raises dissection risk and demands proactive monitoring and counseling, particularly around pregnancy.

pearls: Suspect Turner syndrome in any girl with short stature plus primary amenorrhea; confirm with karyotype (classically 45,X).
Streak gonads cause hypergonadotropic hypogonadism: high FSH/LH with low estradiol.
Growth hormone improves adult height and should precede low-dose estrogen, which is timed to allow further growth.
Lifelong surveillance is needed for aortic dilation/dissection, hypertension, autoimmune thyroiditis, and renal anomalies.

cases.title: An 8-Year-Old Boy Who Has Slipped Off His Growth Curve
cases.chiefComplaint: Falling height percentiles noted by the pediatrician over the last two years.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy is referred after his pediatrician noted that his height has crossed downward from the 50th percentile at age 5 to below the 3rd percentile now, while his weight has remained relatively preserved. His parents report he is otherwise healthy, eats well, and has normal energy and school performance. He has no headaches, vomiting, visual changes, or polyuria. He has had no chronic illnesses, no recurrent infections, and no gastrointestinal symptoms. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He was born full-term at a normal weight; there was one episode of neonatal hypoglycemia that resolved. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Social history: he lives with both parents and a younger sibling who is growing normally. Family history: father's height is at the 50th percentile, mother's at the 40th percentile, giving a mid-parental target near the 50th percentile; no family history of short stature, thyroid disease, or pubertal delay. Review of systems is negative for cold intolerance, constipation, and abdominal pain.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, proportionate young boy who looks his age but is notably short, with height below the 3rd percentile and weight at the 25th percentile, giving a slightly chubby appearance with mild central adiposity. Growth velocity is clearly reduced. Body proportions are normal. He has a youthful, immature facial appearance and a high-pitched voice. Genital exam is Tanner stage 1, appropriate for age, with no micropenis noted. Thyroid is normal. Visual fields are full and fundoscopy is normal. No midline facial defects (normal philtrum, no cleft). Cardiopulmonary, abdominal, and neurologic exams are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What pattern of growth is the strongest clue that this child has an acquired or endocrine cause of short stature rather than a normal variant?
options: Height consistently at the 3rd percentile since infancy
Crossing downward across major percentile lines with a low growth velocity
A delayed bone age matching height age
stages.explanation: Crossing downward through major height percentiles with a subnormal growth velocity is the red-flag pattern that signals a pathologic process, including endocrine disorders such as growth hormone deficiency or hypothyroidism, and mandates workup. Children with familial short stature or constitutional delay typically track steadily along (not across) a percentile. Weight being preserved or even high relative to height (as here) actually argues against systemic illness or malnutrition and is more consistent with an endocrine cause - in malnutrition, weight usually falls before height. A delayed bone age matching height age is characteristic of constitutional delay, a benign variant.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of tests best establishes the diagnosis of growth hormone deficiency?
options: A single random growth hormone level
Low IGF-1 (and IGF-BP3) plus a subnormal response on GH stimulation testing
Elevated IGF-1 with high random GH
stages.explanation: Because GH is secreted in pulses, a random GH level is uninterpretable; instead, a low IGF-1 (and IGF-BP3, which integrate GH activity over time) raises suspicion, and the diagnosis is confirmed by a subnormal GH rise on two provocative stimulation tests after first excluding hypothyroidism and other causes. A single random GH is therefore inadequate. Elevated IGF-1 with high GH would suggest GH excess or insensitivity, not deficiency. Karyotype and pelvic ultrasound are part of the Turner syndrome workup in girls and are not relevant to confirming GH deficiency in a boy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Bone age: 5.5 years (chronologic age 8) - delayed
IGF-1: low for age
IGF-BP3: low
TSH 2.0 mIU/L, free T4 1.1 ng/dL (normal)
GH stimulation test #1 (arginine): peak GH 4.1 ng/mL
GH stimulation test #2 (clonidine): peak GH 3.2 ng/mL (both subnormal, <10)
CBC, CMP, tissue transglutaminase IgA: normal
MRI pituitary: small anterior pituitary; otherwise no mass

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After confirming GH deficiency with subnormal stimulation testing, what imaging is essential before initiating therapy?
stages.explanation: MRI of the hypothalamic-pituitary region is essential in confirmed GH deficiency to evaluate the anatomy and exclude a structural lesion such as a craniopharyngioma, other tumor, or congenital midline/pituitary malformations; here it revealed a small anterior pituitary, supporting the diagnosis. A bone-age radiograph is useful for assessing maturation and predicting height but does not assess the cause. Abdominal ultrasound and skeletal survey are not relevant to identifying a central cause of GH deficiency. Identifying or excluding a CNS lesion is critical because it changes management and prognosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate treatment for confirmed childhood growth hormone deficiency?
options: Daily subcutaneous recombinant human growth hormone, titrated to growth response and IGF-1
stages.explanation: Confirmed GH deficiency is treated with daily subcutaneous recombinant human growth hormone, with the dose adjusted according to growth velocity, height gain, and IGF-1 levels, continued until growth plates fuse (and reassessed for adult GH deficiency thereafter). GH is a peptide hormone destroyed by digestion, so no effective oral formulation exists. Testosterone would advance bone age and may be used to induce puberty later but does not treat the underlying GH deficiency in a prepubertal child. Nutritional supplementation does not correct an endocrine hormone deficiency and would not restore normal growth in this setting.

pearls: Crossing downward across height percentiles with a low growth velocity is the key red flag for a pathologic cause of short stature.
Random GH is useless; diagnose GH deficiency with low IGF-1/IGF-BP3 plus subnormal response on two stimulation tests.
Always obtain pituitary MRI after confirming GH deficiency to exclude a structural lesion such as craniopharyngioma.
Treat with daily subcutaneous recombinant GH titrated to growth velocity and IGF-1; there is no oral GH.

cases.title: A Woman with Increasing Coarse Facial and Body Hair
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening dark, coarse hair on the face, chest, and abdomen over the past two years.
cases.history: A 26-year-old woman presents because of progressively coarse, dark hair on her upper lip, chin, chest, and lower abdomen that she now shaves several times a week. The change has been gradual over about two years. Her menses are somewhat irregular, occurring every 35-45 days, but she still has periods and is not trying to conceive. She has noticed mild acne but no scalp hair loss, voice change, or clitoral enlargement, and no rapid muscle gain. She takes no medications, including no anabolic agents, danazol, or testosterone, and uses no supplements. She has no galactorrhea, headaches, or visual symptoms. Past medical history is unremarkable; she has a normal BMI and no skin darkening in skinfolds. Social history: she is a graduate student, drinks socially, does not smoke. Family history: her mother and a sister also have noticeable facial hair and irregular periods; there is no family history of congenital adrenal hyperplasia diagnosed, infertility, or early-onset hypertension. Review of systems is otherwise negative for weight change, easy bruising, and proximal weakness.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman with normal body habitus. Terminal coarse hairs over the upper lip, chin, periareolar region, and linea alba, with a modified Ferriman-Gallwey score of approximately 12 (consistent with hirsutism). Mild comedonal and inflammatory acne on the face. No temporal balding, no clitoromegaly (clitoral size normal), no deepening of the voice on conversation, and no increased muscle bulk. No acanthosis nigricans, no striae, no moon facies, no supraclavicular fat pad. Thyroid normal. Abdominal exam reveals no palpable mass. No signs of Cushing syndrome.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature distinguishes hirsutism (which this patient has) from simple hypertrichosis?
options: Fine vellus hair diffusely over the body
Coarse terminal hair in an androgen-dependent male pattern
stages.explanation: Hirsutism is the growth of coarse, dark terminal hair in a male (androgen-dependent) distribution - upper lip, chin, chest, abdomen, and inner thighs - reflecting androgen action on hair follicles, and is quantified with the modified Ferriman-Gallwey scale. Hypertrichosis, by contrast, is generalized excess fine vellus hair that is not androgen-dependent and is often drug-induced or genetic. Hair limited to the scalp is not hirsutism, and lifelong generalized hair from birth suggests congenital hypertrichosis rather than acquired androgen excess. Recognizing the androgen-dependent terminal-hair pattern is what directs the workup toward an androgen source.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial laboratory workup for this patient with moderate, gradually progressive hirsutism?
options: Total testosterone, plus 17-hydroxyprogesterone and consideration of DHEA-S, prolactin, and TSH
An overnight dexamethasone suppression test alone
stages.explanation: For hirsutism that is more than mild or is progressive, measuring total testosterone screens for significant androgen excess, while an early-morning 17-hydroxyprogesterone screens for nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia; DHEA-S helps assess an adrenal source, and prolactin and TSH evaluate other contributors to menstrual irregularity. Empiric treatment without any labs risks missing a tumor or CAH in a patient with progression. Adrenal vein sampling is an invasive, specialized test used for lateralizing aldosterone (or rarely androgen) excess, not an initial screen. An overnight dexamethasone suppression test screens for Cushing syndrome, which is not suggested here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Total testosterone: 62 ng/dL (mildly elevated; upper limit ~45-50)
DHEA-S: normal
17-hydroxyprogesterone (early AM): 120 ng/dL (normal, <200)
Prolactin: normal
TSH: 2.2 mIU/L
LH/FSH ratio: mildly elevated
Fasting glucose 92 mg/dL; pelvic ultrasound: ovaries with multiple small peripheral follicles

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the gradual onset, only mildly elevated testosterone, normal DHEA-S, normal 17-OHP, and polycystic-appearing ovaries, which diagnosis is most consistent?
stages.explanation: Gradual hirsutism with irregular menses, only mildly elevated testosterone, normal DHEA-S, a normal 17-OHP, polycystic-appearing ovaries, and a family history of similar features fits PCOS, the most common cause of androgen-driven hirsutism in reproductive-age women; idiopathic hirsutism is diagnosed when androgens are entirely normal. An androgen-secreting tumor is excluded by the modest testosterone and the slow, non-virilizing course (markedly high levels and rapid virilization would be expected). Cushing syndrome is unlikely without weight gain, striae, or proximal weakness. Nonclassic CAH is largely excluded by the normal early-morning 17-OHP, which is the screening test for that condition.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic therapy for hirsutism in this woman who does not currently desire pregnancy?
options: Combined oral contraceptive pills, with an antiandrogen such as spironolactone added if needed
Metformin as monotherapy for the hair
stages.explanation: Combined oral contraceptives are first-line for hirsutism in women not seeking pregnancy: they suppress ovarian androgen production and raise sex-hormone-binding globulin, lowering free testosterone, and also regulate menses; an antiandrogen such as spironolactone can be added (with reliable contraception, given teratogenicity) if response is inadequate after about 6 months. Metformin improves insulin resistance and ovulation in PCOS but is not effective primary therapy for hirsutism itself. Glucocorticoids are used in CAH, not PCOS-related hirsutism. GnRH agonists are reserved for severe refractory cases and are not first-line because of cost and hypoestrogenic side effects.

cases.diagnosis: Androgen-driven hirsutism due to polycystic ovary syndrome
pearls: Hirsutism is coarse terminal hair in a male pattern (androgen-dependent); hypertrichosis is generalized fine vellus hair.
Initial workup includes total testosterone, early-AM 17-OHP (for nonclassic CAH), and often DHEA-S, prolactin, and TSH.
PCOS is the most common cause; gradual onset with mild androgen elevation is typical, and idiopathic hirsutism has normal androgens.
First-line treatment for women not seeking pregnancy is a combined oral contraceptive, adding spironolactone if needed.

cases.title: A 48-Year-Old Woman with a Deepening Voice and Rapid Hair Growth
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapidly increasing facial and body hair with a noticeably deeper voice over six months.
cases.history: A 48-year-old woman presents with a six-month history of rapidly worsening coarse facial and body hair, deepening of her voice, increasing muscle bulk, and thinning of her scalp hair at the temples. Her periods, previously regular for her age, have stopped over the past four months. She also reports an increased libido. She denies easy bruising, purple striae, central weight gain, or proximal muscle weakness. She takes no exogenous androgens, no danazol, and no anabolic supplements. Past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily; no diabetes. She has no prior menstrual irregularity and three prior uncomplicated pregnancies. Social history: nonsmoker, occasional wine. Family history: no known endocrine tumors, no CAH, mother with type 2 diabetes. Review of systems is notable for the rapid timeframe of the changes and is otherwise negative for headaches, visual changes, flushing, and abdominal pain. She is alarmed by how quickly the changes have developed.
cases.exam: Woman appearing her age with clear signs of virilization. Marked terminal hair over the face, chest, and abdomen; temporal scalp recession (male-pattern balding). Voice is audibly deep. Clitoromegaly is present on genital exam. Increased muscle bulk across the shoulders. No moon facies, no supraclavicular or dorsocervical fat pads, no wide violaceous striae, and no proximal weakness on testing. No acanthosis nigricans. Thyroid normal. Abdominal exam: no palpable mass; on bimanual pelvic exam there is a suggestion of a left adnexal fullness. No lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which features in this presentation most strongly suggest a tumor rather than a benign cause of androgen excess?
options: Rapid onset of virilization (deep voice, clitoromegaly, balding, amenorrhea) over months
stages.explanation: Rapid-onset, progressive virilization - deepening voice, clitoromegaly, male-pattern balding, increased muscle bulk, and new amenorrhea developing over months - is the hallmark of a marked androgen excess and strongly suggests an androgen-secreting tumor of the ovary or adrenal gland, mandating urgent evaluation. Gradual hirsutism over years, only mild testosterone elevation, and a family history of hirsutism all point toward benign functional causes such as PCOS or idiopathic hirsutism. The tempo and the presence of true virilization (not just hirsutism) are what separate an ominous tumoral process from a common benign one.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory pattern would best localize the source to the ovary rather than the adrenal gland?
options: Very high DHEA-S with normal testosterone
Very high testosterone with normal or only mildly elevated DHEA-S
stages.explanation: A markedly elevated total testosterone with a normal or only modestly elevated DHEA-S points to an ovarian source, because DHEA-S is produced almost exclusively by the adrenal gland; a very high DHEA-S, by contrast, implicates an adrenal tumor. A high 17-hydroxyprogesterone suggests congenital adrenal hyperplasia, a non-tumoral adrenal enzyme defect that would not explain rapid virilization in a 48-year-old. An elevated aldosterone-to-renin ratio indicates primary aldosteronism, unrelated to androgen excess. Using the testosterone-versus-DHEA-S pattern is the classic way to triage the search between ovary and adrenal.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Total testosterone: 320 ng/dL (markedly elevated; normal female <50)
Free testosterone: markedly elevated
DHEA-S: normal
17-hydroxyprogesterone: normal
FSH 48 IU/L, LH 32 IU/L (postmenopausal range)
Estradiol low
24-hour urinary free cortisol: normal
Transvaginal ultrasound: solid 4 cm left ovarian mass; right ovary atrophic
CT abdomen/pelvis: normal adrenal glands; left ovarian mass confirmed

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: With a markedly elevated testosterone, normal DHEA-S, and a solid left ovarian mass on imaging, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start spironolactone and reassess in 6 months
Surgical removal of the ovarian tumor (oophorectomy)
Initiate a GnRH agonist as definitive therapy
stages.explanation: A solid ovarian mass with a very high testosterone and normal DHEA-S indicates an androgen-secreting ovarian tumor (e.g., Sertoli-Leydig cell tumor); definitive treatment is surgical removal, typically oophorectomy (or salpingo-oophorectomy) with histologic diagnosis and staging as indicated, which both treats the source and confirms the pathology. Spironolactone only blunts peripheral androgen effects and would dangerously delay removal of a neoplasm. Glucocorticoids treat adrenal enzyme defects, not an ovarian tumor. A GnRH agonist may transiently lower ovarian androgen output but is not definitive and does not address the mass; surgery is required.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: If imaging had shown no ovarian or adrenal mass despite a markedly elevated testosterone, what would be the most appropriate next step to localize a small occult source?
options: Empiric bilateral oophorectomy without further testing
Selective ovarian and adrenal venous sampling for androgens
stages.explanation: When biochemistry confirms a marked, tumor-range androgen excess but cross-sectional imaging fails to identify a lesion, selective venous sampling of the ovarian and adrenal veins can lateralize the source by measuring a gradient in androgen concentrations, guiding targeted surgery. Empiric bilateral oophorectomy without localization is excessive and may remove the wrong (or unnecessary) organ, especially since an occult adrenal source could be missed. Lifelong glucocorticoids treat CAH, which is excluded by the normal 17-OHP. Reassurance and observation are unacceptable when a virilizing neoplasm is strongly suspected biochemically; the source must be found and removed.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful removal of the tumor, what should the patient be counseled to expect regarding her virilizing changes?
options: Soft-tissue effects (hirsutism, libido) often improve, but voice changes and clitoromegaly may persist
All changes, including voice deepening and clitoromegaly, will fully reverse within days
Symptoms will not change at all after surgery
She will require lifelong testosterone replacement
stages.explanation: After the androgen source is removed and testosterone normalizes, androgen-driven soft-tissue effects such as hirsutism, acne, and increased libido typically improve over months, and menses may resume if she is not already menopausal; however, voice deepening and clitoromegaly are often only partially reversible because they reflect more permanent tissue changes. Telling her everything reverses within days is inaccurate, as is saying nothing will change - many symptoms do improve. She will not need testosterone replacement; the problem was androgen excess, and her own physiology resumes once the tumor is gone.

cases.diagnosis: Androgen-secreting ovarian tumor (e.g., Sertoli-Leydig cell tumor)
pearls: Rapid virilization (deep voice, clitoromegaly, balding, amenorrhea) signals marked androgen excess and a likely tumor.
Very high testosterone with normal DHEA-S localizes to the ovary; very high DHEA-S localizes to the adrenal gland.
Definitive treatment of an androgen-secreting ovarian tumor is surgical removal with pathologic diagnosis.
Post-treatment, hirsutism and libido often improve, but voice deepening and clitoromegaly may be permanent.

cases.title: A Young Woman with Irregular Periods and Persistent Hair Growth
cases.chiefComplaint: Irregular menstrual periods and unwanted coarse hair, previously told she has PCOS.
cases.history: A 22-year-old woman presents for a second opinion. Since her mid-teens she has had irregular menses (cycles 35-60 days) and coarse hair on her face, chest, and lower abdomen. She was diagnosed with PCOS at 18 and tried a combined oral contraceptive with partial benefit. She reports occasional acne and has had difficulty conceiving with her partner over the past year. She denies rapid progression, voice change, clitoromegaly, galactorrhea, or visual symptoms. She is of Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry. She takes no medications currently and no androgenic supplements. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; she is of normal weight without acanthosis nigricans. Social history: nonsmoker, no alcohol concerns, works as a teacher. Family history: a maternal cousin has similar hirsutism and infertility, and there is a vague history of a relative with 'adrenal problems'; no known congenital adrenal hyperplasia diagnosis. Review of systems is negative for weight change, easy bruising, salt craving, and episodes of dehydration in childhood.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing young woman of normal body habitus. Coarse terminal hair over the upper lip, chin, periareolar area, and lower abdomen, with a Ferriman-Gallwey score around 14. Mild facial acne. No clitoromegaly, no temporal balding, and no deepening of the voice. No acanthosis nigricans, no striae, no cushingoid features. Thyroid is normal. No abdominal masses. No hyperpigmentation of skin creases or buccal mucosa. The remainder of the exam, including cardiovascular and neurologic, is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single screening test is most important to order to distinguish this patient's condition from typical PCOS?
stages.explanation: An early-morning, follicular-phase 17-hydroxyprogesterone is the key screening test for nonclassic (late-onset) congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to 21-hydroxylase deficiency, which clinically mimics PCOS but is caused by a partial enzyme defect; an elevated level prompts confirmatory testing. Fasting insulin and AMH may be abnormal in PCOS but do not identify CAH. The LH/FSH ratio is neither sensitive nor specific and does not separate nonclassic CAH from PCOS. Given her ancestry (nonclassic CAH is more common in Ashkenazi Jews) and a relative with 'adrenal problems,' screening 17-OHP is especially warranted.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Early-morning 17-hydroxyprogesterone: 480 ng/dL (elevated; normal <200)
Total testosterone: 58 ng/dL (mildly elevated)
DHEA-S: upper-normal
Prolactin and TSH: normal
ACTH stimulation test: 17-OHP rises to 1,650 ng/dL at 60 minutes (markedly elevated)
Electrolytes: Na 139, K 4.1 (normal)
Pelvic ultrasound: ovaries unremarkable to mildly polycystic

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The markedly elevated 17-OHP after ACTH stimulation confirms which diagnosis, and which enzyme is deficient?
options: Cushing syndrome from an adrenal adenoma
stages.explanation: A baseline 17-OHP elevation that rises markedly after ACTH stimulation is diagnostic of nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia from 21-hydroxylase (CYP21A2) deficiency: the partial enzyme block diverts precursors into androgens, producing hirsutism and oligomenorrhea that mimic PCOS, but typically without the neonatal salt-wasting or ambiguous genitalia of the classic form. Classic salt-wasting CAH presents in infancy with adrenal crisis, and 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency causes hypertension and hypokalemia, neither seen here. Cushing syndrome is excluded by the clinical picture. Primary ovarian insufficiency causes high gonadotropins and estrogen deficiency, not androgen excess with high 17-OHP.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before she had decided to pursue pregnancy, when her main concerns were hirsutism and irregular menses, which treatment would have been most appropriate?
options: A combined oral contraceptive, with spironolactone added for hirsutism if needed
No treatment because nonclassic CAH never requires therapy
stages.explanation: For a woman with nonclassic CAH who is not seeking pregnancy and whose concerns are hirsutism and menstrual irregularity, a combined oral contraceptive (which suppresses ovarian androgens, raises SHBG, and regulates cycles), with spironolactone added for hirsutism if needed, is first-line - the same symptom-directed approach used for PCOS-related hirsutism. High-dose or daily stress-dose glucocorticoids are unnecessary and cause iatrogenic Cushingoid side effects; unlike classic CAH, nonclassic patients do not have clinically significant cortisol deficiency requiring routine replacement. Saying it never requires therapy is wrong, since she has symptomatic androgen excess; treatment is tailored to her reproductive goals at the time.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: This patient is now actively trying to conceive. What is the most appropriate treatment approach?
options: Low-dose glucocorticoid (e.g., hydrocortisone or prednisone) to suppress adrenal androgens
No treatment is needed for nonclassic CAH
stages.explanation: In nonclassic CAH, excess adrenal androgens result from ACTH-driven overstimulation of the partially blocked pathway; low-dose glucocorticoid suppresses ACTH and thereby reduces androgen production, which can restore ovulation and improve fertility - the appropriate choice for a woman trying to conceive. Combined oral contraceptives and spironolactone help hirsutism but prevent or are contraindicated in pregnancy, so they are inappropriate for someone trying to conceive. 'No treatment' is wrong here because she has symptomatic androgen excess and infertility; glucocorticoid therapy targets the underlying mechanism and supports conception.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Regarding reproductive genetic counseling, what is the most important point for this couple?
options: The child will definitely have classic CAH
Genetic counseling is unnecessary because she has the mild form
Carrier testing of the partner is advised because a child could inherit classic CAH if the partner carries a severe CYP21A2 mutation
stages.explanation: Nonclassic CAH is autosomal recessive; an affected woman carries CYP21A2 mutations, and if her partner is a carrier of a severe (classic) allele, a child could inherit one mild and one severe allele - or two severe alleles - potentially resulting in classic CAH. Therefore partner carrier testing and genetic counseling are advised before or during pregnancy. Saying it is not heritable is incorrect. The child will not 'definitely' have classic CAH; the risk depends on the partner's genotype. Dismissing counseling because her own phenotype is mild ignores the real risk to offspring conferred by allele combinations.

pearls: Nonclassic CAH mimics PCOS; screen with an early-morning 17-OHP, especially in Ashkenazi Jewish patients.
An ACTH stimulation test showing a marked 17-OHP rise confirms 21-hydroxylase deficiency.
For fertility, low-dose glucocorticoid suppresses ACTH-driven adrenal androgens and can restore ovulation.
It is autosomal recessive; offer partner carrier testing because offspring could inherit classic CAH.

cases.title: A 34-Year-Old Woman with Missed Periods and Hot Flashes
cases.chiefComplaint: Periods stopped four months ago along with new hot flashes and night sweats.
cases.history: A 34-year-old woman presents because her menstrual periods, previously regular, have stopped for the past four months, and she has developed hot flashes, night sweats, vaginal dryness, and difficulty sleeping. She also notes decreased libido and some mood changes. She has not been pregnant and a home pregnancy test was negative. She has no galactorrhea, headaches, or visual changes, and no recent severe weight loss, excessive exercise, or major psychological stress. She has no history of chemotherapy, pelvic radiation, or ovarian surgery. She takes no medications and has no allergies. Past medical history includes Hashimoto thyroiditis on levothyroxine. Social history: nonsmoker, works as an accountant, no eating disorder. Family history: her mother went through menopause at age 41, and there is a maternal history of autoimmune thyroid disease; no known fragile X carriers identified. Review of systems is otherwise negative for cold intolerance changes, polyuria, and skin hyperpigmentation. She is distressed because she and her partner had hoped to have children.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Normal body habitus, BMI 23. No goiter or thyroid nodules. Breast exam normal with no galactorrhea expressible. No hirsutism, no acne, no acanthosis nigricans. Pelvic exam shows a normal-sized uterus and slightly atrophic, dry vaginal mucosa; no adnexal masses. Visual fields are full and fundoscopy is normal. No skin hyperpigmentation in creases. Cardiovascular, pulmonary, and neurologic exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After excluding pregnancy, which initial laboratory tests are most appropriate for secondary amenorrhea with menopausal symptoms in a woman under 40?
stages.explanation: After a negative pregnancy test, the core workup for secondary amenorrhea is FSH, estradiol, prolactin, and TSH: FSH and estradiol distinguish ovarian failure (high FSH, low estradiol) from a central/hypothalamic cause (low or normal FSH), while prolactin and TSH screen for hyperprolactinemia and thyroid dysfunction, both reversible causes. Testosterone and DHEA-S evaluate androgen excess, which is not the issue in a woman with hot flashes and estrogen-deficiency symptoms. An ACTH stimulation test screens for adrenal enzyme defects/insufficiency, not amenorrhea. A glucose tolerance test is irrelevant to this presentation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Beta-hCG: negative
FSH: 68 IU/L (elevated; repeat 4 weeks later: 74 IU/L)
LH: 41 IU/L
Estradiol: 14 pg/mL (low)
Prolactin: normal
TSH: 2.3 mIU/L on levothyroxine
Karyotype: 46,XX
Fragile X (FMR1) premutation testing: pending
Pelvic ultrasound: small ovaries with few follicles, normal uterus

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An FSH in the menopausal range on two occasions with low estradiol in a 34-year-old woman best supports which diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Premature ovarian insufficiency is defined by amenorrhea/oligomenorrhea with menopausal-range FSH (often confirmed on two measurements at least a month apart) and low estradiol before age 40 - exactly this patient's pattern, accompanied by hot flashes and an early-menopause family history. Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea shows low or normal FSH from suppressed central drive, not elevated FSH. PCOS causes androgen excess and anovulation with normal or low FSH, not estrogen deficiency. A prolactinoma elevates prolactin and typically lowers gonadotropins; her prolactin is normal and FSH is high, excluding that diagnosis.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Because POI can have identifiable causes, which additional evaluations are appropriate in this patient?
options: Karyotype, FMR1 (fragile X) premutation testing, and screening for associated autoimmune conditions (e.g., adrenal antibodies, thyroid)
Brain MRI for all patients with POI
stages.explanation: POI under age 40 warrants a search for cause: a karyotype detects chromosomal abnormalities (e.g., Turner mosaicism), FMR1 premutation testing screens for fragile X-associated POI (relevant for family planning and relatives), and autoimmune screening is important because POI clusters with autoimmune disorders - this patient already has Hashimoto thyroiditis, raising concern for autoimmune polyglandular involvement including adrenal insufficiency (21-hydroxylase antibodies). Doing no further testing misses treatable and heritable associations. Brain MRI is reserved for central causes (high prolactin or low gonadotropins), not high-FSH ovarian failure. Bone marrow biopsy has no role here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: In the absence of contraindications, what is the recommended hormonal management for a 34-year-old with POI?
options: No hormone therapy; symptoms should be managed with lifestyle alone
Estrogen-progestin (hormone) therapy until at least the average age of natural menopause
stages.explanation: Young women with POI are estrogen-deficient for what should be their reproductive years, so estrogen-progestin therapy (progestin to protect the endometrium in women with a uterus) is recommended until at least the average age of natural menopause (~51) to relieve vasomotor symptoms and, importantly, to protect bone and cardiovascular health from prolonged early estrogen deficiency. Withholding hormones leaves her at risk for osteoporosis and cardiovascular disease and does not control symptoms. Testosterone does not treat estrogen deficiency. A GnRH agonist would further suppress the axis and worsen estrogen deficiency, the opposite of what is needed.

pearls: POI is amenorrhea with menopausal-range FSH and low estradiol before age 40; confirm FSH on two occasions.
Work up the cause: karyotype, FMR1 premutation, and autoimmune screening (especially adrenal and thyroid).
POI often coexists with autoimmune disease - this patient's Hashimoto raises concern for polyglandular autoimmunity.
Give estrogen-progestin therapy until ~age 51 to control symptoms and protect bone and cardiovascular health.

cases.title: A 55-Year-Old Man with Sudden Back Pain After a Minor Strain
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute mid-back pain that started after lifting a light box, with a loss of height noticed by his wife.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents with sharp mid-thoracic back pain that began two weeks ago after lifting a light box at home; the trauma seemed trivial for the severity of pain. His wife notes he has lost about an inch in height over the last year and has become slightly stooped. On further questioning he reports low libido, erectile dysfunction, decreased morning erections, reduced energy, and fewer shaves per week over the past two years. He denies fever, weight loss, night sweats, or bone pain elsewhere. He has no history of glucocorticoid use, no prior fractures, and no excessive alcohol intake (1-2 drinks/week). He does not smoke. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no chronic medications. Social history: office worker, sedentary, limited dairy intake, minimal sun exposure. Family history: no known osteoporosis, no hip fractures, no hypogonadism, no thyroid or parathyroid disease. Review of systems is otherwise negative for kidney stones, polyuria, constipation, and visual changes.
cases.exam: Middle-aged man with mild thoracic kyphosis and point tenderness over a mid-thoracic vertebra; no step-off, no midline spinal mass. Neurologic exam of the lower extremities is intact with normal strength, sensation, and reflexes and no saddle anesthesia. He has decreased body and facial hair and small, soft testes (approximately 10 mL bilaterally) without masses. No gynecomastia. Thyroid normal. No proximal muscle weakness on confrontation. No abdominal mass. No skin hyperpigmentation. Visual fields are full.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A low-trauma (fragility) vertebral fracture in a 55-year-old man should prompt what immediate consideration?
options: It is a normal age-related finding requiring no workup
Underlying osteoporosis is present and a secondary cause should be investigated
It is almost certainly metastatic cancer
He simply needs bed rest and analgesia
stages.explanation: A vertebral fracture from minimal trauma is, by definition, a fragility fracture and is diagnostic of osteoporosis regardless of bone-density results; in a man this relatively young, a secondary cause of bone loss should be sought because men with osteoporosis frequently have an identifiable contributor. Calling it normal aging or treating with rest alone misses an opportunity to prevent further fractures and to find a reversible cause. While malignancy must be considered for vertebral fractures, jumping to 'almost certainly metastatic' is premature without red flags like weight loss or a known primary; the broader, correct first step is to recognize osteoporosis and pursue secondary causes.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Given his low libido, erectile dysfunction, decreased body hair, and small soft testes, which test is most important to include in his secondary osteoporosis workup?
options: Morning total testosterone (with LH and FSH)
stages.explanation: His symptoms and exam point to hypogonadism, a major and reversible cause of osteoporosis in men, so an early-morning total testosterone (testosterone peaks in the morning) together with LH and FSH is essential; LH/FSH then distinguish primary (testicular) from secondary (central) hypogonadism. A random afternoon testosterone can be falsely low and is unreliable for diagnosis. Prolactin is worth checking as part of the central evaluation but alone does not establish hypogonadism. Semen analysis assesses fertility, not the androgen deficiency driving bone loss, and is not the priority for an osteoporosis workup.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Spine X-ray: wedge compression fracture of T8
Morning total testosterone: 142 ng/dL (low; normal ~300-1000)
Repeat morning testosterone: 155 ng/dL (confirmed low)
LH 2.1 IU/L, FSH 2.8 IU/L (inappropriately low/normal)
Prolactin: normal
TSH normal, 25-OH vitamin D 22 ng/mL (insufficient), calcium and PTH normal
DXA: lumbar spine T-score -2.8, femoral neck T-score -2.4
SPEP/serum free light chains: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The low testosterone with low/normal LH and FSH indicates which type of hypogonadism?
stages.explanation: Low testosterone accompanied by low or inappropriately normal LH and FSH defines secondary (hypogonadotropic) hypogonadism, indicating the problem is at the hypothalamus or pituitary rather than the testes. In primary (hypergonadotropic) hypogonadism the testes fail and gonadotropins rise to high levels. Androgen insensitivity is a receptor disorder presenting differently (usually with a female or undervirilized phenotype from birth), not acquired low testosterone in an adult man. Subclinical hyperthyroidism is unrelated and his TSH is normal. Because gonadotropins are not elevated, further central evaluation (e.g., prolactin, iron studies, and pituitary MRI if indicated) is appropriate.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of this man's osteoporosis and hypogonadism?
options: Testosterone replacement alone will fully treat his osteoporosis
Calcium and vitamin D repletion plus an antiresorptive/osteoporosis agent, and testosterone replacement to correct symptomatic hypogonadism after appropriate evaluation
Observation with repeat DXA in 5 years
Glucocorticoids to reduce inflammation in the spine
stages.explanation: He needs comprehensive treatment: repletion of calcium and vitamin D (his level is insufficient) plus a proven osteoporosis pharmacotherapy (e.g., a bisphosphonate) given that he already has a fragility fracture and osteoporotic-range DXA, which substantially lowers refracture risk; testosterone replacement is indicated to treat his symptomatic hypogonadism and supports bone, but on its own it is not adequate fracture-prevention therapy in a man with established osteoporosis. Observation is unacceptable after a fragility fracture. Glucocorticoids would worsen bone loss and are not indicated; there is no inflammatory spinal process here. Evaluation of the central cause of hypogonadism should accompany treatment.

pearls: A low-trauma vertebral fracture defines osteoporosis and, in a younger man, demands a secondary-cause workup.
Hypogonadism is a common, reversible cause of male osteoporosis; check a morning testosterone with LH and FSH.
Low testosterone with low/normal LH and FSH = secondary (hypogonadotropic) hypogonadism (central origin).
Treat with calcium, vitamin D, and an antiresorptive agent for the fracture risk, plus testosterone for symptomatic hypogonadism.

cases.title: Routine Physical With a Widening Waistline
cases.chiefComplaint: He presents for an annual wellness visit and is worried about his weight.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man comes in for a routine physical. He has no acute complaints but notes his pants have been getting tighter over the past few years despite eating 'the same as always.' He works a desk job, exercises rarely, and drinks two or three beers most evenings. His diet is heavy in fast food and sweetened beverages. He has a remote history of borderline blood pressure but takes no medications. He denies polyuria, polydipsia, chest pain, or claudication. Review of systems is otherwise negative. Past medical history is notable only for a fatty liver mentioned on a prior ultrasound. He takes no prescription medications and only occasional ibuprofen for back pain. He has no known drug allergies. He has smoked half a pack of cigarettes daily for 25 years. Family history is significant for type 2 diabetes in his mother and a myocardial infarction in his father at age 60. He is married and works in accounting.
cases.exam: He is a centrally obese man in no distress. Waist circumference measures 112 cm. BMI is 31 kg/m². Cardiovascular exam reveals a regular rhythm with no murmurs or bruits. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is protuberant and soft with no hepatomegaly appreciated and no tenderness. There is no acanthosis nigricans on the neck. There is no peripheral edema, and distal pulses are intact. Skin shows no xanthomas or xanthelasma. Neurologic exam, including monofilament testing of the feet, is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given his central obesity and elevated blood pressure, which set of screening laboratory tests is most appropriate to order first?
options: 24-hour urinary free cortisol and overnight dexamethasone suppression test
Fasting lipid panel and fasting plasma glucose
Serum insulin level and oral glucose tolerance test with C-peptide
stages.explanation: A fasting lipid panel and fasting glucose directly assess the components of metabolic syndrome (dyslipidemia and impaired fasting glucose) that complete the clustering already suggested by his central obesity and hypertension. Cortisol testing for Cushing syndrome is not warranted without specific features (striae, proximal weakness, easy bruising). A serum insulin level and formal glucose tolerance test with C-peptide are unnecessary and not part of routine metabolic syndrome screening; fasting glucose or A1c suffices. TSH/free T4 would evaluate thyroid dysfunction, which is not suggested by his presentation and would not address the cardiometabolic cluster that is most relevant here.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Fasting plasma glucose: 112 mg/dL
Hemoglobin A1c: 5.9%
Total cholesterol: 224 mg/dL
LDL cholesterol: 138 mg/dL
HDL cholesterol: 34 mg/dL
Triglycerides: 232 mg/dL
ALT: 58 U/L
AST: 41 U/L
TSH: 2.1 mIU/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the criteria of waist circumference 112 cm, BP 146/92 mmHg, fasting glucose 112 mg/dL, triglycerides 232 mg/dL, and HDL 34 mg/dL, how many of the five metabolic syndrome criteria does he meet?
stages.explanation: He meets all five criteria. Central obesity (waist >102 cm in men; his is 112 cm), elevated BP (≥130/85 or treatment; his is 146/92), elevated fasting glucose (≥100 mg/dL; his is 112), elevated triglycerides (≥150 mg/dL; his are 232), and low HDL (<40 mg/dL in men; his is 34) are each satisfied. Metabolic syndrome requires three or more, so 'two,' 'three,' and 'four' all undercount his findings and would mislabel his severity. Recognizing that he satisfies every single criterion underscores his substantial cardiovascular and diabetes risk and the urgency of intervention. The diagnosis is clinical and based on these readily available bedside and laboratory measurements rather than on formal insulin resistance testing such as a clamp study.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which single intervention should be emphasized first as the cornerstone of management for his metabolic syndrome?
options: Initiate metformin to prevent progression to diabetes
Start a statin and a thiazide and recheck in one year
Intensive lifestyle modification with weight loss, diet change, and increased physical activity
