stages.explanation: Intensive lifestyle modification is the foundational therapy for metabolic syndrome; a 7–10% weight loss improves every component simultaneously—glucose, blood pressure, triglycerides, and HDL. Metformin can be considered for diabetes prevention in high-risk patients but is adjunctive, not the cornerstone. Pharmacotherapy for individual components (statin, antihypertensive) is appropriate and will likely be needed, but lifestyle change should be emphasized first and underpins all other treatment. Bariatric surgery is reserved for higher BMI thresholds (≥40, or ≥35 with comorbidity) after lifestyle measures fail; his BMI of 31 does not meet criteria. Counseling should set a realistic initial goal of roughly 5–7% weight loss, reinforced at every visit.

stages.label: RISK ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Beyond cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes, which condition is he at clearly increased risk for given his lab and imaging findings?
options: Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis with progression to cirrhosis
stages.explanation: His central obesity, insulin resistance, and elevated transaminases with a prior fatty liver point to non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, which can progress to non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), fibrosis, and cirrhosis—a recognized complication of metabolic syndrome. Primary hyperaldosteronism causes hypertension with hypokalemia but is not driven by metabolic syndrome. Medullary thyroid carcinoma is associated with MEN2, unrelated here. Pheochromocytoma causes paroxysmal hypertension with palpitations and sweating, not the chronic metabolic clustering seen in this patient. Weight loss and glycemic control are the mainstays of reducing his hepatic risk. A fibrosis assessment (e.g., FIB-4 score or transient elastography) helps stratify which patients with NAFLD need closer hepatology follow-up.

pearls: Metabolic syndrome requires 3 of 5: central obesity, BP ≥130/85, fasting glucose ≥100, triglycerides ≥150, and HDL <40 (men)/<50 (women).
A 7–10% weight loss via lifestyle change improves all components and is the cornerstone of therapy.
Metabolic syndrome markedly raises risk for type 2 diabetes, atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, and NAFLD/NASH.
Treat individual components (statin, antihypertensive) as indicated, but never let pharmacotherapy substitute for lifestyle intervention.

cases.title: A Borderline Sugar on Routine Bloodwork
cases.chiefComplaint: She returns to discuss a slightly elevated blood sugar found on screening labs.
cases.history: A 46-year-old woman presents to discuss results of screening labs drawn at her last visit, which showed a hemoglobin A1c of 6.1%. She feels well and denies polyuria, polydipsia, weight changes, blurred vision, or recurrent infections. She has gained about 12 pounds over the past three years and is mostly sedentary, working from home at a computer. Her diet includes frequent takeout and sweetened coffee drinks. Past medical history includes gestational diabetes during her second pregnancy 10 years ago and mild hypertension. Her only medication is lisinopril 10 mg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol socially. Family history is notable for type 2 diabetes in both her mother and an older sister. She is married with two children and is anxious about 'becoming diabetic like my mom,' who is on insulin. She asks specifically what she can do to avoid that outcome.
cases.exam: She is an overweight woman in no distress. BMI is 29 kg/m². Waist circumference is 92 cm. There is faint acanthosis nigricans at the posterior neck and in both axillae. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams are normal. The abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly. There is no peripheral edema. Foot exam shows intact pulses, normal monofilament sensation, and no ulcers or deformities. The remainder of the exam, including fundoscopic and thyroid exams, is unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on a hemoglobin A1c of 6.1%, which classification best fits this patient?
stages.explanation: An A1c of 5.7–6.4% defines prediabetes, so 6.1% places her in this category. Normal is <5.7%, so 'normal' is incorrect. Diabetes requires A1c ≥6.5% (or fasting glucose ≥126, 2-hour OGTT ≥200, or random ≥200 with symptoms), which she does not meet, making 'type 2 diabetes' incorrect. Latent autoimmune diabetes of adults is a slowly progressive autoimmune (type 1-like) diabetes typically seen in leaner adults with islet autoantibodies; nothing here suggests autoimmunity, and her risk profile (obesity, family history, prior gestational diabetes) is classic for the insulin-resistant prediabetes/type 2 pathway. Confirming the category matters because it shifts management from routine screening to active risk-reduction counseling and closer surveillance.

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most effective intervention to reduce her risk of progressing to type 2 diabetes?
options: Structured lifestyle program targeting 7% weight loss and 150 minutes/week of exercise
stages.explanation: The Diabetes Prevention Program showed intensive lifestyle intervention (7% weight loss, 150 minutes/week of moderate activity) reduced progression to diabetes by 58%—more effective than metformin (31%)—and is first-line for prediabetes. Basal insulin is never used to treat prediabetes; it would risk hypoglycemia and is not indicated. Sulfonylureas cause weight gain and hypoglycemia and are not used for prevention. Acarbose modestly reduces progression but causes significant GI side effects and is not first-line; lifestyle change remains the most effective and safest intervention for her. Pairing dietary change with regular activity also improves blood pressure and lipids, addressing her broader cardiometabolic risk, not just glycemia.

stages.label: PHARMACOLOGY
stages.question: She is motivated but also has several risk factors. Which feature most strongly supports adding metformin for diabetes prevention?
options: Her BMI ≥35, age <60, or prior gestational diabetes
stages.explanation: Guidelines recommend considering metformin for prediabetes especially in those with BMI ≥35, age <60 years, or a history of gestational diabetes—she has the latter two, making her a strong candidate. Hypertension is a cardiovascular risk factor but is not a specific trigger for metformin in diabetes prevention. Age over 45 is the threshold for screening, not specifically for metformin. Acanthosis nigricans signals insulin resistance but by itself is not a guideline criterion for initiating preventive metformin; the validated factors are the markers of higher progression risk listed above. Metformin is generally well tolerated, weight-neutral to weight-favorable, and does not cause hypoglycemia, making it a reasonable adjunct to lifestyle change in her.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP LABS
stages.content:
Repeat hemoglobin A1c (3 months later): 6.0%
Fasting plasma glucose: 108 mg/dL
LDL cholesterol: 118 mg/dL
ALT: 26 U/L
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio: 8 mg/g

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: After starting lifestyle changes and metformin, how should she be monitored for progression to diabetes?
options: Repeat A1c (or fasting glucose) at least annually
No further glucose testing is needed while on metformin
stages.explanation: Patients with prediabetes should have glycemic status reassessed at least yearly with A1c or fasting glucose to detect progression to diabetes promptly. Testing only every 5 years is the interval for low-risk patients with normal glucose, not for someone with established prediabetes. Saying no testing is needed on metformin is wrong—metformin reduces but does not eliminate progression risk, and ongoing surveillance is essential. C-peptide measures endogenous insulin secretion and is used to characterize diabetes type, not to monitor prediabetes progression in this insulin-resistant patient. She should also have cardiovascular risk factors such as blood pressure and lipids reviewed at these visits, since prediabetes raises overall cardiometabolic risk.

pearls: Prediabetes is defined by A1c 5.7–6.4%, fasting glucose 100–125 mg/dL, or 2-hour OGTT 140–199 mg/dL.
Intensive lifestyle change (7% weight loss, 150 min/week activity) is the most effective prevention—superior to metformin in the DPP.
Consider metformin for prediabetes in patients with BMI ≥35, age <60, or prior gestational diabetes.
Reassess glycemic status at least annually in patients with prediabetes.

cases.title: Bumps Over the Knuckles and Heels
cases.chiefComplaint: He presents with firm nodules over his hands and Achilles tendons.
cases.history: A 35-year-old man presents after noticing firm, painless lumps over the backs of his hands and on both Achilles tendons over the past year. He is otherwise asymptomatic with no chest pain or exertional symptoms. He is physically active and not overweight. He eats a reasonable diet and does not smoke or drink heavily. Past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. He has no known drug allergies. He sought care largely because of his family history: his father died of a heart attack at age 45, a paternal uncle had bypass surgery in his late 40s, and his older brother was recently told he has 'sky-high cholesterol.' He has never had his own cholesterol checked. He works as a software engineer, exercises three times weekly, and is married with one young child. He is concerned about his cardiac risk given the strong family pattern of premature heart disease.
cases.exam: He is a fit, well-appearing man in no distress. BMI is 24 kg/m². There are firm, nontender nodular thickenings over the extensor tendons of both hands and palpable nodularity of both Achilles tendons. A grayish arc is visible at the corneal periphery of both eyes. There are subtle yellowish deposits at the medial canthi of the eyelids. Cardiovascular exam reveals a regular rhythm with no murmurs, rubs, or bruits. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft and nontender. Peripheral pulses are full and symmetric.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The tendon nodules, corneal arcus in a young man, and eyelid deposits most strongly suggest which underlying problem? What test should you order first?
options: Serum uric acid for tophaceous gout
Fasting lipid panel for a severe inherited dyslipidemia
Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP for rheumatoid nodules
Serum calcium and phosphate for calcinosis
stages.explanation: Tendon xanthomas (Achilles and extensor tendons), corneal arcus before age 45, and xanthelasma in a young patient are hallmarks of familial hypercholesterolemia, so a fasting lipid panel is the essential first test. Gouty tophi are typically asymmetric, can be tender, and favor first MTP and ears—uric acid would not explain the corneal and eyelid findings. Rheumatoid nodules occur near pressure points and come with synovitis, absent here. Calcinosis from calcium/phosphate disturbance is not consistent with the symmetric tendon xanthomas and lipid-laden eyelid deposits, which point directly to a profound LDL elevation.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Total cholesterol: 392 mg/dL
LDL cholesterol: 318 mg/dL
HDL cholesterol: 44 mg/dL
Triglycerides: 118 mg/dL
Fasting glucose: 88 mg/dL
TSH: 1.8 mIU/L
ALT: 24 U/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His LDL is 318 mg/dL with tendon xanthomas and premature coronary disease in first-degree relatives. Which diagnosis is most likely?
stages.explanation: An untreated LDL >190 mg/dL (here 318) with tendon xanthomas and premature coronary disease in first-degree relatives is classic for heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia, often diagnosed using clinical criteria such as the Dutch Lipid Network score. Familial combined hyperlipidemia typically shows elevated LDL and triglycerides with variable phenotype and lacks tendon xanthomas. Polygenic hypercholesterolemia produces more modest LDL elevations and no xanthomas. Familial dysbetalipoproteinemia causes palmar and tuberoeruptive xanthomas with roughly equal cholesterol and triglyceride elevations (his triglycerides are normal), not the isolated severe LDL elevation with tendon xanthomas seen here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic therapy for his familial hypercholesterolemia?
options: High-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg)
stages.explanation: High-intensity statin therapy is first-line for familial hypercholesterolemia, with ezetimibe and PCSK9 inhibitors added as needed to reach aggressive LDL goals (often >50% reduction, LDL <100 or <70 mg/dL with risk factors). Fenofibrate primarily lowers triglycerides and raises HDL; it does little for the markedly elevated LDL driving his risk. Omega-3 fatty acids target triglycerides, not LDL, and are inappropriate as primary therapy here. Niacin is no longer recommended for routine LDL management given lack of outcome benefit and significant side effects; it would not adequately treat his severe hypercholesterolemia.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond treating him, what is an essential next step in managing familial hypercholesterolemia?
options: Cascade screening of his first-degree relatives, including his young child
Annual abdominal ultrasound to monitor xanthoma size
Empiric anticoagulation to prevent coronary events
Restricting all dietary fat to under 5% of calories
stages.explanation: Familial hypercholesterolemia is autosomal dominant, so cascade screening of first-degree relatives—siblings, parents, and children—is essential to identify and treat affected family members early, including initiating statins in childhood when indicated. Abdominal ultrasound has no role in monitoring this condition. Empiric anticoagulation does not address atherosclerotic risk in FH; lipid lowering and standard preventive measures do. Extreme fat restriction below 5% of calories is neither evidence-based nor sustainable; a heart-healthy diet is advised, but the LDL elevation is genetically driven and requires pharmacotherapy rather than diet alone. Early identification and statin treatment of affected relatives dramatically reduces their lifetime risk of premature atherosclerotic events.

pearls: Suspect familial hypercholesterolemia with untreated LDL >190 mg/dL, tendon xanthomas, corneal arcus before age 45, or premature CAD in relatives.
High-intensity statins are first-line; add ezetimibe and PCSK9 inhibitors to reach aggressive LDL targets.
It is autosomal dominant—perform cascade screening of first-degree relatives, including children.
Tendon xanthomas (Achilles, extensor tendons) are the most specific physical finding for FH.

cases.title: Severe Belly Pain After a Weekend of Drinking
cases.chiefComplaint: He presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to his back.
cases.history: A 42-year-old man arrives at the emergency department with 10 hours of severe, constant epigastric pain radiating to his back, associated with nausea and several episodes of vomiting. The pain worsens when lying flat and improves slightly when he leans forward. He admits to a weekend of heavy drinking and a large, fatty meal the night before. He has had milder, self-limited episodes of similar pain over the past year. Past medical history includes obesity, poorly controlled type 2 diabetes, and 'high cholesterol' for which he stopped his medications months ago. His only intermittently taken medication is metformin. He reports no drug allergies. He drinks alcohol heavily on weekends and does not smoke. Family history includes a brother with very high triglycerides. He works in construction. He denies hematemesis, melena, fever, or jaundice but notes his urine has been dark.
cases.exam: He is an obese man in moderate distress, diaphoretic and lying still. The abdomen is distended with marked epigastric tenderness and voluntary guarding; bowel sounds are diminished. There is no rebound or rigidity. There are eruptive yellow papules over the extensor surfaces of both elbows and the buttocks. No scleral icterus is appreciated. There is no flank ecchymosis. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia with a regular rhythm. Lungs are clear. Extremities are warm with intact pulses. A drawn blood sample appears grossly milky.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given epigastric pain radiating to the back, vomiting, and a milky-appearing blood sample, which initial test will most directly confirm the suspected acute process?
options: Abdominal CT with contrast as the first study
stages.explanation: His presentation is classic for acute pancreatitis, and serum lipase (≥3× the upper limit of normal) is the most sensitive and specific initial confirmatory test. CT is not the first-line diagnostic study in early pancreatitis and is reserved for uncertain diagnoses or to assess complications after 48–72 hours; obtaining it first delays care and adds contrast risk. RUQ ultrasound is useful to evaluate for gallstones but does not confirm pancreatitis itself. Troponin would assess myocardial injury, which is not the leading concern given the classic pancreatic presentation and lipemic serum suggesting hypertriglyceridemia.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Lipase: 1,820 U/L
Triglycerides: 3,240 mg/dL
Glucose: 318 mg/dL
Calcium: 8.2 mg/dL
Sodium: 128 mEq/L
Anion gap: 14 mEq/L
Lactate: 2.1 mmol/L
Lipid panel notable for grossly lipemic serum
Right upper quadrant ultrasound: no gallstones; pancreas obscured by bowel gas

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With lipase >3× normal and triglycerides of 3,240 mg/dL, what is the most likely cause of his acute pancreatitis?
stages.explanation: Triglycerides above 1,000 mg/dL—here 3,240—are a well-established cause of acute pancreatitis, and the lipemic serum and eruptive xanthomas confirm severe hypertriglyceridemia as the driver. Gallstone pancreatitis is excluded by an ultrasound showing no stones and a normal duct. Alcoholic ketoacidosis can occur in heavy drinkers but presents with an elevated anion gap and ketosis rather than lipase >3× normal with this triglyceride level, and it would not explain the markedly elevated lipase. A perforated ulcer would typically show free air and a rigid, diffusely tender abdomen, not the lipemic serum and triglyceride elevation seen here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond aggressive IV fluids and analgesia, which therapy is most appropriate to lower his markedly elevated triglycerides acutely given his hyperglycemia?
options: Insulin infusion (with glucose monitoring), with apheresis considered for severe or refractory cases
stages.explanation: An IV insulin infusion activates lipoprotein lipase and rapidly lowers triglycerides, and it conveniently also treats his hyperglycemia; dextrose is co-administered to prevent hypoglycemia when glucose normalizes. Plasmapheresis is reserved for severe presentations (e.g., very high triglycerides with organ dysfunction or lactic acidosis) or refractory cases. Oral fenofibrate is part of long-term management but works too slowly for the acute setting. High-dose niacin is not used acutely and has fallen out of favor. Oral omega-3 fatty acids lower triglycerides modestly over weeks and have no role in acute stabilization of life-threatening hypertriglyceridemia.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After his pancreatitis resolves, what is the most important long-term measure to prevent recurrence?
options: Sustained triglyceride control with a fibrate, dietary fat restriction, alcohol cessation, and glycemic control
stages.explanation: Preventing recurrent hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis hinges on durable triglyceride lowering: a fibrate (often first-line), strict reduction of dietary fat and refined carbohydrates, complete alcohol abstinence, and tight glucose control, since uncontrolled diabetes worsens triglycerides. A proton pump inhibitor treats acid-related disease, not hypertriglyceridemia. Cholecystectomy prevents gallstone pancreatitis, but his ultrasound showed no stones, so it is not indicated. Chronic antibiotics have no role in preventing this metabolic form of pancreatitis and would only promote resistance. Addressing the underlying lipid and metabolic derangements is the key to preventing future attacks. Repeat fasting triglyceride monitoring confirms that levels stay well below the threshold associated with pancreatitis risk.

pearls: Triglycerides >1,000 mg/dL can precipitate acute pancreatitis; suspect it when serum appears lipemic.
Diagnose acute pancreatitis with lipase ≥3× the upper limit of normal plus a compatible clinical picture—CT is not the first test.
Acute management of hypertriglyceridemic pancreatitis uses IV insulin (activates lipoprotein lipase) ± apheresis for severe cases.
Long-term prevention requires fibrates, fat/alcohol restriction, and glycemic control; eruptive xanthomas signal severe hypertriglyceridemia.

cases.title: Struggling to Lose Weight Despite Trying
cases.chiefComplaint: She seeks help with weight loss after years of unsuccessful dieting.
cases.history: A 38-year-old woman presents requesting help with weight management. She has struggled with her weight since her twenties and has tried multiple commercial diets and exercise programs, achieving only transient results before regaining the weight. She is frustrated and increasingly limited by knee pain and snoring with daytime fatigue. Past medical history includes hypertension, obstructive sleep apnea diagnosed last year (she uses CPAP nightly), and dyslipidemia. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily. She has no known drug allergies and no personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2. She does not smoke, drinks rarely, and has no history of pancreatitis. She has no history of gallstones. She is not pregnant, uses an IUD, and is not planning pregnancy. Family history includes obesity and type 2 diabetes in her mother. She works as a teacher and feels her weight is affecting her health and quality of life.
cases.exam: She is a well-appearing woman with central and generalized obesity. BMI is 38 kg/m². Waist circumference is 108 cm. The thyroid is normal in size without nodules. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams are normal. The abdomen is obese, soft, and nontender. There is mild crepitus and tenderness of both knees with full range of motion. There is no acanthosis nigricans, no proximal muscle weakness, and no purple striae. Distal pulses and sensation are intact. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: With a BMI of 38 and weight-related comorbidities, what is the appropriate first step before considering pharmacotherapy?
options: Confirm she has engaged in comprehensive lifestyle intervention (diet, activity, behavior) as the foundation of treatment
Order a brain MRI to exclude a hypothalamic tumor
Begin a very-low-calorie ketogenic diet without follow-up
stages.explanation: Comprehensive lifestyle intervention—reduced-calorie diet, increased physical activity, and behavioral support—is the foundation of obesity treatment and should accompany (not be replaced by) any pharmacotherapy. She has already attempted diets, but structured, sustained lifestyle change remains essential and is the appropriate baseline. Bariatric surgery is an option for her BMI with comorbidities but is not the mandatory first step before trying medication plus lifestyle measures. A brain MRI is unwarranted without signs of hypothalamic disease. An unsupervised very-low-calorie ketogenic diet without monitoring is not an evidence-based or safe foundational approach for long-term obesity management.

stages.label: INDICATION
stages.question: Does she meet criteria for anti-obesity pharmacotherapy?
options: No, medication requires a BMI ≥40 regardless of comorbidities
Yes, her BMI ≥30 (and ≥27 with comorbidities) qualifies her for pharmacotherapy
No, pharmacotherapy is only for patients who have failed bariatric surgery
Yes, but only after a 12-month supervised fast
stages.explanation: Anti-obesity pharmacotherapy is indicated for a BMI ≥30, or ≥27 with at least one weight-related comorbidity; her BMI of 38 with hypertension, OSA, and dyslipidemia clearly qualifies. A threshold of ≥40 is one criterion for bariatric surgery, not a requirement for medication. Pharmacotherapy is not restricted to post-surgical patients; it is a standard adjunct to lifestyle change. A 12-month supervised fast is neither safe nor a recognized prerequisite. Combining lifestyle intervention with appropriate pharmacotherapy is consistent with current obesity management guidelines for a patient like her. Documenting her qualifying comorbidities also supports insurance authorization for anti-obesity pharmacotherapy when cost is a barrier.

stages.label: PHARMACOLOGY
stages.question: Considering her comorbidities and the goal of substantial weight loss, which agent is a strong first choice?
options: A GLP-1 receptor agonist such as semaglutide (or GLP-1/GIP agonist tirzepatide)
Sympathomimetic appetite suppressants combined with her CPAP
stages.explanation: GLP-1 receptor agonists such as semaglutide (and the dual GLP-1/GIP agonist tirzepatide) produce substantial, durable weight loss and offer cardiometabolic benefits, making them excellent choices for a patient with obesity and comorbidities. Orlistat yields modest weight loss with frequent GI side effects and poor adherence. Phentermine monotherapy is approved only for short-term use and raises blood pressure and heart rate—undesirable given her hypertension. Generic 'sympathomimetic appetite suppressants' similarly carry cardiovascular risk and are not preferred. Given her risk profile and need for meaningful weight reduction, an incretin-based agent is the strongest option.

stages.label: BASELINE LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin A1c: 5.9%
Fasting glucose: 104 mg/dL
TSH: 1.6 mIU/L
LDL cholesterol: 128 mg/dL
Triglycerides: 168 mg/dL
ALT: 30 U/L
Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL
Urine pregnancy test: negative

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Before starting a GLP-1 receptor agonist, which point is most important to review with her?
options: It must be stopped permanently if any nausea occurs
Common GI side effects, the need to screen for personal/family history of medullary thyroid cancer or MEN2, and contraindication in pregnancy
It eliminates the need for any dietary changes
It should be combined with phentermine in all patients for best results
stages.explanation: Counseling should cover the common dose-related GI side effects (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) that usually improve with gradual titration, the boxed contraindication in those with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2, and the need to avoid pregnancy while on therapy (she uses an IUD, which is reassuring). Nausea is common and typically managed with titration, not automatic permanent discontinuation. GLP-1 agonists augment but do not replace dietary and lifestyle changes. Routine combination with phentermine is not standard and adds cardiovascular risk, so it should not be presented as universally recommended.

cases.diagnosis: Obesity (class II) managed with GLP-1 receptor agonist pharmacotherapy
pearls: Pharmacotherapy for obesity is indicated at BMI ≥30, or ≥27 with a weight-related comorbidity, always alongside lifestyle change.
GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide) and GLP-1/GIP agonists (tirzepatide) produce the greatest weight loss among current agents.
GLP-1 agonists are contraindicated with personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2 and in pregnancy.
Avoid long-term phentermine and sympathomimetics in patients with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

cases.title: Found Confused and Sweaty at Home
cases.chiefComplaint: She is brought in confused, diaphoretic, and difficult to arouse.
cases.history: A 75-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her daughter, who found her confused, sweaty, and difficult to wake this morning. She had eaten poorly the day before because of a stomach bug and skipped dinner but took all her usual medications. Her daughter reports she has seemed 'off' for several hours. Past medical history includes long-standing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.6 mg/dL). Her medications include glipizide 10 mg twice daily, lisinopril, and amlodipine; she was recently switched to a higher glipizide dose by an outside clinic. She has no known drug allergies. She does not drink alcohol or smoke. She lives alone and her daughter helps with medications. On arrival, a fingerstick glucose reads 32 mg/dL. There is no report of insulin use, and no other household members take glucose-lowering drugs.
cases.exam: She is an elderly woman who is diaphoretic, tremulous, and confused but arousable to voice, oriented only to self. She has no focal neurologic deficits and moves all extremities; pupils are equal and reactive. There is no neck stiffness. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia with a regular rhythm. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft and nontender. Skin is cool and clammy. There is no tongue laceration and no evidence of trauma. After initial treatment her mental status improves transiently.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her fingerstick glucose is 32 mg/dL with altered mental status. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Obtain a CT head before any treatment
Give insulin to stabilize glucose metabolism
Order a sulfonylurea level and wait for results before treating
stages.explanation: Symptomatic hypoglycemia with a glucose of 32 mg/dL is a medical emergency; immediate IV dextrose (or IM glucagon if no access) is required to restore glucose and reverse neuroglycopenia. Delaying treatment for a CT head risks irreversible brain injury—imaging can follow once glucose is corrected if deficits persist. Insulin would worsen the hypoglycemia and is dangerous. Waiting for a sulfonylurea drug level before treating is inappropriate; the diagnosis is made at the bedside by the low glucose and symptoms, and treatment must not be delayed for confirmatory testing. A confirmatory venous or laboratory glucose can be sent simultaneously, but treatment proceeds immediately on the bedside value.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given her medications and presentation, what is the most likely cause of her hypoglycemia?
options: Sulfonylurea (glipizide)-induced hypoglycemia worsened by poor intake and renal impairment
stages.explanation: Glipizide is a sulfonylurea that stimulates endogenous insulin secretion; in an elderly woman with reduced oral intake and chronic kidney disease (which impairs drug clearance), a recently increased dose readily produces prolonged hypoglycemia. Insulinoma causes fasting hypoglycemia but is far less likely than an obvious offending drug in this context. Factitious insulin use would show high insulin with low C-peptide and is not suggested here. Adrenal insufficiency can cause hypoglycemia but usually with hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypotension; her clear medication trigger and risk factors point directly to sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia. Recognizing the drug as the culprit is critical because it dictates prolonged observation rather than a single corrective bolus.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Repeat glucose (after D50): 96 mg/dL
Glucose 45 minutes later: 41 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1.9 mg/dL (baseline 1.6)
Potassium: 4.4 mEq/L
C-peptide: elevated
Insulin: elevated
Sulfonylurea screen: positive for glipizide
Cortisol (random): 18 µg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Despite repeated dextrose boluses and a continuous D10 infusion, she keeps dropping below 50 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate next therapy?
options: Switch to subcutaneous insulin sliding scale
Give a single large bolus of D50 and discharge once normoglycemic
Start hydrocortisone for presumed adrenal crisis
stages.explanation: Octreotide suppresses pancreatic insulin release and is the targeted therapy for refractory sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia, breaking the cycle in which dextrose itself stimulates further insulin secretion. Subcutaneous insulin would worsen her hypoglycemia and is contraindicated. A single dextrose bolus followed by discharge is unsafe—sulfonylurea hypoglycemia is prolonged (especially with renal impairment), and these patients require extended observation, often 24 hours or more. Hydrocortisone is unnecessary given her normal random cortisol and normal potassium, which argue against adrenal crisis; the clear cause is the sulfonylurea. Octreotide is typically dosed subcutaneously every several hours and markedly reduces the dextrose requirement and recurrent hypoglycemic episodes.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate disposition after she is stabilized on octreotide and dextrose?
options: Discharge home from the ED once a single glucose is normal
Admit for at least 24 hours of monitoring with frequent glucose checks and medication review
Discharge with a prescription for a higher glipizide dose
Transfer directly to a psychiatric facility
stages.explanation: Sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia, particularly with glipizide and renal impairment, can recur for many hours, so admission for at least 24 hours with frequent glucose monitoring and a careful medication reconciliation is required; the offending agent should be discontinued or dose-reduced and her regimen reassessed. Discharging after one normal glucose risks fatal rebound hypoglycemia. Increasing the glipizide dose would be dangerous and is the opposite of correct management. Psychiatric transfer is irrelevant—there is no evidence this was intentional, and her problem is a pharmacologic and renal one requiring medical monitoring. On discharge, simplifying her regimen toward agents with lower hypoglycemia risk and educating the family on sick-day rules help prevent recurrence.

pearls: Treat symptomatic hypoglycemia immediately with IV dextrose—never delay for imaging or confirmatory labs.
Sulfonylurea hypoglycemia is prolonged and recurrent, especially in the elderly and in renal impairment; observe ≥24 hours.
Octreotide suppresses insulin secretion and is the targeted therapy for refractory sulfonylurea hypoglycemia.
High insulin with high C-peptide points to endogenous insulin secretion (sulfonylurea or insulinoma); a positive sulfonylurea screen confirms the drug.

cases.title: Nausea and Rapid Breathing on a New Diabetes Pill
cases.chiefComplaint: He presents with nausea, malaise, and rapid breathing.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man presents to the emergency department with two days of nausea, vomiting, abdominal discomfort, and progressive fatigue. He notes he has been breathing harder than usual. He has type 2 diabetes and was started on empagliflozin three weeks ago for better glucose control. Over the past several days he has eaten very little due to a viral illness and reduced his usual insulin because he 'wasn't eating.' Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes (on metformin and basal insulin), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily, insulin glargine, empagliflozin 10 mg daily, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol occasionally and does not smoke. Family history is notable for type 2 diabetes. He works as an electrician. He denies chest pain, fever, dysuria, or recent surgery but feels increasingly unwell and short of breath.
cases.exam: He is an ill-appearing man with deep, rapid (Kussmaul) respirations. He is alert and oriented. His mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is reduced, suggesting volume depletion. There is mild diffuse abdominal tenderness without guarding or rebound. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia with a regular rhythm. Lungs are clear to auscultation. There is a faint fruity odor to his breath. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. There is no flank tenderness and no peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has nausea, Kussmaul breathing, and a fruity breath odor. What is the most appropriate initial set of tests?
options: Venous blood gas, basic metabolic panel, serum and/or urine ketones, and glucose
stages.explanation: His clinical picture suggests diabetic ketoacidosis, so the priority is to assess acid-base status (venous blood gas), the metabolic panel for the anion gap and electrolytes, ketones, and glucose. A chest X-ray and D-dimer evaluate pulmonary embolism, which does not explain ketotic breath and Kussmaul respirations. Lipase and ultrasound investigate pancreatitis—reasonable later given abdominal symptoms, but they will not diagnose the ketoacidosis driving his presentation. A lumbar puncture is unwarranted without meningitic features. The combination of gas, chemistry, ketones, and glucose efficiently identifies the metabolic emergency at hand. Checking beta-hydroxybutyrate directly, rather than relying on urine ketones alone, gives the most accurate measure of his ketotic burden.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Glucose: 188 mg/dL
Sodium: 134 mEq/L
Potassium: 5.1 mEq/L
Chloride: 99 mEq/L
Bicarbonate: 12 mEq/L
Anion gap: 23 mEq/L
Venous pH: 7.21
Beta-hydroxybutyrate: 5.8 mmol/L
Lactate: 1.4 mmol/L
Creatinine: 1.3 mg/dL
Urinalysis: glucose 3+, ketones large

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: He has a high anion-gap metabolic acidosis with large ketones but a glucose of only 188 mg/dL while on empagliflozin. What is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: An anion-gap metabolic acidosis with markedly elevated beta-hydroxybutyrate and a near-normal or only mildly elevated glucose, in a patient on an SGLT2 inhibitor, defines euglycemic DKA—the drug's urinary glucose loss masks the expected hyperglycemia. Lactic acidosis is excluded by the normal lactate. Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state features very high glucose (often >600) with minimal ketosis, the opposite of this picture. Alcoholic ketoacidosis can produce ketosis but typically with low or normal glucose after binge drinking and poor intake; his clear SGLT2 inhibitor exposure, reduced insulin, and intercurrent illness make euglycemic DKA the precise diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for his euglycemic DKA, given his relatively normal glucose?
options: IV insulin alone without dextrose until glucose falls
IV fluids plus IV insulin co-administered with dextrose, with potassium repletion as needed, and stopping the SGLT2 inhibitor
IV bicarbonate as the primary therapy
stages.explanation: Euglycemic DKA still requires insulin to halt ketogenesis, but because glucose is already near-normal, dextrose must be co-administered with insulin from the start to allow continued insulin infusion without causing hypoglycemia, alongside IV fluids and potassium monitoring/repletion; the SGLT2 inhibitor must be discontinued. Insulin without dextrose would precipitate hypoglycemia before the acidosis clears. Bicarbonate is reserved for severe acidemia (pH <6.9) and is not primary therapy. Oral hydration and observation are inadequate for an anion-gap acidosis with large ketones—this is a true ketoacidosis requiring IV insulin and fluids. The insulin infusion is continued until the anion gap closes and beta-hydroxybutyrate normalizes, not merely until glucose falls.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two hours into the insulin infusion, his repeat potassium is 3.2 mEq/L. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Continue insulin unchanged and recheck in 6 hours
Hold/reduce insulin and aggressively replete potassium before resuming full insulin dosing
Give IV bicarbonate to shift potassium
Administer additional insulin to drive glucose lower
stages.explanation: Insulin drives potassium intracellularly, and a potassium of 3.2 mEq/L is dangerously low; insulin should be held or reduced and potassium aggressively repleted before resuming full insulin, since continued insulin can precipitate life-threatening hypokalemia and arrhythmia. Continuing insulin unchanged and waiting six hours risks worsening hypokalemia. Bicarbonate further lowers serum potassium by shifting it intracellularly and would aggravate the problem. Giving more insulin to lower glucose ignores the immediate threat from hypokalemia. Standard DKA protocols require holding insulin when potassium falls below ~3.3 mEq/L until it is corrected. Frequent potassium monitoring during the infusion is essential, since rapid intracellular shifts can occur even when total body potassium is depleted.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: As he recovers, what counseling will best prevent recurrence of SGLT2 inhibitor-associated euglycemic DKA?
options: Resume the SGLT2 inhibitor immediately at a higher dose
Hold the SGLT2 inhibitor during acute illness, surgery, or fasting ('sick-day' rules) and never inappropriately reduce insulin
stages.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors should be held during acute illness, perioperative periods, prolonged fasting, or marked reduction in carbohydrate intake—the 'sick-day' rules—because these states promote ketogenesis that the drug can unmask; patients must also avoid inappropriately stopping insulin. Permanently stopping all insulin would worsen his diabetes and ketosis. Restarting the SGLT2 inhibitor at a higher dose immediately ignores the precipitating risk and could trigger recurrence. Avoiding all carbohydrates indefinitely is itself a ketogenic stress and would increase, not decrease, his risk of euglycemic DKA. Patients should also be taught to monitor ketones when ill, recognize early warning symptoms, and seek care promptly rather than self-adjusting insulin.

pearls: Euglycemic DKA = anion-gap acidosis with ketosis and glucose <250 mg/dL; suspect it in any unwell patient on an SGLT2 inhibitor.
Treat with IV fluids and insulin PLUS dextrose from the start (glucose is already low), correct potassium, and stop the SGLT2 inhibitor.
Hold insulin if potassium falls below ~3.3 mEq/L; insulin shifts potassium intracellularly.
Prevent recurrence with SGLT2 inhibitor 'sick-day' rules—hold during illness, fasting, or surgery, and never inappropriately reduce insulin.

cases.title: Sudden Double Vision and a Drooping Eyelid
cases.chiefComplaint: He presents with acute double vision and a drooping right eyelid.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man presents to the emergency department with the sudden onset this morning of double vision and a drooping right eyelid, accompanied by a dull ache around the right eye. He denies headache of maximal intensity, jaw or scalp symptoms, weakness, slurred speech, or limb numbness. Past medical history includes long-standing, poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (last A1c 9.4%), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Medications are metformin, glipizide, lisinopril, and atorvastatin; he admits to inconsistent adherence. He has no known drug allergies. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history includes diabetes and stroke. He works as a long-haul truck driver and is anxious that he is having a stroke. He reports the double vision resolves when he covers either eye. There has been no recent trauma, fever, or weight loss.
cases.exam: He is alert and oriented in no acute distress. The right eye is deviated 'down and out' at rest with ptosis of the right upper lid. The right pupil is 3 mm and briskly reactive to light, equal to the left; there is no anisocoria. Extraocular movements show impaired adduction, elevation, and depression of the right eye, with intact abduction. Visual acuity and fundoscopic exam are normal bilaterally; there is no papilledema. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam is normal. Motor strength, sensation, coordination, and gait are intact. There is no neck rigidity and no temporal artery tenderness.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: His right eye is 'down and out' with ptosis and impaired adduction, elevation, and depression. Which cranial nerve is affected?
stages.explanation: The oculomotor nerve (CN III) innervates the levator palpebrae (lid), the superior/inferior/medial recti, and the inferior oblique; its palsy causes ptosis with the eye resting 'down and out' and impaired adduction, elevation, and depression—exactly his findings. CN IV palsy causes vertical diplopia with a hypertropic eye, not ptosis or 'down and out' deviation. CN VI palsy causes isolated impaired abduction with the eye deviated inward, the opposite of his preserved abduction. CN VII controls facial muscles and would cause facial droop, not extraocular or lid abnormalities. His preserved abduction and intorsion on attempted downgaze localize the deficit cleanly to CN III rather than a combined ophthalmoplegia.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His pupil is spared (3 mm, briskly reactive) despite the CN III palsy, and he has poorly controlled diabetes. What is the most likely cause?
options: Posterior communicating artery aneurysm compressing CN III
stages.explanation: Pupil-sparing CN III palsy in a patient with vascular risk factors (poorly controlled diabetes, hypertension, smoking) is the classic picture of an ischemic microvascular third nerve palsy; the pupillomotor fibers run peripherally and are relatively spared by ischemia of the nerve's core. A posterior communicating artery aneurysm compresses the peripheral pupillary fibers first, producing a dilated, poorly reactive pupil—pupil involvement, which is absent here. Uncal herniation causes a blown pupil with depressed consciousness, not an isolated palsy in an alert patient. Myasthenia gravis causes fatigable ptosis and diplopia but never affects the pupil and lacks a fixed 'down and out' eye.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Although the picture suggests a microvascular palsy, what is the most important immediate step to avoid missing a dangerous mimic?
options: Reassure and discharge without imaging since the pupil is spared
Neuroimaging including vascular imaging (CTA or MRA) to exclude an aneurysm
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation for presumed stroke
stages.explanation: Even with pupil sparing, a posterior communicating artery aneurysm can occasionally present without full pupil involvement, so vascular imaging (CT angiography or MR angiography) is the prudent step to exclude an aneurysm that could rupture catastrophically. Discharging without imaging risks missing a life-threatening aneurysm. Empiric high-dose steroids are not indicated for an ischemic palsy and would worsen his diabetes. Therapeutic anticoagulation is inappropriate—this is not an embolic stroke, and anticoagulation could be dangerous if an aneurysm were present. Confirming a benign microvascular cause requires first ruling out the compressive mimic.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head without contrast: no hemorrhage, no mass
CT angiography head and neck: no aneurysm, no large-vessel occlusion
MRI brain: no acute infarct; no brainstem lesion
ESR: 12 mm/hr
CRP: 0.4 mg/dL
A1c: 9.6%

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging excludes aneurysm, stroke, and giant cell arteritis. What is the appropriate management of his ischemic diabetic third nerve palsy?
options: Supportive care with risk-factor control (glycemic, blood pressure, lipid management) and expectant observation for spontaneous recovery
stages.explanation: Ischemic microvascular third nerve palsies typically resolve spontaneously over weeks to a few months; management is supportive—optimizing glucose, blood pressure, and lipids to reduce future vascular events, with symptomatic measures such as an eye patch or prism for diplopia and close follow-up to confirm recovery. Neurosurgical clipping treats aneurysms, which were excluded. Lifelong corticosteroids have no role and would harm his diabetes. Plasma exchange treats conditions like myasthenic crisis or certain autoimmune neuropathies, not an ischemic mononeuropathy. The expected natural history is favorable once dangerous causes are ruled out and risk factors are addressed.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: You counsel him on supportive care. Which development during follow-up should prompt urgent re-evaluation rather than continued observation?
options: Gradual improvement in his diplopia over several weeks
New pupillary dilation with poor light reactivity, or failure to improve by about 3 months
Persistent mild ptosis that is slowly lessening
Transient blurring relieved by closing one eye
stages.explanation: New pupillary involvement (a dilating, poorly reactive pupil) or failure of the palsy to improve within roughly three months is a red flag that argues against a benign microvascular cause and mandates urgent re-imaging to exclude a compressive lesion such as an aneurysm or tumor. Gradual improvement in diplopia over weeks is the expected, reassuring course. Slowly lessening ptosis likewise reflects normal recovery and needs no escalation. Transient blurring relieved by covering one eye simply reflects the binocular diplopia of his palsy and is not alarming. Tracking the pupil and the recovery timeline is the key safety net during observation, and he should also be counseled not to drive until his diplopia resolves.

cases.diagnosis: Ischemic (microvascular) diabetic third nerve palsy, pupil-sparing
pearls: CN III palsy = 'down and out' eye with ptosis and impaired adduction/elevation/depression; abduction is preserved.
Pupil-SPARING CN III palsy with vascular risk factors suggests an ischemic microvascular cause; pupil-INVOLVING palsy raises concern for a compressive aneurysm.
Obtain vascular imaging (CTA/MRA) even with pupil sparing—posterior communicating artery aneurysm is the must-not-miss mimic.
Microvascular third nerve palsies usually recover spontaneously over weeks to months with risk-factor control.

cases.title: Confusion and Seizures With a Thyroid History
cases.chiefComplaint: She is admitted for fluctuating confusion and a witnessed seizure.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman is admitted after weeks of fluctuating confusion, behavioral changes, and a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home. Her family reports she has been increasingly forgetful, intermittently disoriented, and occasionally agitated, with symptoms waxing and waning. There has been no fever, headache, or recent travel. Past medical history includes Hashimoto thyroiditis with hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine; she has otherwise been healthy. She has no known drug allergies. She does not drink alcohol or use drugs. Family history includes autoimmune thyroid disease in her sister and type 1 diabetes in a cousin. She works as a librarian. On the ward she has had two further brief episodes of confusion and one focal seizure. Her family is alarmed by how rapidly she has changed over a few weeks and notes she had been compliant with her thyroid medication.
cases.exam: She is intermittently inattentive and disoriented to time and place, with fluctuating alertness. There is no neck rigidity, and Kernig and Brudzinski signs are negative. Cranial nerves are intact. There is no focal motor weakness, but intermittent myoclonic jerks of the upper extremities are noted. Deep tendon reflexes are normal and symmetric; there are no pathologic reflexes. There is no thyromegaly or goiter on exam, and no tremor. Gait could not be fully assessed due to her mental status. The remainder of the general exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given subacute encephalopathy with seizures and myoclonus, what is the most appropriate initial workup?
options: Broad evaluation including metabolic panel, brain MRI, EEG, and lumbar puncture to exclude infectious, structural, and metabolic causes
Empiric long-term antipsychotics for presumed primary psychiatric disease
Thyroid ultrasound as the single key test
stages.explanation: New subacute encephalopathy with seizures and myoclonus demands a broad, urgent workup—metabolic panel, brain MRI, EEG, and lumbar puncture—to exclude infectious (encephalitis), structural, metabolic, and autoimmune causes before attributing symptoms to any single diagnosis. Outpatient follow-up is unsafe for an acutely confused, seizing patient. Empiric antipsychotics without a workup could mask a treatable organic cause and risk lowering the seizure threshold. A thyroid ultrasound evaluates gland structure but does not diagnose an encephalopathy; the relevant thyroid testing here is for autoantibodies, and imaging of the brain plus CSF analysis is what guides the differential.

stages.label: EMPIRIC THERAPY
stages.question: While the workup is underway and before results return, which empiric treatment is most important to start given her presentation?
options: Empiric IV acyclovir to cover herpes simplex encephalitis until it is excluded
High-dose corticosteroids immediately, before any infectious workup
stages.explanation: In any patient with acute encephalopathy and seizures, empiric IV acyclovir should be started promptly to cover herpes simplex encephalitis—a treatable, rapidly fatal condition—until HSV PCR and the CSF return, because delayed treatment markedly worsens outcomes. Starting high-dose corticosteroids before excluding CNS infection is hazardous, as immunosuppression could worsen an untreated encephalitis; steroids come later once infection is excluded and Hashimoto encephalopathy is suspected. Stress-dose levothyroxine is not indicated and does not treat encephalopathy. Empiric antifungals are not warranted without specific risk factors or evidence of fungal infection. Covering the must-not-miss HSV encephalitis takes priority while the autoimmune cause is being established.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
TSH: 3.8 mIU/L
Free T4: normal
Anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibody: 1,240 IU/mL (markedly elevated)
MRI brain: nonspecific scattered white matter changes; no mass or infarct
EEG: diffuse slowing, no status epilepticus
Lumbar puncture: mildly elevated protein, no pleocytosis, negative Gram stain and cultures
HSV PCR: negative
Serum and CSF autoimmune encephalitis panel: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With encephalopathy, markedly elevated anti-TPO antibodies, normal thyroid function, bland CSF, and negative infectious workup, what is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Hashimoto encephalopathy (steroid-responsive encephalopathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis) presents with subacute encephalopathy, seizures, and myoclonus, markedly elevated anti-thyroid antibodies, often-normal thyroid function, nonspecific MRI, diffuse EEG slowing, and bland or mildly elevated-protein CSF after infection is excluded. Herpes encephalitis is excluded by negative HSV PCR and the absence of temporal-lobe involvement and CSF pleocytosis. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease is more slowly progressive with characteristic MRI/EEG findings and is not steroid-responsive. Myxedema coma occurs with severe hypothyroidism (very high TSH, low free T4, hypothermia)—but her thyroid function is normal, making it incorrect. It is a diagnosis of exclusion supported by the antibodies and treatment response.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for her Hashimoto encephalopathy?
options: Antiepileptic monotherapy alone with no immunotherapy
stages.explanation: Hashimoto encephalopathy is typically dramatically steroid-responsive, so high-dose corticosteroids (such as IV methylprednisolone) are first-line and both therapeutic and confirmatory when symptoms improve; IVIG, plasma exchange, or other immunosuppression are used for refractory cases. Increasing levothyroxine does not treat the encephalopathy, especially with normal thyroid function. Indefinite antibiotics and acyclovir are unnecessary once infection is excluded by negative cultures and HSV PCR. Antiepileptics control seizures symptomatically but do not address the underlying autoimmune process; immunotherapy with steroids is the key disease-directed treatment that produces recovery. A favorable response also helps confirm the diagnosis retrospectively, since Hashimoto encephalopathy remains a clinical diagnosis of exclusion.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: She improves markedly within days of starting steroids. What does this response best support, and what is appropriate next?
options: It confirms herpes encephalitis; continue acyclovir for 21 days
It supports the diagnosis of Hashimoto encephalopathy; taper steroids gradually with monitoring for relapse
It proves a primary seizure disorder; stop all immunotherapy and steroids abruptly
It indicates the symptoms were factitious; arrange psychiatric admission
stages.explanation: Rapid improvement with corticosteroids is characteristic of and supports the diagnosis of Hashimoto (steroid-responsive) encephalopathy; steroids should then be tapered gradually with close monitoring, since relapses can occur and some patients need prolonged or steroid-sparing immunotherapy. A steroid response does not confirm herpes encephalitis, which is treated with acyclovir and would not respond this way. Abruptly stopping steroids risks relapse and adrenal suppression, so it is unsafe. The clear organic and immunologic basis—elevated anti-TPO antibodies and steroid responsiveness—argues firmly against a factitious or purely psychiatric explanation. Some patients require a prolonged steroid taper or steroid-sparing immunotherapy, and ongoing neurologic follow-up is needed to detect recurrence early.

pearls: Suspect Hashimoto encephalopathy in subacute encephalopathy with seizures/myoclonus, high anti-TPO antibodies, and often-normal thyroid function.
It is a diagnosis of exclusion—rule out infectious, structural, metabolic, and other autoimmune encephalitides first.
MRI is often nonspecific, EEG shows diffuse slowing, and CSF may show mildly elevated protein without pleocytosis.
It is typically dramatically steroid-responsive; high-dose corticosteroids are first-line, with a gradual taper and monitoring for relapse.

cases.title: Blurry Vision in a Long-Standing Diabetic
cases.chiefComplaint: He reports gradually worsening blurry vision over several months.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man presents to clinic with several months of gradually worsening blurry vision in both eyes, occasionally noticing dark 'floaters.' He has not had a dilated eye exam in over five years. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes for 15 years that has been poorly controlled (most recent A1c 9.8%), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and diabetic chronic kidney disease. Medications include metformin, glargine insulin, lisinopril, and atorvastatin, though he admits to missing doses frequently. He has no known drug allergies. He has a 20-pack-year smoking history and drinks alcohol socially. Family history includes type 2 diabetes and 'eye problems' in his mother. He works as a warehouse manager. He denies eye pain, redness, flashing lights, or a curtain-like visual field loss, but is worried about his ability to keep driving and working given the visual changes.
cases.exam: He is a well-appearing man in no distress. Visual acuity is 20/50 in the right eye and 20/40 in the left. Extraocular movements are intact and pupils are equal and reactive without an afferent defect. The anterior segments are normal, and intraocular pressures are within normal limits. Dilated fundoscopic exam reveals scattered microaneurysms, dot-and-blot hemorrhages, hard exudates, and a few cotton-wool spots in both eyes; in the right eye there are fragile new vessels at the optic disc. There is no retinal detachment. Foot exam shows reduced monofilament sensation bilaterally. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has blurry vision and 15 years of poorly controlled diabetes. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Prescribe corrective lenses and reassess in one year
Perform or refer for a dilated fundoscopic examination
Order a brain MRI for the visual complaints
Reassure him that vision changes are an expected part of aging
stages.explanation: A patient with long-standing, poorly controlled diabetes and new visual symptoms needs a dilated fundoscopic exam (or prompt ophthalmology referral) to evaluate for diabetic retinopathy, the leading cause of vision loss in this population. Prescribing glasses and waiting a year could miss sight-threatening, treatable pathology. A brain MRI is not indicated for bilateral gradual blurring without neurologic signs or visual field defects suggesting a chiasmal/retrochiasmal lesion. Attributing the changes to normal aging is incorrect and dangerous given his risk profile; the retina must be directly examined to detect microvascular disease and neovascularization.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His dilated exam shows microaneurysms, dot-and-blot hemorrhages, hard exudates, cotton-wool spots, and new vessels at the disc in the right eye. What is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: The presence of new vessels (neovascularization) at the optic disc defines proliferative diabetic retinopathy, the most advanced and sight-threatening stage, where ischemia drives VEGF-mediated growth of fragile vessels prone to bleeding. Mild non-proliferative disease shows only microaneurysms without neovascularization, so it understages him. Hypertensive retinopathy can cause cotton-wool spots and hemorrhages but not the microaneurysm-rich picture with disc neovascularization typical of diabetes. Central retinal vein occlusion produces sudden, often unilateral, dramatic 'blood and thunder' hemorrhages, not the chronic bilateral diabetic findings with neovascularization seen here. Correctly staging the disease as proliferative is critical because it mandates urgent ophthalmologic treatment to prevent irreversible vision loss.

stages.label: OPHTHALMOLOGY EVALUATION
stages.content:
Optical coherence tomography: macular edema present in the right eye
Fluorescein angiography: areas of capillary nonperfusion and leakage from neovascular fronds, right eye
No vitreous hemorrhage or tractional retinal detachment at this time
Repeat A1c: 9.9%

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For his proliferative diabetic retinopathy with macular edema, which treatment(s) should ophthalmology consider?
options: Anti-VEGF intravitreal injections and/or panretinal photocoagulation, with anti-VEGF or focal laser for the macular edema
stages.explanation: Proliferative diabetic retinopathy is treated with panretinal photocoagulation and/or intravitreal anti-VEGF injections to regress neovascularization and prevent vitreous hemorrhage and tractional detachment; coexisting diabetic macular edema is treated with anti-VEGF agents or focal/grid laser. Topical antibiotics and artificial tears treat surface/infectious problems, not retinal neovascularization. Systemic corticosteroids are not standard therapy for proliferative diabetic retinopathy and would worsen glycemic control. Observation alone is inappropriate for sight-threatening proliferative disease with macular edema, which carries a high risk of severe vision loss without timely treatment. Close ophthalmology follow-up is essential to monitor treatment response and detect progression to vitreous hemorrhage or tractional detachment.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Beyond ocular treatment, what systemic management is most important to slow progression of his retinopathy?
options: Optimizing glycemic control along with blood pressure and lipid management, plus smoking cessation
Stopping all diabetes medications to 'rest' the eyes
Strict bed rest and avoidance of reading
stages.explanation: Tight glycemic control (and correcting modifiable risks—blood pressure, lipids, and smoking) is proven to slow the onset and progression of diabetic retinopathy, so optimizing his A1c, treating hypertension, and stopping smoking are essential alongside ophthalmologic care. Stopping diabetes medications would worsen hyperglycemia and accelerate retinopathy. High-dose vitamin A does not treat or prevent diabetic retinopathy and risks toxicity. Bed rest and avoiding reading have no effect on the underlying microvascular disease. Systemic risk-factor control complements local therapy and is fundamental to preserving his remaining vision over the long term. Establishing a schedule of regular dilated eye exams ensures future sight-threatening changes are caught and treated early.

cases.diagnosis: Proliferative diabetic retinopathy with diabetic macular edema
pearls: Neovascularization (new vessels) defines PROLIFERATIVE diabetic retinopathy—the most sight-threatening stage.
Non-proliferative findings include microaneurysms, dot-and-blot hemorrhages, hard exudates, and cotton-wool spots.
Treat proliferative disease with panretinal photocoagulation and/or anti-VEGF injections; treat macular edema with anti-VEGF or focal laser.
Tight glycemic, blood pressure, and lipid control plus smoking cessation slow progression; ensure regular dilated eye exams.
